title: Why Linear Isn't Enough
text: Lines Can Only Do So MuchA neuron without activation does y = mx + bStrip away the activation and a neuron computes: output = (input × weight) + bias. m is the weight (slope), b is the bias (y-intercept).y = mx + bm (slope)= the weight. Controls how steep the line is.b (bias)= the bias. Shifts the line up or down.x (input)= the input value going in.y (output)= the output. Always a straight line.Key problem: no matter what m and b are, this is ALWAYS a straight line.Continue

title: Why Linear Isn't Enough
text: his a Problem? Real Patterns are CurvedLine graph of study hours vs grade showing a linear relationshipstudy hoursgradeA straight line can model this ✓Curve graph of temperature vs growth showing a parabolic peak pattern that no straight line can fitpeaktemperaturegrowthNo straight line fits this curve ✗S-curve graph of drug dose vs effect showing a nonlinear responsedrug doseeffectOr this S-curve ✗Think about real-world patterns. A 1500 sqft house costs more than 1000 sqft - that's roughly linear. But plant growth vs temperature? A plant grows faster as temperature rises from 10°C to 25°C, then STOPS growing and DIES above 40°C. No straight line can go up, peak, and then come down. Same with medicine dosage: 100mg helps, 200mg helps more, but 1000mg is toxic. These real patterns require curves that bend and change direction. A straight line always goes in one direction.Continue

title: Why Linear Isn't Enough
text: l Problem: Stacking Without ActivationMaybe adding more layers helps?Layer 1: output₁ = input × 3 + 1Layer 2: output₂ = output₁ × 2 + 5Substitute:output₂ = (input × 3 + 1) × 2 + 5=> output₂ = input × 6 + 7Still just y = 6x + 7. A straight line!No matter how many layers: 2 layers, 10 layers, 100 layers - without activation they all collapse into one straight line. 100 layers = 1 layer.Continue

title: Why Linear Isn't Enough
text: Need: a BEND in the LineWe need something that adds a bend to the line. Something that breaks the linearity so layers actually DO different things when stacked. That something is called an activation function.

title: Loss - How Wrong Were We?
text: Measuring Our MistakesLoss is a single number that measures how wrong our prediction was.Loss=(actual − predicted)²We square the error to make all errors positive (so they don't cancel out), and big errors get punished much more than small ones.Continue

title: Loss - How Wrong Were We?
text: ple CalculationPredicted: 807kActual: 900kLoss=900−807)²Loss=(93)²Loss=8,649Continue

title: Loss - How Wrong Were We?
text: InsightThe loss depends on the prediction. The prediction depends on weights and biases. So if we change a weight, the prediction changes, and the loss changes.There exists some perfect set of weights that makes the loss as small as possible. Our job is to find those values.But how do we know which direction to change them? We need to understand derivatives first.Bar chart comparing predicted value against actual value with an annotated gap, showing how loss measures prediction error807kPredicted900kActual93k gapweights↓prediction↓lossContinue

title: Loss - How Wrong Were We?
text: re the Error?1. Big Errors Get Punished MoreError of 2 gives loss of 4. Error of 10 gives loss of 100. A 5x error becomes 25x loss.2. Smooth Curve for DerivativesThe derivative of x² is 2x (simple, works everywhere). Backpropagation needs smooth derivatives to work cleanly.3. One Clear MinimumSquared loss creates a parabolic bowl - one clear lowest point. Gradient descent rolls down smoothly to find it.

title: Learning - What Does It Mean?
text: What Changes During Training?When a neural network is first created, all weights and biases are random numbers. It knows nothing. It makes garbage predictions.Our network predicted 807k but the correct answer was 900k. That's how bad random weights are.Continue

title: Learning - What Does It Mean?
text: k Starts DumbLearning is adjusting weights and biases until predictions get good. The loop has 4 steps:1Forward PassFeed input through network, get prediction2Calculate LossCompare prediction with correct answer3Backward PassFigure out which weights caused the error4Update WeightsNudge each weight to reduce error↻ Repeat until loss is small enoughContinue

title: Learning - What Does It Mean?
text: agation + Gradient DescentThis process has a name: "Backpropagation + Gradient Descent."BackpropagationStep 3 - figuring out which weights to blame for the errorGradient DescentStep 4 - actually updating the weightsTogether, they are "training." When someone says "training a model," they mean running this loop millions of times.Continue

title: Learning - What Does It Mean?
text: llions of LoopsAfter running this loop millions of times, the weights converge to values that make good predictions. The network has "learned."Those final weight values are the model. When you download GPT-3, you are downloading 175 billion carefully-tuned weight values that have been optimized through this exact process.

title: Learning - What Does It Mean?

title: Derivatives - The Core Intuition
text: How Sensitive Is the Output?A derivative answers one simple question: "If I nudge the input a tiny bit, how much does the output change?"No Greek symbols, no limits, no complicated math. Just: small change in, how much change out?Continue

title: Derivatives - The Core Intuition
text: ExampleOur network: weight = 0.5, input = 1500, bias = 50Simple neuron flow diagram showing input connected by weight to a neuron with bias, producing output, for derivative computation0.5input1500Neuronb=50output800Prediction=1500×0.5+50=800Actual=900Loss=(900−800)²=10,000Now what if we nudge the weight to 0.6?New Prediction=1500×0.6+50=950New Loss=(900−950)²=2,500Weight changed by +0.1 → Loss changed by -7,500. Increasing the weight made loss go DOWN. Good direction!Continue

title: Derivatives - The Core Intuition
text: vative RatioThe derivative is this ratio:∂ Loss / ∂ weighthow much loss changed / how much weight changedPlugging in our numbers:∂Loss / ∂weight=(2,500 - 10,000)/(0.6 - 0.5)=-7,500/0.1=-75,000Negative = increasing weight decreases loss. The magnitude (75,000) tells us it's very sensitive.The ∂ symbol (read "partial") just means "tiny change in." So ∂Loss/∂weight means "the tiny change in loss caused by a tiny change in weight."Continue

title: Derivatives - The Core Intuition
text: Derivative Tells UsNegative (-)Increasing weight → loss goes DOWN → increase the weight!Positive (+)Increasing weight → loss goes UP → decrease the weight!Large NumberLoss is very sensitive to this weight → matters a lotSmall NumberLoss barely changes → matters littleContinue

title: Derivatives - The Core Intuition
text: vative is Your CompassThe derivative tells us which direction to adjust each weight to reduce loss.With multiple weights, we turn one knob at a time. ∂L/∂w means "how does loss change when we nudge only w, holding everything else constant?" That is what "partial" means - changing one thing, leaving everything else still.Parabolic loss curve with a marked current point, tangent line labeled slope equals derivative, and an arrow toward the minimumweightlosscurrentslope = derivative→ go this wayminimum

title: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: Tracing the Blame BackwardsThere are MULTIPLE steps between weight and loss. We have to trace through all of them:weightw = 0.5→compute1500 × w + 50→prediction800→subtract actual800 - 900→error-100→square it(-100)²→loss10,000Continue

title: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: n Rule: Work BackwardsWork backwards, one step at a time. Multiply all the derivatives together.The Chain Rule FormulaChain rule formula as partial derivatives showing the three backward pass steps in different colors∂Loss∂weight=∂Loss∂error×∂error∂pred×∂pred∂weightEach fraction is one step. Multiply them all to get the final answer.Step 1: Loss with respect to Errorloss = error²derivative = 2 x error = 2 x (-100) = -200Step 2: Error with respect to Predictionerror = prediction - 900derivative = 1Step 3: Prediction with respect to Weightprediction = 1500 x w + 50derivative = 1500Continue

title: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: Them TogetherNow multiply them all together (that is the chain rule):∂Loss/∂weight =-200×1×1500=-300,000This is negative - increasing weight will decrease loss. The magnitude (300,000) tells us the loss is very sensitive to this weight.Continue

title: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: BiasThe same process works for bias:∂Loss/∂bias = -200 × 1 × 1 = -200The prediction formula is prediction = 1500w + bias. So the derivative of prediction with respect to bias is 1 (bias is directly added).Now we have a gradient for every learnable parameter: weight and bias. Time to update them.

title: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: Neural Network Foundations: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule. This chapter covers the backward pass - the chain rule.

title: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: Diagram in The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule: Chain rule formula rendered as partial derivatives: dLoss/dweight decomposed into three colored fractions showing the three backward pass steps

title: Gradient Descent - Fixing the Weights
text: The Weight Update RuleEach weight gets updated using this rule:new_weight = old_weight - learning_rate × gradientold_weightthe weight's current value (before this update)gradienthow much loss changes when this weight changes (from backward pass)learning_ratea small number (like 0.001) that controls step sizenew_weightthe updated value we'll use going forwardWe move in the OPPOSITE direction of the gradient. If the gradient says "loss increases when you go right," we go left.Gradient descent visualization showing a U-shaped loss curve with labeled steps rolling downhill toward the minimumweightlossstep 1step 2step NminimumContinue

title: Gradient Descent - Fixing the Weights
text: Rate ScalingOur gradient is -300,000. If applied directly: new_w = 0.5 - (-300,000) = 300,000.5. Insane!The learning rate (like 0.0000001) scales the step to something reasonable:Weight update:new_w =0.5−(0.0000001×-300,000)new_w =0.5+0.03=0.53Bias update:new_b =50−(0.0000001×-200)new_b =50+0.00002≈50.00002Continue

title: Gradient Descent - Fixing the Weights
text: he ImprovementBefore Updatew=0.5, b=50pred=800loss=10,000After Updatew=0.53, b≈50pred=1500×0.53+50=845loss=3,025Loss dropped from 10,000 to 3,025! Repeat thousands of times and loss approaches zero.Continue

title: Gradient Descent - Fixing the Weights
text: scape AnalogyImagine you are blindfolded on a hilly landscape. Height represents loss. You want to reach the lowest valley.Start (w=0.5)High up on the hill, loss is 10,000Gradient tells us the slopeWhich direction is downhill? That is what the derivative shows.Goal (minimum loss)Weights that make excellent predictions, loss near zeroLearning rate too big: you jump over the valley. Learning rate too small: takes forever. Just right: steady descent to the minimum.Continue

title: Gradient Descent - Fixing the Weights
text: PictureThat is backpropagation + gradient descent!1Forward - Feed input through network → get prediction2Loss - Compare prediction with correct answer3Backward - Chain rule to find gradients4Update - Nudge weights opposite to gradientIn real networks with millions of weights, the exact same process happens - just with longer chains and more derivatives. The math scales, the idea stays identical.

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: Our Network Got It Wrong - Now Fix ItIn chapter 1.7, we traced a forward pass. The network predicted a house price:Inputsx₁=1500, x₂=3Hiddenh₁=950, h₂=185Outputy=807Target900Loss = (807 - 900)²8,649The network predicted 807 but the real price is 900. Loss = 8,649. How do we fix this? We need to figure out which weights are to blame and how to adjust each one.Continue

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: ork - Now We Trace BackwardsThis is the same network from chapter 1.7. Every weight is labeled on its edge. The backward pass will compute a gradient for each one.Two-layer neural network annotated with all weights, biases, intermediate outputs, target, loss, and backward gradient flow for backpropagation tracingInputHiddenOutput0.50.150100.80.2x₁1500x₂3h₁b=50→ 950h₂b=5→ 185yb=10→ 807target = 900Loss = 8,649← Gradients flow backwards through this network ←The chain rule traces backward through every operation, multiplying derivatives at each step. Think of it like tracing blame: "Loss, who caused you? The prediction. Prediction, who caused you? The weights and hidden neurons..."Continue

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: How Does Loss Change with y?Start at the very end. Loss = (y - target)². How much does loss change when y changes by a tiny amount?Derivative of Loss with respect to yloss = (y - target)²dLoss/dy = 2 x (y - target)dLoss/dy = 2 x (807-900) = 2 x (-93) =-186This is negative (-186). It means: if we increased y by 1 unit, loss would decrease by 186. Makes sense - y = 807 is too low, so pushing it up reduces the error.Continue

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: Output Layer Weight GradientsNow trace one step further back. y = h₁ x w_o1 + h₂ x w_o2 + b_o. How does each output weight affect loss?dLoss/dw_o1:dLoss/dy x dy/dw_o1 = dLoss/dy x h₁= -186 x 950 =-176,700dLoss/dw_o2:dLoss/dy x dy/dw_o2 = dLoss/dy x h₂= -186 x 185 =-34,410dLoss/db_o:dLoss/dy x dy/db_o = dLoss/dy x 1= -186 x 1 =-186Why is the gradient for w_o1 five times larger than w_o2? Because h₁ = 950 is five times larger than h₂ = 185. A bigger input means the weight has more influence on the output.Continue

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: How Does Gradient Reach the Hidden Layer?The output layer is done. But what about the hidden layer weights (w₁₁, w₂₁, etc.)? They are TWO steps away from loss. The gradient must flow backward through the output neuron first.How much does loss care about h₁?y = h₁ x w_o1 + ..., so dy/dh₁ = w_o1 = 0.8dLoss/dh₁ = dLoss/dy x w_o1 = -186 x 0.8 =-148.8How much does loss care about h₂?y = ... + h₂ x w_o2 + ..., so dy/dh₂ = w_o2 = 0.2dLoss/dh₂ = dLoss/dy x w_o2 = -186 x 0.2 =-37.2Notice: h₁ gets 4x more gradient than h₂ because w_o1 (0.8) is 4x bigger than w_o2 (0.2). The weight connecting h₁ to the output is like a pipe - a bigger weight means a wider pipe that carries more gradient backward.Continue

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: Through ReLU - The GateBefore reaching the hidden layer weights, the gradient must pass through ReLU. Remember: h₁ = ReLU(z₁) where z₁ is the pre-activation value.ReLU DerivativeIf z > 0:derivative = 1Gradient passes through unchangedIf z ≤ 0:derivative = 0Gradient is completely blocked. Dead.Neuron h₁: z₁ = 950 (positive) - ReLU derivative = 1dLoss/dz₁ = dLoss/dh₁ x 1 = -148.8 x 1 =-148.8Neuron h₂: z₂ = 185 (positive) - ReLU derivative = 1dLoss/dz₂ = dLoss/dh₂ x 1 = -37.2 x 1 =-37.2Both neurons had positive pre-activation values, so ReLU let the full gradient through. But if z had been negative (like -50), ReLU would have output 0 during the forward pass, and the gradient would be completely blocked during backprop. That neuron would learn nothing from this example - a "dead neuron."Continue

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: Hidden Layer Weight GradientsNow we are at the hidden layer weights. z₁ = x₁ x w₁₁ + x₂ x w₂₁ + b₁. So dz₁/dw₁₁ = x₁, dz₁/dw₂₁ = x₂, dz₁/db₁ = 1.Neuron h₁ weights (dLoss/dz₁ = -148.8)dL/dw₁₁=dL/dz₁ × x₁(1500)=-148.8 × 1500=-223,200dL/dw₂₁=dL/dz₁ × x₂(3)=-148.8 × 3=-446.4dL/db₁=dL/dz₁ × 1=-148.8 × 1=-148.8Neuron h₂ weights (dLoss/dz₂ = -37.2)dL/dw₁₂=dL/dz₂ × x₁(1500)=-37.2 × 1500=-55,800dL/dw₂₂=dL/dz₂ × x₂(3)=-37.2 × 3=-111.6dL/db₂=dL/dz₂ × 1=-37.2 × 1=-37.2Notice w₁₁ has a gradient of -223,200 while w₂₁ has only -446.4. Why? Because x₁ = 1500 is 500x larger than x₂ = 3. The gradient for a weight is proportional to its input - bigger inputs create bigger gradients.Continue

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: Chain for w₁₁Let's trace the COMPLETE chain from loss all the way back to w₁₁. This is four multiplications - the chain rule in action:dLoss/dw₁₁ =dLoss/dyxdy/dh₁xdReLU/dz₁xdz₁/dw₁₁=-186x0.8x1x1500=-223,200Four multiplications. Each one traces through one operation in the computational graph. This is why it is called the "chain" rule - you chain derivatives together, link by link, all the way from loss back to each weight.Continue

title: Backprop Through the Real Network
text: Gradient Table - Every ParameterWe just computed the gradient for ALL 9 learnable parameters in our network:ParameterValueGradientMeaningw₁₁0.5-223,200Increase a lotw₂₁50-446.4Increase a bitb₁50-148.8Increase a bitw₁₂0.1-55,800Increasew₂₂10-111.6Increase a bitb₂5-37.2Increase a bitw_o10.8-176,700Increase a lotw_o20.2-34,410Increaseb_o10-186Increase a bitEvery gradient is negative. This means: increasing any weight will decrease the loss. That makes sense - the prediction (807) was too low, so the network needs to push everything up to get closer to 900.This is backpropagation. Start at the loss, trace backward through every operation, multiply derivatives at each step. Every weight gets a gradient that tells it exactly how to change.

Each weight gets nudged in the opposite direction of its gradient:w₁₁=0.500- lr ×-223,200=0.522w₂₁=50.000- lr ×-446.4=50.000b₁=50.000- lr ×-148.8=50.000w₁₂=0.100- lr ×-55,800=0.106w₂₂=10.000- lr ×-111.6=10.000b₂=5.000- lr ×-37.2=5.000w_o1=0.800- lr ×-176,700=0.818w_o2=0.200- lr ×-34,410=0.203b_o=10.000- lr ×-186=10.000Weights with large gradients (w₁₁, w_o1) changed the most. Biases barely moved because their gradients are small. All 9 parameters updated simultaneously - one step of training.Continue

title: Gradients in Action - The Full Training Loop
text: Pass with Updated WeightsLet's run the network again with the new weights and see if it improved:h₁ = ReLU(1500 x 0.522 + 3 x 50 + 50) = ReLU(983) = 983h₂ = ReLU(1500 x 0.106 + 3 x 10 + 5) = ReLU(194) = 194y = 983 x 0.818 + 194 x 0.203 + 10 = 854Beforey = 807Loss = 8,649→After 1 stepy = 854Loss = 2,116Loss dropped from 8,649 to 2,116. Prediction moved from 807 to 854 - closer to the target of 900. One single step of backprop + gradient descent already cut the loss by 75%!Continue

title: Gradients in Action - The Full Training Loop
text: Watch the Network ConvergeKeep repeating: forward pass, compute loss, backprop, update weights. Each step gets closer:Step 0 (initial)y=807loss=8,649Step 1y=854loss=2,116Step 2y=877loss=529Step 3y=889loss=121Step 4y=895loss=25Step 10y=899.8loss=0.04After just 10 steps, the network predicts 899.8 - nearly perfect. Loss went from 8,649 to 0.04. This is gradient descent: small, calculated steps downhill until you reach the minimum.Continue

title: Gradients in Action - The Full Training Loop
text: lete Training LoopEvery neural network in the world trains using this exact loop:→Forward PassFeed input through network, get prediction?Compute LossHow wrong was the prediction?←Backward PassChain rule: compute gradient for every weight↓Update WeightsNudge each weight opposite to its gradient↻RepeatThousands to billions of timesGPT-4 runs this loop trillions of times on billions of text examples. Each loop adjusts millions of weights by tiny amounts. After enough loops, the model can write poetry, answer questions, and write code - all because of this simple loop: predict, measure error, trace blame, fix weights, repeat.

title: Why Deep Backprop Gets Hard
text: Our 2-Layer Network Was the Easy CaseFor w₁₁, the chain had 4 multiplications: dLoss/dy x dy/dh₁ x dReLU/dz₁ x dz₁/dw₁₁. That is manageable.But modern networks have 50, 100, or even 1000+ layers. GPT-4 has 120 layers. For a weight in layer 1 of a 120-layer network, the chain rule has 120+ multiplications.Layer 1×Layer 2×Layer 3×Layer 4×Layer 5... ×Loss120 multiplications chained togetherContinue

title: Why Deep Backprop Gets Hard
text: shing Gradient ProblemIf each derivative in the chain is less than 1, the product shrinks exponentially:2 layers0.255 layers0.03110 layers0.00150 layers0.000000000000001At 50 layers, the gradient is essentially zero. The early layers never learn because the gradient signal has completely vanished by the time it reaches them. It is like whispering a message through 50 people - by the end, nothing is left.Continue

title: Why Deep Backprop Gets Hard
text: oding Gradient ProblemThe opposite problem: if each derivative is greater than 1, the product grows exponentially:2 layers45 layers3210 layers1,02450 layers1,125,899,906,842,624At 50 layers, the gradient is astronomically large. Weight updates become wild jumps that overshoot the minimum and the network diverges - the loss goes to infinity instead of zero. Training crashes.Continue

title: Why Deep Backprop Gets Hard
text: s That Made Deep Learning WorkThese problems held back AI for decades. Here is what finally solved them:ReLU Activation (instead of sigmoid)Sigmoid's derivative is at most 0.25 - guaranteed to vanish. ReLU's derivative is exactly 1 for positive inputs - no shrinkage. This alone was a breakthrough.Residual Connections (Skip Connections)Instead of output = f(input), use output = input + f(input). The gradient for "input" is always 1 - it flows straight through the skip connection, no matter how deep the network. The Transformer uses this everywhere.Layer NormalizationKeep activations in a stable range at every layer. Prevents both tiny and huge values from accumulating through the network. We will see this in Section 8.Careful Weight InitializationStart weights in a range that keeps gradients close to 1. Too large = explosion. Too small = vanishing. Methods like "He initialization" and "Xavier initialization" solve this mathematically.Continue

title: Why Deep Backprop Gets Hard
text: Matters for Everything AheadEvery architecture choice in the Transformer - residual connections, layer normalization, scaled dot-product attention - exists partly to solve these gradient problems. When you see "Add & Norm" in the Transformer diagram, you will know exactly why it is there: to keep gradients healthy so backprop works through 120 layers.Backpropagation is the single most important algorithm in deep learning. Every model you have ever used - GPT, DALL-E, AlphaGo - learned through this exact process: forward pass, compute loss, chain rule backward, update weights, repeat.

These examples are hidden during training - they are the honest test.The real measure of learningTraining loss tells you how well the model memorizes. title: Dropout - The Regularization Trick
text: Works - Forced RedundancyNo single neuron can become a "lone specialist" that the network depends on entirely. Every neuron must be useful even when its neighbors are missing.Without DropoutNeuron 3 memorizes a specific pattern. If neuron 3 fails, the network breaks. Knowledge is fragile and concentrated.With DropoutAll neurons learn overlapping representations. Any 3 of 6 can carry the signal. Knowledge is distributed and robust.Think of a team of 6 people where any 3 might be absent on any given day. Everyone must learn everyone else's job. The team becomes resilient - no single point of failure.Continue

title: Dropout - The Regularization Trick
text: al MathStart with an input vector and apply a random binary mask, then scale up:Step 1: Input vector0.80.30.50.20.70.4Step 2: Random Bernoulli mask (p = 0.5)101010Step 3: Multiply element-wise0.800.500.70Step 4: Scale by 1/(1 - p) = 1/0.5 = 21.60101.40Why scale?Without scaling, the expected output is only 0.5 x original (half the neurons are zero). Scaling by 2 restores the expected value to match the original. This way, the network sees the same magnitude at training and inference.Continue

title: Dropout - The Regularization Trick
text: ence - No DropoutWhen the model is deployed and making predictions, dropout is completely turned off. All neurons are active, no mask is applied, no scaling is needed.Six-neuron layer at inference showing all neurons active, illustrating that dropout is turned off during predictionAll 6 Neurons Active at Inferencen1ACTIVEn2ACTIVEn3ACTIVEn4ACTIVEn5ACTIVEn6ACTIVEThe scaling during training (multiplying by 1/(1 - p)) already compensated for the missing neurons. So at inference, using all neurons with their original weights produces the correct expected output. This is called "inverted dropout."Continue

title: Adam - The Real Optimizer
text: The Problem with Plain SGDIn gradient descent (chapter 1.12), every parameter gets the same learning rate. But a neural network has millions of parameters, and they are not all equal. Some get massive gradients. Others get tiny ones. One learning rate cannot fit both.Imagine two parameters in the same network:Parameter A - an embedding weightGradients are huge because many tokens flow through it.step = 0.001 x -50.0 = 0.05A step of 0.05 might be way too big - it could overshoot the minimum.Parameter B - a deep layer biasGradients are tiny because the signal gets diluted through many layers.step = 0.001 x -0.0003 = 0.0000003A step of 0.0000003 is almost nothing - this parameter barely learns.The core problemParameter A's step is 170,000x bigger than Parameter B's. If we lower the learning rate to fix A, B learns even slower. If we raise it to fix B, A explodes. SGD has no way to treat each parameter differently.Adam solves this with two ideas: momentum (smoothing noisy gradients) and adaptive rates (giving each parameter its own effective learning rate). Let's build it up step by step.Continue

title: Adam - The Real Optimizer
text: MomentumInstead of using only the current gradient, keep a running average called m (for "first moment"). This smooths out noisy zigzags and builds speed in consistent directions.Momentum formula: m_t equals beta_1 times m_(t-1) plus (1 minus beta_1) times g_t, showing exponential moving average of gradientsmt=β1·mt-1+(1-β1)·gtmomentum0.9gradientThe default is β₁ = 0.9. That means 90% of the momentum comes from history, 10% from the current gradient. Think of it like a heavy ball rolling downhill: it does not change direction instantly.Concrete walkthrough - 4 gradient steps:t = 1g = +0.80.9 x 0 + 0.1 x 0.8=m = +0.080t = 2g = +0.60.9 x 0.08 + 0.1 x 0.6=m = +0.132t = 3g = -0.90.9 x 0.132 + 0.1 x (-0.9)=m = +0.029t = 4g = +0.70.9 x 0.029 + 0.1 x 0.7=m = +0.096At step 3, the gradient flips to -0.9, but momentum does not panic - it barely moves to +0.029. By step 4, when the positive signal returns, momentum recovers quickly. This is exactly what we want: smooth out noise, keep moving in the overall direction.Why "exponential" moving average?Each past gradient is weighted by 0.9 raised to a power: the gradient from 1 step ago gets weight 0.9, from 2 steps ago gets 0.81, from 10 steps ago gets 0.35. Recent gradients matter most, old ones fade exponentially.Continue

title: Adam - The Real Optimizer
text: Adaptive Learning RatesTrack the squared gradient magnitude for each parameter individually. This is called v (the "second moment"). Parameters with large gradients get smaller steps. Parameters with small gradients get larger steps.Adaptive learning rate formula: v_t equals beta_2 times v_(t-1) plus (1 minus beta_2) times g_t squared, tracking average squared gradient magnitude per parametervt=β2·vt-1+(1-β2)·gt2velocity0.999squared gradThe default is β₂ = 0.999. This tracks how "large" gradients typically are for each parameter by averaging their squares over time.How does v create per-parameter learning rates?In the final update, we divide by √v. Watch what happens:Parameter ATypical gradient: 50v tracks 50² = 2,500Divide by √2500 = 50Effective rate: lr / 50Big gradients get tamed automatically.Parameter BTypical gradient: 0.01v tracks 0.01² = 0.0001Divide by √0.0001 = 0.01Effective rate: lr / 0.01Small gradients get boosted automatically.The result: Parameter A's effective learning rate is 5,000x smaller than Parameter B's. Each parameter automatically gets a step size proportional to its gradient scale. The same learning rate α works for both - √v handles the rest.Continue

title: Adam - The Real Optimizer
text: rection - Fixing the Cold StartBoth m and v are initialized to zero before training begins. In the first few steps, these running averages are biased toward zero because they have not had enough data to "warm up." Adam applies a mathematical correction to fix this.Why is this a problem?At step 1 with β₁ = 0.9, the momentum formula gives m₁ = 0.9 x 0 + 0.1 x g₁ = 0.1 x g₁. We only captured 10% of the true gradient. The other 90% was multiplied by zero (the initialization). Our estimate of the gradient direction is 10x too small.Bias correction formulas: m-hat_t equals m_t divided by (1 minus beta_1 to the power t), and v-hat_t equals v_t divided by (1 minus beta_2 to the power t), correcting for zero initializationm^t=mt1 -β1tv^t=vt1 -β2tcorrected momentumcorrected velocityHow much does the correction actually change?t = 1 (first step)1 - β₁¹ = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1m-hat = m₁ / 0.110x boostThe correction is massive - m only captured 10% of the true value, so we multiply by 10 to compensate.t = 1000 (late training)1 - 0.9¹⁰⁰⁰ = 1 - 0 = 1.0m-hat = m₁₀₀₀ / 1.0No changeAfter many steps, 0.9¹⁰⁰⁰ is essentially 0. The denominator is 1, so the correction vanishes completely.The same logic applies to v-hat with β₂ = 0.999. At t = 1, the correction is 1,000x. By t = 5000, it is gone. Bias correction is only significant during the first few hundred steps.Continue

title: Adam - The Real Optimizer
text: Adam Update RuleCombine momentum, adaptive rates, and bias correction into one formula:Full Adam update rule: theta_(t+1) equals theta_t minus alpha times m-hat_t divided by (square root of v-hat_t plus epsilon), combining momentum, adaptive rates, and bias correctionθt+1=θt-α·m^t√v^t+εnew weightold weightlearning rateθthe parameter value (a single weight or bias)αlearning rate (typically 0.001)m-hatbias-corrected momentum - which direction to gov-hatbias-corrected velocity - how much to scale the stepεtiny constant (1e-8) to prevent division by zeroFull numerical walkthrough (t = 1):θ = 0.500, g₁ = 0.4, α = 0.001Step 1: m₁ = 0.9 x 0 + 0.1 x 0.4 = 0.04Step 2: v₁ = 0.999 x 0 + 0.001 x 0.4² = 0.001 x 0.16 = 0.00016Step 3: m-hat = 0.04 / (1 - 0.9) = 0.04 / 0.1 = 0.4Step 4: v-hat = 0.00016 / (1 - 0.999) = 0.00016 / 0.001 = 0.16Step 5: update = 0.001 x 0.4 / (√0.16 + 1e-8) = 0.001 x 0.4 / 0.4 = 0.001θ₂ = 0.500 - 0.001 = 0.499Notice something remarkableAt t = 1 with bias correction, the effective step is almost exactly α = 0.001 regardless of the gradient magnitude. That is the beauty of Adam: the step size is naturally bounded near α, while the direction comes from momentum.Continue

title: Adam - The Real Optimizer
text: Weight Decay Done RightWeight decay is a regularization technique that shrinks all weights slightly each step, preventing them from growing too large. The original Adam paper mixed weight decay into the gradient, but this was a mistake.The problem with L2 regularization in AdamStandard L2 adds a decay term (λ x θ) to the gradient before Adam processes it. But Adam divides by √v - and that √v includes the decay term. So parameters with large v (large gradients) barely feel the decay, while parameters with small v get too much. The decay is not applied equally.AdamW update rule: theta_(t+1) equals theta_t minus alpha times (m-hat_t divided by (square root of v-hat_t plus epsilon) plus lambda times theta_t), with decoupled weight decay applied directlyθt+1=θt-α·(m^t√v^t+ε+λ·θt)Adam step (unchanged)decay (separate)L2 in AdamDecay is added to the gradient, then divided by √v.Parameters with large v feel almost no decay.Decay strength varies per parameter - unintended.AdamWDecay is applied directly to θ, outside of Adam.Every parameter gets the exact same decay rate λ.Clean separation - decay is uniform as intended.Why this matters in practiceGPT, BERT, LLaMA, Gemini, Claude - virtually all modern transformers use AdamW with β₁ = 0.9, β₂ = 0.999, ε = 1e-8, and λ between 0.01 and 0.1. The 2017 paper by Loshchilov and Hutter showed that decoupled weight decay consistently improves generalization. AdamW is not just an option - it is the default optimizer for deep learning.The complete Adam/AdamW recipe:1Compute gradient g for the current mini-batch2Update momentum: m = β₁ x m + (1 - β₁) x g3Update velocity: v = β₂ x v + (1 - β₂) x g²4Bias-correct: m-hat = m / (1 - β₁ᵗ), v-hat = v / (1 - β₂ᵗ)5Update: θ = θ - α x (m-hat / (√v-hat + ε) + λ x θ)

title: Learning Rate Warmup & Decay
text: Why a Constant Learning Rate FailsNo single learning rate works well for the entire training process. Too high and you diverge. Too low and you never converge.lr = 0.01Loss explodes to NaN after a few stepslr = 0.0001Training crawls - still not converged after 100K stepslr = 0.001Works OK... but still not optimal for the whole runThe best learning rate changes over the course of training. Early on you need small steps. In the middle, larger steps. Near the end, small steps again.Continue

title: Learning Rate Warmup & Decay
text: Start Tiny, Ramp UpAt step 1, weights are random garbage. Gradients are noisy and unreliable. A large learning rate plus bad gradients equals immediate divergence.Line graph showing learning rate increasing linearly from zero during warmup phase over 2000 training stepsStepLRLinear Warmup (2000 steps)0400800120016002000Step 0lr = 0 (no updates yet)Step 1000lr = 0.0005 (halfway to peak)Step 2000lr = 0.001 (peak reached)Small LR lets the model "find its footing" before taking bigger steps. The gradients stabilize as the weights move toward a reasonable region of the loss landscape.Continue

title: Learning Rate Warmup & Decay
text: ecay - Smooth DescentAfter warmup, smoothly decrease the LR following a cosine curve. The formula:lr = lr_min + 0.5 x (lr_max - lr_min) x (1 + cos(pi x step / total))Line graph showing learning rate with linear warmup to a peak then smooth cosine decay to zero, illustrating the complete LR scheduleStepLRWarmup + Cosine Decay01000200040006000800010000PeakWarmup PhaseSteps 0-2000: Linear ramp from 0 to peakDecay PhaseSteps 2000+: Cosine curve gently reduces to minimumContinue

title: Learning Rate Warmup & Decay
text: ld Schedule: GPT-3Here is the actual schedule used to train GPT-3 (175 billion parameters):Warmup375M tokens (~2,000 steps)Peak LR6 x 10^-4 (0.0006)Decay over300B tokens (cosine schedule)Min LR6 x 10^-5 (10% of peak)Why cosine and not linear decay?Cosine spends more time at useful learning rates (the middle range) and approaches zero very gradually. Linear decay spends equal time at every LR, wasting steps at near-zero rates where no meaningful learning occurs.

title: Weight Initialization - How Random?
text: Why Not Start at Zero?If all weights start at zero, every neuron in a layer computes the exact same output. All gradients are identical. No neuron ever becomes different from any other. The network is stuck forever.Diagram showing the symmetry problem when all weights are zero: three neurons all produce identical outputAll Weights = 0: The Symmetry ProblemInput[1.0, 0.5][0,0][0,0][0,0]n1w=[0,0]n2w=[0,0]n3w=[0,0]0.00.00.0Allidentical!Same weights, same outputs, same gradients, stuck foreverThis is called the "symmetry problem." Every neuron is a clone of every other. No matter how long you train, they will never differentiate.Continue

title: Weight Initialization - How Random?
text: l - Numbers Shrink to ZeroIf weights are initialized with very small random values (std = 0.001), the numbers flowing through the network shrink toward zero at every layer. Each layer multiplies the previous output by tiny weights, so the values get smaller and smaller.Input[1.0, 0.5]After Layer 1[0.002, -0.001]After Layer 3[~0.000004, ~0.000002]After Layer 5[~0, ~0]Each layer multiplies by tiny weights, shrinking the output exponentially. By layer 5-10, every value is effectively zero. Gradients also vanish - the network cannot learn. The network is "dead."Continue

title: Weight Initialization - How Random?
text: e - Numbers ExplodeIf weights are initialized with large random values (std = 5.0), the numbers flowing through the network grow uncontrollably at every layer. Each layer multiplies by large weights, so the values skyrocket.Input[1.0, 0.5]After Layer 1[8.5, -12.3]After Layer 3[~450, ~-820]After Layer 5[~45000, ~-89000]The values overflow, activations saturate, gradients explode, and training produces NaN within a few steps. The network is unstable from the very first forward pass.Continue

title: Weight Initialization - How Random?
text: lorot - The "Just Right" ScaleScale random weights so the variance of the output matches the variance of the input. The key: divide by the square root of the number of inputs (fan_in).std = sqrt(2 / (fan_in + fan_out))Example: a 512 to 512 layerstd = sqrt(2 / (512 + 512)) = sqrt(2 / 1024) = sqrt(0.00195) =0.044With this scale, the signal variance stays approximately 1.0 through many layers:Layer 1~1.0Layer 3~0.98Layer 5~0.95Layer 10~0.90Stable signal - no vanishing, no exploding. Named after Xavier Glorot, who proved this mathematically in 2010.Continue

title: Weight Initialization - How Random?
text: alization - Designed for ReLUReLU sets all negative values to zero, which kills roughly half the neurons' outputs. This halves the variance at each layer. To compensate, multiply Xavier's scale by sqrt(2).std = sqrt(2 / fan_in)Example: a layer with 512 inputsstd = sqrt(2 / 512) = sqrt(0.00391) =0.0625Compare Xavier: 0.044. He is about 42% larger to compensate for ReLU's halving.Xavier + ReLUL11.0L50.5L100.25Variance shrinks - signal fadesHe + ReLUL11.0L50.98L100.95Variance stable - signal preservedNamed after Kaiming He (2015). Most modern networks with ReLU activations use He initialization. Transformers often use a variant scaled by 1/sqrt(depth) for the residual connections.

title: Tokenization - From Words to Numbers
text: Computers only understand numbers.Text is useless to an AI - no matter how eloquent. First step: convert "I love cats" into [0, 1, 2].This is Tokenization - slicing text into chunks and assigning each a number from a vocabulary.Continue

title: Tokenization - From Words to Numbers
text: kenization strategies exist:Character-level'love' → ['l','o','v','e'][108, 111, 118, 101]✓ Works for ANY word. New letters rare✗ Way too granular. Sequences become huge.Word-level'unhappy' → ['unhappy'][5942]✓ Human intuitive✗ 50K+ vocab needed. New words crash the modelSub-word (BPE)'unhappy' → ['un','##happy'][100, 6420]✓ Sweet spot. ~50K tokens covers everything✗ Need to learn splits firstContinue

title: Tokenization - From Words to Numbers
text: r Encoding (BPE) - the gold standardBPE learns which character pairs appear together most. It starts with characters, then merges the most common pairs repeatedly.Step 0: Split into charactersI␣love␣catsStep 1: Count adjacent pairsl + o = 1o + v = 1v + e = 1c + a = 1a + t = 1t + s = 1Step 2: Merge most common pairAll pairs tie at frequency 1. Pick l + o → loI␣love␣catsStep 3: Repeat mergingRecount pairs. lo + v is highest. Merge → lov. Then lov + e → love. Then c + a + t + s → cats.I␣love␣catsFinal tokens → IDsI0␣1love2␣1cats3This sequence [0, 1, 2, 1, 3] is what gets fed to the modelContinue

title: Tokenization - From Words to Numbers
text: mple: GPT tokenizerModern tokenizers like GPT-2 use learned vocabularies of ~50,000 tokens. One sentence becomes a sequence of IDs:The cat sat on the mat last weekThe464cat3797sat2906on319the262mat4416last1297week1285Sequence: [464, 3797, 2906, 319, 262, 4416, 1297, 1285]Each token ID points to a learned embedding (a 768-dimensional vector in GPT-3). The model never sees text - only numbers.

title: Self-Supervised Learning - How GPT Trains
text: Self-Supervised Learning: the trick behind GPTGPT learns from raw text with NO labels. It doesn't need humans to annotate. Instead, it creates its own training signal by predicting the next token.One simple rule: given all the words so far, predict what comes next.Continue

title: Self-Supervised Learning - How GPT Trains
text: "The cat sat on the mat"One sentence becomes FIVE training examples. Each one says: "given these words, the next word is..."INPUT:"The"PREDICT:cat[3797]INPUT:"The cat"PREDICT:sat[2906]INPUT:"The cat sat"PREDICT:on[319]INPUT:"The cat sat on"PREDICT:the[262]INPUT:"The cat sat on the"PREDICT:mat[4416]From a tiny 8-word sentence, we get 5 (input, target) pairs. From 1 billion words, we get 1 billion training examples. For FREE.Continue

title: Self-Supervised Learning - How GPT Trains
text: he model: raw predictionWhen we give the model "The cat sat on", it outputs probabilities for the next token. Let's say its distribution is:the35%✓cat15%mat12%on10%(all others)28%The model predicted "the" with 35% confidence. The correct answer is "the". Good! But not perfect - 35% is not 100%.Continue

title: Self-Supervised Learning - How GPT Trains
text: w wrong was the prediction?We use cross-entropy loss to measure the gap. For this example:Loss = -log(0.35) = 1.05Higher probability → lower loss. If we predicted 1.0 (100%), loss = 0. If 0.1 (10%), loss ≈ 2.3.P=1.00Loss=0.00Perfect predictionP=0.50Loss=0.69So-so predictionP=0.35Loss=1.05Our exampleP=0.10Loss=2.30Bad predictionContinue

title: Self-Supervised Learning - How GPT Trains
text: -supervised worksThe model learns to predict next words by trial and error on BILLIONS of examples. Through this alone, it learns:📖GrammarPredicting next word requires understanding subject-verb agreement, tenses🧠FactsTo predict 'The capital of France is ___', it must memorize that Paris is the answer🎯ReasoningTo predict output of a math problem, it must perform the computation internally💬Style transferAfter SFT, the model uses this learned knowledge to follow human instructionsContinue

title: Self-Supervised Learning - How GPT Trains
text: raining: gradient descentAfter each batch of examples, the model computes loss, then uses backprop to adjust weights to reduce future loss.Iteration 1: avg loss on batch = 4.2 → adjust weightsIteration 2: avg loss on batch = 3.8 → adjust weightsIteration 3: avg loss on batch = 3.1 → adjust weights...After 1 trillion tokens: avg loss on batch = 0.8The model gets better at predicting. This is "pretraining" - GPT-3 trained for 300+ billion tokens.

title: Cross-Entropy Loss - The LLM Score
text: How do we grade the model?The model predicts probabilities for every word in the vocabulary. But how do we turn that into one number that says "how good are your predictions?"We need a loss function - a single score that tells us how wrong the model was. Lower loss = better predictions. The loss function used by every LLM is called cross-entropy loss.Continue

title: Cross-Entropy Loss - The LLM Score
text: ula: Loss = -log(P)The Cross-Entropy Loss FormulaCross-entropy loss formula as mathematical notation: Loss equals negative log of P_correctLoss=-loge(Pcorrect)← the probability the modelassigned to the actualnext word↑ negative sign flips itIn plain English: take the natural log of how confident the model was in the right answer, then flip the sign.The graph of -log(P): why this shape mattersPlotted negative log probability curve showing loss vs probability with annotated points demonstrating exponential penalty for low confidenceLoss (-log P)probability assigned to correct word0.01.02.03.04.00.00.20.40.60.81.0P=0.95confident & rightP=0.50coin flipP=0.10wrong guessP=0.01very wrongBut wait - how does the formula know what the right answer is?The formula is Loss = -ln(Pcorrect). But where does "correct" come from? It is NOT inside the formula. It comes from the training data.During training, the model sees real text like "The cat sat on the mat". We already know the next word is "mat" because it is right there in the sentence. The model outputs 50,000 probabilities (one per word). We reach into that list, pull out the one probability the model assigned to "mat", and plug THAT number into the formula.Model output: {the: 2%, cat: 5%, mat: 62%, dog: 1%, ...}Correct answer (from training data): "mat"So Pcorrect = P("mat") = 0.62Loss = -ln(0.62) = 0.48The "comparison" between actual and expected is implicit: we only look at the probability the model gave to the known correct word. If that probability is high, loss is low. If that probability is low, loss is high. There is no subtraction (actual - expected). The entire comparison is: "how confident were you in the right answer?"What does this graph mean for our model?Model gives "mat" P=0.95 (the correct answer from training data)Loss = 0.05Almost zero loss. The model was confident and correct. Barely any weight adjustment needed.Model gives "mat" P=0.50 (correct answer, but model wasn't sure)Loss = 0.69Medium loss. Right word, but not confident enough. Weights get nudged to be more confident next time.Model gives "mat" P=0.01 (correct answer, but model almost ignored it)Loss = 4.60Massive loss. The model was almost certain "mat" was wrong - but it was right. Weights get a huge correction.The curve is steep on the left (low probability = massive penalty) and flat on the right (high probability = tiny penalty). This is exactly what we want: the model gets punished harshly for being confidently wrong, but barely punished when it's already doing well.Continue

title: Cross-Entropy Loss - The LLM Score
text: ? The intuitionImagine you're a weather forecaster. You say "70% chance of rain". If it does rain, you did well (70% ≠ 100%, but decent). If it doesn't rain, you were wrong (you said 70%!).Loss measures your mistake: Loss = -log(your prediction for what actually happened)You: It WILL rain (P=0.95)Reality: IT DID RAINLoss = 0.05 (A+)You: Maybe rain (P=0.50)Reality: It rainedLoss = 0.69 (C)You: Probably dry (P=0.10)Reality: It rainedLoss = 2.30 (F)Continue

title: Cross-Entropy Loss - The LLM Score
text: tropy formula in detailWhen predicting one token from a 50K vocabulary:Loss = -loge(Pcorrect)where P_correct is between 0 and 1 (it's a probability)P = 0.99L=0.01P = 0.90L=0.10P = 0.50L=0.69P = 0.10L=2.30P = 0.01L=4.60As probability increases, loss approaches zero. As probability drops, loss explodes. This penalizes confident wrong answers.Continue

title: Cross-Entropy Loss - The LLM Score
text: s-entropy, not MSE?MSE (Mean Squared Error) is the default loss for predicting numbers - like "predict the house price." But language models predict probabilities over a vocabulary. Why does that need a different loss?MSE LossFormula: (P_pred - P_true)²Example: P_pred=0.1, P_true=1.0 → (0.1-1.0)² = 0.81Why: Treats small & large errors equally. Doesn't penalize confidence enough.Cross-Entropy LossFormula: -log(P_correct)Example: P_pred=0.1 when true=1.0 → -log(0.1) = 2.30Why: HEAVILY punishes confident mistakes. Correct!Continue

title: Cross-Entropy Loss - The LLM Score
text: ss: averaging across examplesDuring training, we compute loss on a BATCH of examples, then take the mean:Example 1: Predict 'sat' → model says P=0.4 → loss = 0.92Example 2: Predict 'on' → model says P=0.8 → loss = 0.22Example 3: Predict 'mat' → model says P=0.1 → loss = 2.30Batch Loss = (0.92 + 0.22 + 2.30) / 3 = 1.15The model updates weights to reduce this batch loss. Then the next batch is loaded and loss is recomputed.

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: Our tiny world: only 20 words existForget 50,000 words. Our model only knows these 20 words. Every word gets a number (its ID). The model never sees letters - only these numbers.[0] the[1] cat[2] sat[3] on[4] mat[5] a[6] dog[7] ran[8] is[9] was[10] big[11] small[12] red[13] blue[14] and[15] in[16] it[17] my[18] to[19] withContinue

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: t: "The cat sat on" - predict what?We feed in four words. The model's job: figure out what word #5 should be.The [0]→cat [1]→sat [2]→on [3]→???Continue

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: al network: what it actually looks likeThe last input word ("on") gets converted to numbers, flows through hidden layers where every circle does: multiply inputs by weights → add up → apply activation. Then the output layer has one circle for every word in our vocabulary (all 20).Input(word: 'on')0001→weightsHidden 1(8 circles)→weightsHidden 2(8 circles)→weightsOutput(20 words)thecatsatonmat...withEvery line is a weight - a number that was learned during training. The input (4 numbers representing "on") gets multiplied by weights, transformed, multiplied by more weights, transformed again, until it reaches the 20 output circles. Each output circle produces one score for one word.Continue

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: put circle computes a score (called a "logit")After the input flows through all the hidden layers, the final layer has 20 circles. Each circle collects numbers from the layer before it, multiplies each by its own weight, and adds them up. That sum is the score for that word.Example: How the "mat" circle computes its scoreValues arriving from previous layer: [2.1, -0.8, 1.5, 0.3, -1.2, 0.9, 1.7, -0.4]Weights for "mat" circle: [0.6, 0.3, 0.8, 0.1, -0.5, 0.2, 0.9, 0.4]Score = (2.1 x 0.6) + (-0.8 x 0.3) + (1.5 x 0.8) + (0.3 x 0.1) + (-1.2 x -0.5) + (0.9 x 0.2) + (1.7 x 0.9) + (-0.4 x 0.4)= 1.26 + (-0.24) + 1.20 + 0.03 + 0.60 + 0.18 + 1.53 + (-0.16)Score for "mat" = 4.40The same thing happens for all 20 output circles, each with its own set of weights. So "the" circle has different weights, "cat" circle has different weights, etc. That's why they produce different scores.Continue

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: es for all 20 wordsEach output circle has computed its score. Here is what the model produced for input "The cat sat on":the+4.2mat+3.8a+2.1in+1.6was+1.4cat+1.1and+0.9dog+0.8to+0.7is+0.5sat+0.3it+0.2ran-0.2on-0.5with-0.9big-1.5red-1.8small-2.1blue-2.3my-3.1Notice: "the" and "mat" have the highest scores. "my" is deeply negative. But these are not probabilities yet - they are just raw numbers. We need to convert them.Continue

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: scores to probabilities (softmax)Two simple steps: raise e (2.718) to the power of each score, then divide each result by the total. This makes everything positive and add up to 100%.WordScoree^scoreProbthe+4.2→66.69→46.0%mat+3.8→44.70→30.8%a+2.1→8.17→5.6%in+1.6→4.95→3.4%was+1.4→4.06→2.8%cat+1.1→3.00→2.1%and+0.9→2.46→1.7%dog+0.8→2.23→1.5%...blue-2.3→0.10→0.1%my-3.1→0.05→0.0%Sum of all e^score = 144.97 → each probability = e^score / 144.97Continue

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: l answer: probability for each wordThis is what the model outputs. A probability for every single one of the 20 words. The highest probability is the model's best guess for the next word.the46.0%mat30.8%a5.6%in3.4%was2.8%cat2.1%and1.7%dog1.5%to1.4%is1.1%...plus 10 more words with less than 1% eachModel's prediction: "the" (46.0%) → "The cat sat on the"The actual next word in training was "the" → loss = -log(0.460) = 0.78Not bad! But not 100% confident. Backprop will nudge the weights to make it more confident next time.Continue

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: insight: it is ALL just multiply and addThe entire process - from input word to 20 probabilities - is nothing but:1. Take numbers in - the word "on" becomes [0, 0, 0, 1, 0, ...] or an embedding vector2. Multiply by weights, add up, apply activation - each circle in each hidden layer does this3. Repeat through every layer - GPT-2 has 12 layers, GPT-4 has ~100+ layers4. Last layer: one circle per vocabulary word - 20 circles for us, 50,000 for real GPT5. Softmax to get probabilities - exponentiate and divide. Now they sum to 100%That's it. No magic. The only "smart" part is the weights - the numbers on each connection line. Training (gradient descent) adjusts those weights trillions of times until the multiply-and-add chain produces good predictions.

title: The Neural Network in Action
text: How LLMs Actually Train: The Neural Network in Action. This chapter covers the neural network in action.

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: What is a hidden state?We keep saying the neural network "processes" the input. But what does that actually produce? The answer is a hidden state - and understanding it is the key to understanding this entire chapter.Step 1: Input sentence enters the modelThecatsatontheEach word gets converted to a vector of 768 numbers (the embedding from chapter 2.1). So "the" becomes [-0.14, 0.62, -0.38, ...]Step 2: Hidden layers transform these vectors, one layer at a timeLayer 1Learns basic patterns: "the" is an article, "cat" is a nounLayer 2Learns combinations: "the cat" is a noun phraseLayer 6Learns grammar: "sat on the" expects a location nextLayer 12 (final)Encodes the full understanding: what word should come nextEach layer takes in 768 numbers and outputs 768 new numbers. The vector gets refined and enriched at every step.Step 3: The final layer's output = the hidden stateAfter ALL layers have processed the input, the last layer outputs a vector of 768 numbers for the last token ("the"). This final vector is called the hidden state.Hidden state for "the" (768 numbers):[-0.14, 0.62, -0.38, 1.05, -0.72, 0.91, ..., 0.21]It is called "hidden" because you never see it - it lives inside the model between layers. It is called a "state" because it represents the model's current understanding. These 768 numbers encode everything: the meaning of "the cat sat on the", the grammar, the context, and a prediction of what word should come next.Now we have this hidden state - 768 numbers that encode the model's understanding. But we need a word, not 768 numbers. The vocabulary has 50,000 tokens. How do we go from 768 numbers to a prediction?Continue

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: den state to scores: what is a logit?We have the hidden state: 768 numbers. The model needs to decide which word comes next. There are 50,000 words in the vocabulary. So the model computes a score for every single word. Higher score = "I think this word is more likely to come next".Example: scores for "The cat sat on the ___"The model computes one score for each of its 50,000 vocabulary words:"mat"+8.2"floor"+7.5"sofa"+6.1"the"+0.3"because"-3.7"photosynthesis"-8.9...and 49,994 more words, each with a scoreThese scores are called "logits"That is all a logit is - the raw score the model gives to a word. Nothing more. The name comes from mathematics ("log-odds"), but for our purposes: logit = score. Note: "logit" is the name for the value that comes out - not the layer and not the neuron. The layer is called the "output projection" or "LM head." The logit is the number it produces.Positive logit (+8.2) = the model thinks this word is a good fitNear-zero logit (+0.3) = the model has no strong opinionNegative logit (-8.9) = the model thinks this word is a terrible fitLogits are NOT probabilitiesLogits are raw numbers. They can be -100 or +100 or anything. They do not add up to 100%. They are not percentages. To turn them into actual probabilities (where everything adds to 100%), we need one more step called softmax - which we will see in a later step of this chapter.So the pipeline so far: hidden state (768 numbers) → ??? → 50,000 logits → softmax → probabilities. The missing piece: how does the model go from 768 numbers to 50,000 logits? That is what the unembedding layer does.Continue

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: bedding Layer: from 768 numbers to 50,000 scoresThere is one more layer after all the hidden layers. It is called the unembedding layer (or output projection). Here is what the full pipeline looks like:Deep neural network output layer diagram showing hidden state neurons connected through an unembedding weight matrix to vocabulary output neuronsLayer 1Layer 2Layer N(= Hidden State)UnembeddingOutput(50K logits)Wn₁n₂n₃n₁n₂n₃.........h₁h₂h₇₆₈themat...dogeach layer has 768 neurons - only 3 shown for clarityThe hidden state (768 numbers) gets multiplied by the unembedding matrix (768 x 50,000). The result: one logit for every word in the vocabulary.Continue

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: it actually work? One dot product per wordThe unembedding matrix has 50,000 rows. Each row is a 768-dimensional vector that represents one vocabulary word. To get the logit for a word, we take the dot product of the hidden state with that word's row.Example: computing the logit for "mat"Hidden state (768 values): [-0.14, 0.62, -0.38, 1.05, ...]Row for "mat" in unembedding matrix: [0.45, 0.82, -0.21, 0.67, ...]logit = dot product = (-0.14 x 0.45) + (0.62 x 0.82) + (-0.38 x -0.21) + (1.05 x 0.67) + ...= -0.063 + 0.508 + 0.080 + 0.704 + ... (768 terms added up)logit for "mat" = 8.2This happens for ALL 50,000 words in parallel. Each word has its own row, its own dot product, its own logit. Words whose rows are "similar" to the hidden state get high scores. Words whose rows point in a different direction get low scores.Continue

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: lt: logits for every word in the vocabularyAfter computing all 50,000 dot products for "The cat sat on the", we get raw scores (logits):mat+8.2floor+7.5sofa+6.1table+5.8bed+5.2she-1.4because-3.7photosynthesis-8.9...plus 49,992 more tokens with their own scoresThese are just raw numbers - they can be negative, large, or small. They are not probabilities yet. We need softmax to convert them.Continue

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: from raw logits to probability distributionSoftmax does two things: makes every number positive (using e^score), then divides by the total so everything adds up to 100%.Worked example for the top 5 tokens:matlogit=8.2→ e^8.2 = 3641→56.8%floorlogit=7.5→ e^7.5 = 1808→28.2%sofalogit=6.1→ e^6.1 = 446→7%tablelogit=5.8→ e^5.8 = 330→5.2%bedlogit=5.2→ e^5.2 = 181→2.8%probability = e^logit / sum of ALL e^logits across 50,000 tokensThe model predicts "mat" with 56.8% confidence. This is the final output - a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary. During training, we compare this to the actual next word and compute the loss.Continue

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: one simple layer work? The hidden layers did the hard partThe output layer is just a matrix multiply - no activation function, no non-linearity. It seems too simple. But the hidden layers already did all the hard work.All hidden layers (many layers deep)Understand context, grammar, meaning. Encode 'what should come next' into the 768-dim hidden state.parameter count: billions of parametersOutput layer (1 matrix)Translate the hidden state into a logit for each vocabulary word. Just one dot product per word.parameter count: 768 x 50K = 38.4M parametersThe output layer has less than 0.02% of the total parameters. It is tiny. All the intelligence lives in the hidden layers.Continue

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: er trick: weight tyingRemember the embedding layer from chapter 2.1? It converts tokens to vectors. The embedding matrix has shape 50,000 x 768 - one 768-dim row per word.Embedding matrix (input side)Shape: 50,000 x 768. Converts word → vector.Row for "mat" = [0.45, 0.82, -0.21, 0.67, ...]Unembedding matrix (output side)Shape: 768 x 50,000. Converts hidden state → one logit per word.This is just the embedding matrix transposed!This is called weight tying. The same matrix is used for both embedding and unembedding (just transposed). The model only needs to learn one set of word vectors, not two.Why does this work? A visual exampleImagine the hidden state encodes: "something you sit on, on the floor". During embedding, similar words were placed near each other in the 768-dim space:"mat"[0.45, 0.82, -0.21, ...]92%"rug"[0.43, 0.79, -0.19, ...]89%"carpet"[0.41, 0.80, -0.23, ...]87%"table"[0.30, 0.55, 0.12, ...]58%"sky"[-0.60, 0.10, 0.85, ...]5%The dot product between the hidden state and each word's embedding measures similarity. "mat", "rug", and "carpet" have embeddings pointing in a similar direction to the hidden state, so their dot products (logits) are high. "sky" points in a completely different direction, so its logit is near zero.Weight tying enforces this: if "mat" and "rug" were learned as similar meanings at input, they will automatically get similar logits at output. The model only learns one definition per word, and it works in both directions.

title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
