title: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: Diagram in The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words: Deep neural network output layer diagram showing hidden state neurons connected through an unembedding weight matrix to vocabulary output neurons, illustrating how hidden states become word predictions

title: Autoregressive Generation - One Token at a Time
text: Two very different modesThe model has two lives. During training, it sees entire sentences and learns to predict the next word at every position. But when you actually use it (generation), it has to build the answer one word at a time.Training (learning phase)ThecatsatonthematThe model sees ALL words at once. At each position, it predicts the next word and checks if it was right. Fast - one pass through the whole sentence.Generation (using the model)Thecatsaton???The model only has the prompt. It must predict the next word, add it, then predict the next, add it... one word at a time. This is called autoregressive generation.Continue

title: Autoregressive Generation - One Token at a Time
text: build the sentence, word by wordPrompt: "The cat sat on". At each step, the full pipeline runs: tokens go in, hidden layers process them, output layer produces 50,000 probabilities via softmax, and the model picks one word.Step 1Thecatsaton→hidden layers → output layer → softmax →"the"72%"a"15%"that"8%Pick "the" → append it to the input for the next stepStep 2Thecatsatonthe→hidden layers → output layer → softmax →"mat"68%"floor"18%"sofa"7%Pick "mat" → append it to the input for the next stepStep 3Thecatsatonthemat→hidden layers → output layer → softmax →"last"45%"all"22%"and"15%Pick "last" → append it to the input for the next stepStep 4Thecatsatonthematlast→hidden layers → output layer → softmax →"week"91%"night"5%"time"2%Pick "week" → append it to the input for the next stepFinal resultThecatsatonthematlastweekCyan = original prompt. Green = generated by the model, one at a time.Continue

title: Autoregressive Generation - One Token at a Time
text: the model pick a word?The model outputs a probability for every word. But how do we choose one? There are three strategies:Greedy: always pick the highest probability"the"72%← always this one"a"15%"that"8%"his"3%Same input always gives same output. Safe and consistent, but can get repetitive - the model might loop the same phrase.Sampling: roll the dice (weighted by probability)Imagine spinning a wheel where "the" takes up 72% of the wheel, "a" takes 15%, and so on. Wherever the wheel lands, that is the word we pick.the: 72%a: 15%that: 8%his: 3%...: 2%More creative and diverse. But sometimes picks low-probability words that make no sense.Top-K: sample, but only from the best K optionsKeep only the top 10 (or 40, or 100) most likely words. Throw out the rest. Then sample from those."the"72%kept"a"15%kept"that"8%kept"photosynthesis"0.001%removedThe sweet spot: creative enough to surprise you, but never picks something completely absurd.Continue

title: Autoregressive Generation - One Token at a Time
text: ure: the creativity dialBefore softmax, we divide each logit by a number called temperature. This controls how "spread out" the probabilities are.Same logits, different temperaturesRaw logits: "the"=4.2, "a"=2.8, "that"=1.5Temperature = 0.2 (cold)Dividing by 0.2 makes logits huge (21, 14, 7.5). Softmax crushes everything onto the winner."the"99%"a"1%"that"0%Temperature = 1.0 (default)Logits stay the same. Probabilities reflect the model's true confidence."the"72%"a"18%"that"10%Temperature = 2.0 (hot)Dividing by 2.0 makes logits small (2.1, 1.4, 0.75). Softmax spreads probability more evenly."the"45%"a"30%"that"25%The formulasoftmax( logit / temperature )Low temperature → winner takes all. High temperature → more even distribution. Temperature = 0 can't go into the formula (division by zero!), so in practice T=0 means argmax - just pick the highest logit, skip softmax entirely. Most APIs use T=0.2 as the lowest practical value.Continue

title: Autoregressive Generation - One Token at a Time
text: this work? And when does it stop?Predicting one word at a time sounds fragile. But it works because of two key facts:Full context at every stepAt step 4, the model does not just see "last". It sees ALL previous words: "The cat sat on the mat last". Every generated word gets the benefit of the entire history. The hidden layers process all of these words together, so the model never "forgets" what came before.Thecatsatonthematlast→ all visible at step 4Training matches generationThe model was trained on exactly this task: given the words so far, predict the next one. So generation is not some new challenge - it is the exact thing the model practiced trillions of times during training.Self-correction is built inEven if the model picks a slightly unexpected word at step 2, it adjusts at step 3. The full context includes the "mistake", and the model learned during training how to continue coherently from any plausible word. Errors rarely compound.When does it stop?The vocabulary includes a special stop token (like <END> or <|endoftext|>). When the model assigns the highest probability to this token, generation stops. It is a learned behavior - the model saw thousands of sentences ending during training and learned to predict the stop token at natural endpoints.Step 5 input: "The cat sat on the mat last week"Model predicts: <END> with 85% confidence → generation completeIn practice, there is also a max length limit (like 4096 tokens). If the model has not produced a stop token by then, generation is cut off.

title: Autoregressive Generation - One Token at a Time
text: How LLMs Actually Train: Autoregressive Generation - One Token at a Time. This chapter covers autoregressive generation - one token at a time.

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
text: The problem: the model just predicts internet textAfter pretraining, the model has read trillions of words from the internet. It can predict the next word really well. But it has no idea that it should be helpful.If you type "What is photosynthesis?", the model does not think "I should answer this question." It thinks: "what text would typically follow this on the internet?"You type: "What is photosynthesis?"On the internet, this could be followed by...A Wikipedia-style answer15%Another question: "What about cellular respiration?"12%A Reddit comment: "just Google it lol"10%A spam link or ad8%A sarcastic reply6%The model gives probability to ALL of these because it saw all of them during pretraining. We need to change this.Continue

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
text: show it what a good assistant saysHumans sit down and write ~100,000 example conversations. Each one has the exact format the model will see when deployed:User:What is photosynthesis?Assistant:Photosynthesis is the process where plants convert light energy into chemical energy. It happens in two stages: light-dependent reactions in the thylakoid, and the Calvin cycle in the stroma.User:Write a Python function to reverse a list.Assistant:def reverse_list(lst): return lst[::-1]Each example says: "When a user asks this, this is what you should say." The model will now train on these conversations instead of random internet text.Continue

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
text: trick: loss only on the assistant's wordsThe model reads the full conversation and predicts the next word at every position - same as pretraining. But the loss is only calculated on the assistant's words. The user's question is just context.User:next: Whatno loss - just readingUser: Whatnext: isno loss - just readingUser: What isnext: photosynthesis?no loss - just reading...photosynthesis?next: Assistant:no loss - just readingAssistant:next: PhotosynthesisLOSS (model guessed "What" at 12%, correct was 8%)...Photosynthesisnext: isLOSS (model guessed "is" at 25%, correct was 25%)...Photosynthesis isnext: theLOSS (model guessed "a" at 18%, correct was 15%)...is thenext: processLOSS (model guessed "study" at 11%, correct was 9%)The model is never graded on predicting the user's question. It is only graded on how well it predicts the ideal assistant response. Backpropagation then nudges the weights to make the correct response words more likely next time.Continue

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
text: s After SFT: what changes in the outputAfter 100,000 training conversations, the weights have shifted. The model now strongly favors helpful assistant text after "Assistant:". Here is the same input, same model, different probabilities for the first word:Before SFTpredicts generic internet textWhat12%The10%Photosynthesis8%I6%So5%It4%After SFTpredicts helpful assistant responsePhotosynthesis35%The8%It5%What2%I2%So1%"Photosynthesis" jumped from 8% to 35% because in the SFT data, answers to "What is X?" almost always start with "X is..." The weights got nudged thousands of times in that direction. "What" dropped from 12% to 2% because good assistants don't repeat the question back.Continue

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
text: en template: what you never seeWhen you type a message to ChatGPT or Claude, you never see the full text that goes to the model. Behind the scenes, your message gets wrapped in a template:What you type:"What is photosynthesis?"What actually goes to the model:User: What is photosynthesis?Assistant:The model then starts predicting what comes after "Assistant:" one word at a time. Because SFT trained it on thousands of examples where helpful text followed "Assistant:", it now strongly favors producing that kind of text.Different models use different template formats. ChatGPT uses special tokens:<|im_start|>systemYou are a helpful assistant.<|im_end|><|im_start|>userWhat is photosynthesis?<|im_end|><|im_start|>assistantPhotosynthesis is the process by which plants...<|im_end|> ← model generates this token to signal "I am done"Everything above <|im_start|>assistant is the template - sent to the model as input. The response text and the final <|im_end|> are generated by the model. It learns during SFT that after producing a complete answer, it should output <|im_end|> to stop.This is also why system prompts work. The "You are a helpful assistant" text sits before the user's message. The model sees all of it as context and adjusts its style accordingly. Change it to "You are a pirate" and the model changes how it speaks.Continue

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
text: examples is enoughPretraining needed trillions of words. SFT needs only ~100K conversations. That feels wrong. Why is so little data enough?Because SFT is not teaching new knowledge. The model already knows what photosynthesis is, already knows Python, already knows grammar. All of that was learned during pretraining and is stored deep in the weights.SFT only changes the surface behavior - the pattern of "when you see User: followed by a question, respond with a clear, helpful answer." That is a much simpler pattern than all of human language.PretrainingData: Trillions of wordsWhat it learns: All of language: grammar, facts, code, reasoning, styleWhat changes: Builds the entire brain from scratchSFTData: ~100K conversationsWhat it learns: One pattern: question goes in, helpful answer comes outWhat changes: Nudges the surface weights slightlySFT uses a very small learning rate (tiny nudges per step). The deep weights that store facts barely move. Only the surface-level behavior changes - the "style" of what follows "Assistant:". Same knowledge, expressed as a helpful conversation instead of random internet text.

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
text: How LLMs Actually Train: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). This chapter covers supervised fine-tuning (sft).

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: RLHF: Reinforcement Learning from Human FeedbackAfter SFT, the model is better - but not perfect. It may be verbose, unsafe, or disagreeable. How do we optimize for qualities humans care about?RLHF is a 4-step process that uses human judgments to train a reward model, then uses that to optimize the LLM.Continue

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: Generate multiple responsesFor a given prompt, the SFT model generates MULTIPLE candidate responses. Temperature controls diversity.Prompt: "What's the best programming language for beginners?"Python is best - simple syntax, huge libraries, great community.← Rank #1: Clear & actionablePython or Go. Python for web, Go for systems. Depends on goals.← Rank #2: NuancedJavaScript. Runs everywhere, but quirky syntax can confuse newcomers.← Rank #3: Decent but flawed reasoningRust. Memory safety. Compile-time errors catch bugs early.← Rank #4: Incorrect - too hard for beginnersI am an AI and cannot learn programming.← Rank #5: NonsensicalContinue

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: Human annotators rank responsesHumans read pairs of responses and judge: "Which is better?" This creates a dataset of pairwise comparisons.Response A:Python is best - simple syntax, huge libraries, great community.Response B:Python or Go. Python for web, Go for systems. Depends on goals.Winner: BReason: More nuanced. Acknowledges tradeoffs.Response A:Python is best - simple syntax, huge libraries, great community.Response B:Rust. Memory safety. Compile-time errors catch bugs early.Winner: AReason: A is actually beginner-friendly. B is too complex.From tens of thousands of such comparisons, we build training data for a reward model.Continue

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: Train a reward modelThe reward model is a separate neural network. Its job is simple: take a prompt and a response, and output a single number - how good is this response? Higher number = more helpful, safer, better.Input: a prompt + a complete response (as one block of text)↓Output: one single number (e.g., 8.5)How does it learn to score?From the human comparisons in Step 2. Each comparison is a training example: a winner response and a loser response for the same prompt.Prompt: "Best language for beginners?"WINNERPython - simple syntax, huge community, tons of tutorials.LOSERRust. Memory safety. Compile-time errors catch bugs early.Python is actually beginner-friendly. Rust is not.Prompt: "Is the earth flat?"WINNERNo, the Earth is roughly spherical. Here is the evidence...LOSERThere are many perspectives on this topic...Direct and accurate. Does not treat misinformation as legitimate.The reward model is trained with one rule: the winner must get a higher score than the loser. After seeing tens of thousands of these pairs, it learns to detect what makes a response helpful, accurate, and safe.Continue

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: aining: the reward model can score any responsePython - simple, huge community.8.5Depends... Python vs Go...7.2Rust is safest.2.1I cannot answer.1.0Now we have a machine that can grade any response automatically. This is what PPO will use in Step 4 - instead of asking a human to grade every response (too slow and expensive), we ask the reward model (instant and free).Continue

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: PPO - How the model actually improvesPPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) is the training loop that uses the reward model to make the LLM better. Here is exactly what happens, step by step, for one training round:1GenerateGive the model a prompt like "Best language for beginners?" and let it write a full response. It produces: "Python is great for beginners because of its simple syntax and large community."2ScoreFeed the prompt + response into the reward model. It outputs a single number: 8.5 out of 10. This means the response is helpful, clear, and accurate.3CompareThe original SFT model (before PPO training started) also looks at the same prompt. We measure: how different is the PPO model's response from what the SFT model would have said? This difference is called the KL divergence. If the PPO model has drifted too far from the SFT model, we penalize it.4NudgeIf reward was high and the model hasn't drifted too far: nudge the weights to make this kind of response MORE likely. If reward was low: nudge the weights to make this kind of response LESS likely. If the model drifted too far from SFT: pull it back, even if the reward was high.This loop repeats thousands of times. Generate, score, compare, nudge. Over time, the model learns to produce responses that get high reward scores while staying close to the well-behaved SFT model.Continue

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: KL (Kullback-Leibler) divergence? A concrete exampleKL divergence just measures: how different are the PPO model's word predictions from the original SFT model's predictions? Here is a real comparison for the first word after "Best language for beginners?":SFT model (original)Python40%I15%The12%others33%PPO model (after 500 rounds)Python85%I3%The2%others10%The SFT model was spread out (40%, 15%, 12%...). The PPO model is concentrated (85% on one word). These distributions look very different, so the KL divergence is high: 1.8.Continue

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: ula: Final Score = Reward - (β x KL)RewardHow good is this response? (from the reward model). Higher = better.KLHow far has the model drifted from the SFT model? Higher = more different.β (beta)A dial engineers set (0.1 to 0.5). Higher β = stricter penalty for drifting.Three examples with β = 0.2:"Python - simple syntax, huge community, tons of tutorials."Final = 8.5 - (0.2 x 0.02) = 8.5 - 0.00 = 8.50Good response, barely drifted. Almost full reward kept."Just learn Python I guess, whatever works, who cares."Final = 3.1 - (0.2 x 0.15) = 3.1 - 0.03 = 3.07Lazy response. Low reward, small nudge."PYTHON PYTHON PYTHON PYTHON BEST BEST BEST!!!"Final = 9.2 - (0.2 x 24.00) = 9.2 - 4.80 = 4.40Reward hacking! High score but model went crazy. KL penalty wipes out the reward entirely.Why is the KL penalty necessary?Without it, the model finds shortcuts. The reward model is not perfect - it has blind spots. The PPO model can discover responses that trick the reward model into giving high scores even though a human would hate them. Repeating keywords, using an overly confident tone, or stuffing filler words that the reward model likes. The KL penalty prevents this by saying: "you can improve, but you cannot become a completely different model."Continue

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: matters: what it fixes that SFT cannotSFT teaches the model to follow instructions by showing it good examples. But it has a fundamental limit: it can only learn from the exact examples humans wrote. RLHF goes further.The problemSFT can only copy the style of examples it sawHow RLHF fixes itThe reward model can score any new response the model invents, even ones no human ever wrote. So the model can discover better responses than exist in the SFT training data.The problemYou cannot write a formula for 'helpful'How RLHF fixes itCross-entropy measures prediction accuracy. But 'helpful', 'safe', 'honest' are fuzzy human judgments. The reward model learns these from human comparisons, then PPO optimizes for them.The problemSFT model can still produce harmful responsesHow RLHF fixes itHumans rate unsafe responses low. The reward model learns to give low scores to harmful content. PPO then nudges the weights away from generating such content.

title: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe
text: How LLMs Actually Train: RLHF - Making AI Helpful & Safe. This chapter covers rlhf - making ai helpful & safe.

title: DPO - Simpler Alignment
text: RLHF Works - But It Needs 4 ModelsIn chapter 2.8 we learned the RLHF pipeline. It aligns the model with human values - but look at what it takes:1. Policy modelThe LLM being trained - generates responses2. Reference modelFrozen copy of the policy before RLHF - prevents drift3. Reward modelSeparate neural net that scores how 'good' a response is4. Value modelAnother neural net that estimates future rewards for PPOThat is 4 models in GPU memory at once. Plus RL training (PPO) is unstable - the reward model can be gamed, hyperparameters are tricky, and training often crashes.Can we get the same result with less machinery?Continue

title: DPO - Simpler Alignment
text: p the Reward Model EntirelyDirect Preference Optimization (Rafailov et al., 2023) has a simple insight:Why train a separate judge to score responses, then do complex RL to optimize for those scores... when you can just directly adjust the model to prefer what humans preferred?Think of it like this. RLHF says: "Hire a food critic, have them score every dish, then optimize your cooking to maximize the critic's score." DPO says: "Just ask diners which dish they liked better, and directly learn from their preference."RLHFPreferences → Train reward model → RL loop (PPO) → Better policy3 training stages, 4 modelsDPOPreferences → Better policy1 training stage, 2 modelsDPO folds the reward model and RL loop into a single supervised loss function. Let's see exactly how.Continue

title: DPO - Simpler Alignment
text: Training Step - Real ExampleDPO needs exactly what RLHF's Step 2 already collected: human preference pairs. Here is one:Prompt: "What's the best programming language for beginners?"Preferred (Winner)"Python - simple syntax, huge libraries, great community."Rejected (Loser)"Rust. Memory safety. Compile-time errors catch bugs early."A human said: "The Python answer is better." That single judgment is ALL DPO needs for this training step. No scores, no rankings - just "A is better than B."What DPO will do:Nudge the model to assign higher probability to the Python answer, and lower probability to the Rust answer - but not too much, so the model does not go off the rails.Continue

title: DPO - Simpler Alignment
text: Ratio - Measuring ChangeDPO compares the current model against a frozen reference copy (the model before DPO training). This prevents the model from changing too much.For each response, we ask: "How much did the model's probability change relative to the reference?"Reference Model (frozen)P(Python answer) = 0.030P(Rust answer) = 0.025Probabilities are tiny because the model distributes across thousands of possible responsesCurrent Model (training)P(Python answer) = 0.045P(Rust answer) = 0.015Model is learning: boosted preferred, reduced rejectedPreferred log-ratio:log(0.045 / 0.030) = log(1.50) = +0.41Positive! Model boosted the preferred response by 50% relative to reference.Rejected log-ratio:log(0.015 / 0.025) = log(0.60) = -0.51Negative! Model reduced the rejected response by 40% relative to reference.The gap:(+0.41) - (-0.51) = +0.92Positive gap = the model is learning in the right direction. It is simultaneously boosting what humans preferred AND reducing what they rejected.Continue

title: DPO - Simpler Alignment
text: DPO Loss FormulaLoss = -log( sigmoid( beta x gap ) )where gap = log(pi/pi_ref) for preferred - log(pi/pi_ref) for rejectedLet's plug in our numbers with beta = 0.1:1. The gap (from above)+0.922. Scale by beta0.1 x 0.92 = 0.0923. Apply sigmoidsigmoid(0.092) = 0.5234. Take -log-log(0.523) = 0.649Loss = 0.649How does loss decrease?As the gap grows (model gets better at preferring what humans prefer), sigmoid approaches 1, and -log(1) = 0. So the loss falls toward zero. The model is doing exactly what the formula rewards: boost preferred, reduce rejected.Continue

title: DPO - Simpler Alignment
text: he Safety LeashBeta controls how much the model can change from the reference. Same gap of 0.92, two different betas:beta = 0.1 (loose leash)0.1 x 0.92 = 0.092sigmoid(0.092) = 0.523Loss = 0.649Loss is barely below the maximum (0.693). The model needs a HUGE gap to reduce loss much. It keeps changing, drifting far from the reference.beta = 0.5 (tight leash)0.5 x 0.92 = 0.46sigmoid(0.46) = 0.613Loss = 0.489Same gap already gives much lower loss. The model gets "credit" for smaller changes, so it stops sooner. Stays close to the reference.In practice:Most DPO implementations use beta between 0.1 and 0.5. Lower beta = model learns aggressively but risks going off the rails. Higher beta = safer, more conservative changes. Typical default: beta = 0.1.Continue

title: DPO - Simpler Alignment
text: DPO - The Full ComparisonRLHFDPOModels needed4 (policy, ref, reward, value)2 models (policy + reference)Training typeRL loop (PPO) - unstableSupervised loss - stableReward hackingReal risk - model games the reward modelNo reward model to hackHyperparamsMany (PPO clip, LR, KL penalty...)Mainly just betaGPU memory4 models loaded at once2 models loaded at onceQualityHighComparable (within 1-2%)DPO VariantsIPOFixes overfitting by using a squared loss instead of log-sigmoidKTOWorks with thumbs-up/down (no pairs needed)ORPOCombines SFT + alignment in a single training stepSame quality, a fraction of the complexity. DPO and its variants have largely replaced RLHF in practice.

title: Tokenizer Deep Dive - BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
text: Recap: BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding)In chapter 2.1, we learned BPE: start with characters, merge the most frequent pair, repeat. Let's trace "low" through BPE from scratch:Start"l""o""w"Split into individual charactersMerge 1"lo""w"Most frequent pair: l + o → "lo"Merge 2"low"Next frequent pair: lo + w → "low"BPE is used by GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4. It picks merges by raw frequency count.Continue

title: Tokenizer Deep Dive - BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
text: e (used by BERT)Similar to BPE, but instead of raw frequency, WordPiece merges the pair that most increases the training data likelihood.BPE merge criterioncount("lo") = 847 - highest count winsWordPiece merge criterionscore("lo") = count("lo") / (count("l") x count("o")) - likelihood ratio winsKey visual marker: the ## prefix marks sub-word continuations.Example: "playing"play##ingThe ## means "I'm a continuation of the previous token, not a standalone word"Continue

title: Tokenizer Deep Dive - BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
text: Piece (used by LLaMA, T5)SentencePiece takes a fundamentally different approach: it treats the input as raw bytes/unicode. No pre-tokenization step - no word splitting first.BPE / WordPiece: pre-tokenize first"I love cats" → split on spaces → ["I", "love", "cats"] → then apply merges within each wordSentencePiece: raw stream"I love cats" → treat as raw character stream including spaces → apply merges directlyWhy this matters: languages without spacesChineseNo spaces between charactersJapaneseMixed scripts, no consistent word boundariesThaiWords run together without separatorsPre-tokenizers that split on spaces would fail completely. SentencePiece handles all of these naturally because it works on raw bytes.Under the hood, SentencePiece can use either BPE or a Unigram model (which starts with a large vocabulary and prunes it down, the reverse of BPE).Continue

title: Tokenizer Deep Dive - BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
text: nization matters more than you thinkThe same concept can cost wildly different numbers of tokens depending on how it is written:Numbers: inconsistent splits break arithmetic"12345"→ ["123", "45"]2 tokens"67890"→ ["678", "90"]2 tokens"12345 + 67890"→ ["123", "45", " +", " ", "678", "90"]6 tokensThe digit boundary shifts depending on the number. The model never sees "12345" as a single unit, making arithmetic unreliable.Non-English: same meaning, 3x the cost"Hello"1 token1x"Bonjour"1-2 tokens1-2x"Namaste"3 tokens3xEnglish dominates the training data, so English words get single tokens. Other languages pay a token tax.Code: similar words, different token counts"print"→ ["print"]1 token"println"→ ["print", "ln"]2 tokens"printf"→ ["print", "f"]2 tokensMore tokens = slower inference (each token needs a full forward pass through the model).Continue

title: Tokenizer Deep Dive - BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece
text: ry size tradeoffsHow many tokens should the vocabulary contain? It is a direct tradeoff:Bigger vocab (100K)+ Shorter sequences (fewer tokens per sentence)+ Faster inference- More embedding parameters- 100K x 4096 dims = 409M just for embeddingsSmaller vocab (32K)+ Fewer embedding parameters+ 32K x 4096 = 131M for embeddings- Longer sequences (more tokens per sentence)- Slower inferenceReal-world vocabulary sizesGPT-250,257768d38.6MFirst widely-used BPE tokenizerLLaMA32,0004,096d131MSmaller vocab, more languagesGPT-4~100,000~4,096d~409MLargest vocab, shortest sequencesWhy LLaMA chose 32KMeta wanted LLaMA to handle many languages efficiently. A smaller vocab means each token is more "general" (sub-word pieces that appear across languages). The tradeoff: slightly longer sequences, but a much smaller embedding table and better multilingual coverage per parameter.

title: Scaling Laws - Why Bigger Models Win
text: The discovery (2020):OpenAI found that language model performance follows predictable mathematical patterns as you scale three things:Continue

title: Scaling Laws - Why Bigger Models Win
text: e scaling axes:N. ParametersModel size (weights)Examples: 1B → 7B → 70B → 175BD. Data (Tokens)Total training dataExamples: 100B tokens → 1T tokensC. ComputeGPU hours, FLOPs (total math operations - each multiply or add counts as one)Examples: 1 GPU → 1000 GPUsContinue

title: Scaling Laws - Why Bigger Models Win
text: scaling law:Scale all three together → smooth, predictable improvementScale one alone → diminishing returnsLoss ∝ N-α × D-β × C-γPerformance improves as a power law with size.Like a recipe: more flour alone doesn't make more cake without more eggs and butter.Continue

title: Scaling Laws - Why Bigger Models Win
text: mples:GPT-2: 1.5BCan complete text decentlyGPT-3: 175BCan do in-context learning, few-shot promptingGPT-4: ~1.8T (estimated)Can reason, code, multimodal, passes LSATEach jump in scale = major capability leap.Continue

title: Scaling Laws - Why Bigger Models Win
text: tical impact:Train a small model (1B params). Measure performance.Use the scaling law to predict how a 175B model will perform.This saves millions in compute. You don't need to actually train the giant model to forecast results.Continue

title: Scaling Laws - Why Bigger Models Win
text: la (DeepMind 2022): Models Were UndertrainedBefore Chinchilla, the AI world had one strategy: make the model bigger. GPT-3 had 175B parameters - but was only trained on 300B tokens.Think about it. You have a brilliant student (175 billion parameters of "brain capacity") but you only gave them two textbooks to read (300B tokens). That student is undertrained - not too small.The question DeepMind asked:If you have a fixed compute budget (say, $10 million of GPU time), should you spend it on a bigger model with less data, or a smaller model with more data?Continue

title: Scaling Laws - Why Bigger Models Win
text: f: Chinchilla vs GopherDeepMind trained two models with the same compute budget, but split it differently:Gopher (old strategy)Parameters: 280BTraining tokens: 300BGiant brain, tiny reading listChinchilla (new strategy)Parameters: 70BTraining tokens: 1.4TSmaller brain, massive reading listChinchilla won. On every benchmark.A model 4x smaller beat the big one - because it saw 4.6x more data. The "smaller" model was actually better trained.This proved that data matters as much as size. Throwing parameters at a problem without enough training data is waste.Continue

title: Scaling Laws - Why Bigger Models Win
text: RuleChinchilla's key finding: for optimal training, use roughly 20 tokens per parameter.7B model7B x 20 = 140B tokensLLaMA 7B used ~1T (well above optimal - even better!)70B model70B x 20 = 1.4T tokensChinchilla hit exactly this ratio175B model175B x 20 = 3.5T tokensGPT-3 only used 300B tokens (10x too few!)The impact on the industry:After Chinchilla, labs stopped just making models bigger. LLaMA (Meta, 2023) proved a 7B model trained on 1T+ tokens per parameter could match much larger models. Today, training data quality and quantity matter as much as parameter count.

title: Parameters at Scale
text: What is a parameter?Every weight. Every bias. One number = one parameter.When people say "LLaMA-7B", they mean 7 billion parameters. 7,000,000,000 floating-point numbers.Continue

title: Parameters at Scale
text: up to real models:LLaMA-7B7 billion - Like a notebook of factsGPT-3175 billion - Like a libraryGPT-4 (estimated)~1.8 trillion - Like all of human knowledgeContinue

title: Parameters at Scale
text: the parameters live?Embedding matrix: ~20%Token → vectorAttention (Q, K, V, O): ~20%Query/Key/Value computationFFN layers: ~50%Feed-forward networksOutput projection: ~10%Vector → logits (token probabilities)Continue

title: Parameters at Scale
text: ical insight:More parameters ≠ smarter.Parameters are capacity. Like a student with a big brain but no education. Still needs:• Quality training data (not just volume)• Proper training time• Good optimizationYou can have 1 trillion parameters trained poorly (worse than 7B parameters trained well).

title: Batch Training - Why Not One Example at a Time?
text: Why batch training? Efficiency and stability.Imagine training on 1 trillion tokens. Do we update weights after every single token? No - that's wasteful and noisy.Instead, we group examples into batches, compute loss for all of them, then update once. This is faster and more stable.Continue

title: Batch Training - Why Not One Example at a Time?
text: s Batch training - a concrete exampleSay we have 3 training examples. Each produces a loss.Online (update after each example)• Ex1: loss=1.2 → compute gradients → update weights• Ex2: loss=0.8 → compute gradients → update weights• Ex3: loss=1.5 → compute gradients → update weightsUpdates: 3 weight updatesSpeed: Slow. GPU not fully utilized.Stability: Noisy. One bad example can derail training.Batch (update after all examples)• Ex1: loss=1.2• Ex2: loss=0.8• Ex3: loss=1.5• Avg loss = (1.2+0.8+1.5)/3 = 1.17 → compute gradients ONCE → update weightsUpdates: 1 weight updateSpeed: Fast. GPU fully parallelized.Stability: Smooth. Noise averages out.Continue

title: Batch Training - Why Not One Example at a Time?
text: averaging in batchesEach example computes its own gradient. With batching, we average them before updating.Ex1: gradient w.r.t weight = [0.05, -0.02, 0.08]Ex2: gradient w.r.t weight = [0.01, 0.03, -0.01]Ex3: gradient w.r.t weight = [0.02, 0.02, 0.04]Batch avg gradient = sum / 3 = [0.027, 0.010, 0.037]Update: weight -= lr * avg_gradient (usually lr = 0.0001 to 0.001)Continue

title: Batch Training - Why Not One Example at a Time?
text: h size affects trainingBatch size is a key hyperparameter. Different sizes have tradeoffs:Batch size = 1Speed: Very slowMemory: MinimalNoise: High (noisy gradient)Quality: Can get stuck(Research only)Batch size = 32Speed: SlowMemory: LowNoise: MediumQuality: Reasonable(Small GPUs)Batch size = 256Speed: FastMemory: MediumNoise: Low (smooth)Quality: Good(Standard)Batch size = 1024+Speed: Very fastMemory: HighNoise: Very lowQuality: Excellent(Large-scale training)

title: Batch Training - Why Not One Example at a Time?
text: Scaling & Modern Techniques: Batch Training - Why Not One Example at a Time?. This chapter covers batch training - why not one example at a time?.

title: Knowledge Distillation - Teacher to Student
text: The problem: big models are expensiveGPT-4 has 1.8 trillion parameters. Running it costs a fortune. It's slow.But your phone app needs instant replies. You can't run GPT-4 on a phone.Question: Can we make a small model that's almost as smart as the big one?Yes. The trick is called knowledge distillation - and it works like a teacher and student.Continue

title: Knowledge Distillation - Teacher to Student
text: teaches StudentInstead of training the small model from scratch (expensive!), let the big model teach it.TeacherGPT-41.8 trillion paramsSlow, expensiveExtremely smart→StudentGPT-4 Mini8 billion paramsFast, cheapLearns from teacherHow? Show both models the same questions. The student tries to copy the teacher's answers - not just the final answer, but how the teacher thinks.Continue

title: Knowledge Distillation - Teacher to Student
text: s "copy how the teacher thinks" mean?Question: "What is the capital of France?"A lazy teacher just says the answer:Just the answer: "Paris"That's it. Right or wrong. No nuance.A great teacher shares their full thinking - as confidence bars:Teacher's confidence on each option:Paris85%London8%Lyon5%Berlin2%This full set of confidences is called "soft probabilities" - and it's the key to distillation.Continue

title: Knowledge Distillation - Teacher to Student
text: probabilities are magicLook at the teacher's confidences again. The "wrong" answers tell a story:85%ParisCorrect answer8%LondonWrong - but it is a European capital5%LyonWrong - but it is a French city0%PizzaNot even a cityIf you only said "Paris = right, everything else = wrong" - the student would think London and Pizza are equally wrong.But the soft probabilities teach: "London is close (it's a capital). Lyon is related (it's French). Pizza is nonsense." The student absorbs how concepts relate - for free.The temperature trickTo get even softer probabilities, we divide the logits by a temperature T before softmax: softmax(z_i / T)T = 1 (normal)Paris: 85%London: 8%Lyon: 5%Berlin: 2%T = 3 (soft)Paris: 45%London: 20%Lyon: 18%Berlin: 17%Higher temperature = softer distribution = more information about the relationships between tokens. This is also the same "temperature" slider you see in ChatGPT settings - higher T = more creative (spread out), lower T = more focused (peaked).Continue

title: Knowledge Distillation - Teacher to Student
text: everywhereMost AI models you actually use daily are distilled students:GPT-4→GPT-4 Mini100× cheaper API callsClaude Opus→Claude HaikuFast enough for your phoneLLaMA 70B→LLaMA 7BRuns on a laptopThe big expensive model trains once. Then it teaches smaller, faster models. That's why you can chat with AI instantly on your phone - you're talking to a student who learned from a genius.

title: Knowledge Distillation - Teacher to Student
text: Scaling & Modern Techniques: Knowledge Distillation - Teacher to Student. This chapter covers knowledge distillation - teacher to student.

title: CLIP - Teaching AI to See & Read
text: A weird question to start withYou see a photo of a golden retriever catching a frisbee on a beach.Someone asks: "Which caption goes with this photo?"A) "A dog catching a frisbee on a beach"B) "A car parked in a garage"C) "A person cooking dinner"Obviously A. Your brain instantly matched the image to the right words.But how do you teach a computer to do this? Images are pixels. Text is words. They're completely different things. How do you even compare them?Continue

title: CLIP - Teaching AI to See & Read
text: : translate both into the same languageYou can't directly compare a photo to a sentence. But what if you could convert both into numbers?Photodog + beach→Image Encoder→[0.8, 0.3, -0.5, 0.9, ...]Caption"dog on beach"→Text Encoder→[0.79, 0.31, -0.48, 0.88, ...]Two separate translators (encoders) convert images and text into lists of numbers (vectors).If the image and text describe the same thing, their numbers end up almost identical. Notice how close the two rows of numbers are above!This system is called CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining), built by OpenAI.Continue

title: CLIP - Teaching AI to See & Read
text: CLIP learn? The matching gameCollect millions of image + caption pairs from the internet. Then play a matching game:Here are 4 images and 4 captions. Which goes with which?"dog on beach""red car""sunset""birthday cake"dog photoYESnononocar photonoYESnonosunset photononoYESnocake photonononoYESThe green diagonal = correct matches. Everything else = wrong.The model adjusts until matching pairs produce similar numbers and non-matching pairs produce very different numbers. This is called contrastive learning - learning by contrast (what matches vs what doesn't).Continue

title: CLIP - Teaching AI to See & Read
text: ilar" are two vectors?After encoding, we get two lists of numbers. We need a way to check: are they similar?The method is called cosine similarity. Here is the actual formula:cos(A, B) = (A · B) / (||A|| x ||B||)A · B = dot product (chapter 1.14)||A|| = magnitude of A = sqrt(sum of a_i squared)||B|| = magnitude of B = sqrt(sum of b_i squared)Dividing by the magnitudes removes the effect of vector length - only the DIRECTION matters. Result is always between -1 (opposite) and +1 (identical direction).Quick example: A = [0.8, 0.3] and B = [0.79, 0.31]A · B = (0.8 x 0.79) + (0.3 x 0.31) = 0.632 + 0.093 = 0.725||A|| = sqrt(0.64 + 0.09) = 0.854||B|| = sqrt(0.624 + 0.096) = 0.849cos(A, B) = 0.725 / (0.854 x 0.849) = 0.9998 - nearly identical!dog photo + "dog on beach"0.95Almost identical!dog photo + "animal playing"0.40Somewhat relateddog photo + "red sports car"0.05Completely differentDuring training, the model is rewarded for making correct pairs score HIGH and wrong pairs score LOW. Millions of image-caption pairs later, it becomes incredibly good at understanding what images and text mean.Continue

title: CLIP - Teaching AI to See & Read
text: changes everythingOnce images and text live in the same number space, you can do magical things:Google Image SearchYou type "fluffy cat sleeping" → encode text to numbers → find photos with the closest numbers. No one manually tagged those photos - CLIP just knows they match.DALL-E / MidjourneyYou type "astronaut riding a horse on Mars" → text becomes numbers → image generator creates pixels that produce those same numbers. The shared space is the bridge.ChatGPT / Claude understanding photosYou upload a photo to ChatGPT → image encoder converts it to numbers → those numbers go into the same space as text → now the model can "read" the image as if it were words.Every time AI "sees" an image, contrastive learning is behind it. It's the reason AI can understand photos, generate art, and search billions of images by typing words.

title: CLIP - Teaching AI to See & Read
text: Scaling & Modern Techniques: CLIP - Teaching AI to See & Read. This chapter covers clip - teaching ai to see & read.

title: The Complete Training Pipeline
text: Let's zoom out.You've now learned every stage of how an LLM is built - tokenization, pretraining, loss functions, SFT, RLHF, scaling laws, distillation, and CLIP.Here's the full journey from "pile of internet text" to "ChatGPT answering your questions."Continue

title: The Complete Training Pipeline
text: Pretraining2-3 months · $10-100 millionFeed the model trillions of words from the internet - books, Wikipedia, code, websites, forums, everything.The model learns by playing the prediction game millions of times:"The cat sat on the ___"Model predicts: "mat" (checks answer, adjusts weights, repeats)GainsKnowledge, language, reasoning, patternsLacksCan't answer questions - just autocompletesContinue

title: The Complete Training Pipeline
text: SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning)1-2 weeks · $1,000-10,000Humans write 10,000-100,000 perfect Q&A examples. The model trains on these to learn:Q: "How do I boil an egg?"A: "Put egg in water, boil, 6 min for soft, 12 for hard."GainsAnswers questions, follows instructions, right toneLacksMight still be rude, harmful, or confidently wrongContinue

title: The Complete Training Pipeline
text: RLHF (Human Feedback)1-2 weeks · $1,000-10,000Humans compare pairs of responses and pick the better one. A reward model learns their preferences, then the LLM optimizes for higher reward.Answer A: helpful, clear, safe → Human picks thisAnswer B: rude, misleading → RejectedResult: Helpful, harmless, honest assistantThis is what becomes ChatGPT, Claude, GeminiContinue

title: The Complete Training Pipeline
text: unch - Optional upgradesThe 3 phases above create the base model. But companies often add extra capabilities:Distillation - make it smaller & cheaperGPT-4 is brilliant but expensive. So the big model "teaches" a smaller one. The student learns 90% of the teacher's ability at 1/100th the cost. That's how GPT-4 Mini and Claude Haiku exist - fast, cheap, and still smart.CLIP - make it understand imagesThe base model only understands text. To make it "see" photos, you plug in a CLIP-style image encoder. The image gets converted into the same number space as text, so the model can "read" the photo. That's how ChatGPT and Claude understand screenshots, diagrams, and photos you upload.RAG - give it access to fresh infoThe model's knowledge is frozen at training time. It doesn't know today's news. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) lets the model search a database or the web BEFORE answering - like giving a student an open-book exam. That's how ChatGPT can browse the web and cite sources.Domain fine-tuning - make it an expertWant an AI that's amazing at medicine? Law? Finance? Take the general model and train it further on domain-specific data. A hospital might fine-tune on millions of medical records. The model keeps its general intelligence but becomes an expert in that field.Continue

title: The Complete Training Pipeline
text: & 3 - CompleteYou now understand the full lifecycle of an LLM:1Tokenization - how text becomes numbers2Pretraining - learn language by predicting next words3Cross-entropy loss - how the model measures its mistakes4SFT - teach it to answer questions properly5RLHF - teach it to be helpful, safe, and honest6Scaling laws - bigger model + more data = predictably better7Distillation - shrink the model without losing smarts8CLIP - connect images and text in one shared spaceNext up: the architecture that makes all of this possible - Transformers and Attention.

title: The Complete Training Pipeline
text: Scaling & Modern Techniques: The Complete Training Pipeline. This chapter covers the complete training pipeline.

title: CNN
text: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)Built for images. A small rectangular "filter" (like a magnifying glass) slides across the entire image, spotting patterns at each position.The key insight: local patterns matter. A corner of a cat's ear is detected the same way everywhere in the image.Continue

title: CNN
text: nvolution works: step by stepLet's convolve a 5×5 image with a 3×3 filter. The filter slides left-to-right, top-to-bottom, and at each position we multiply and sum.5×5 Image (pixel values)10210021011020101020110023×3 Filter0.1-0.20.3-0.10.5-0.20.2-0.30.1Convolution at position (0,0) - top-left cornerMultiply filter by the 3×3 corner, then sum:(1×0.1) + (0×-0.2) + (2×0.3) + (0×-0.1) + (2×0.5) + (1×-0.2) + (1×0.2) + (0×-0.3) + (2×0.1)= 0.1 + 0 + 0.6 + 0 + 1.0 - 0.2 + 0.2 + 0 + 0.2 = 1.9This single number (1.9) becomes the top-left pixel of the output "feature map".Filter slides to all 9 positions (5×5 - 3×3 + 1 = 3×3 output)1.9-0.40.2-0.91.4-0.61.1-0.71.7This 3×3 grid is the output "feature map". High values = strong pattern detected.Continue

title: CNN
text: s the filter detect?Different filters learn to detect different features:Filter 1:Vertical edges (left-dark, right-bright)Filter 2:Horizontal edges (top-dark, bottom-bright)Filter 3:Corners and cornersFilter 4+:Textures, shapes, then full objectsThe stack: Layer 1 finds edges → Layer 2 combines edges into shapes → Layer 3 combines shapes into parts (eyes, nose) → Layer 4 combines parts into objects (cat face).Continue

title: CNN
text: shrinking the feature mapAfter convolution, CNNs usually "pool" - taking the max or average over small regions to:1.Reduce computation (shrink image by 2x or 4x)2.Keep the strongest signals (max pooling)3.Make the network robust to small shifts (if pattern moves 1 pixel, we still detect it)Continue

title: CNN
text: tal Flaw: Local windowCNN filters are small (3×3, 5×5, at most 7×7). They only look at nearby pixels. This is perfect for images but breaks for language.Example sentence:"The cat that I saw yesterday in the park was sleeping.""cat" and "was" are grammatically linked (cat is sleeping), but they're 9 words apart. A 3×3 filter on tokens cannot see from word 1 to word 10. A 5×5 filter struggles too.You'd need a huge filter (17×1 to see all 17 words at once), and even then, the filter would need to learn every possible relative position of "subject" and "verb" separately. That's wasteful.Conclusion: CNNs work great for images. They don't work for language.Continue

title: CNN
text: need for language:A network where every word can directly look at every other word, no matter how far apart. And it should work the same way whether words are 1 position or 100 positions apart.

title: CNN
text: The Road to Transformers: CNN. This chapter covers cnn.

title: RNN
text: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)Built for sequences. Processes words one at a time, left-to-right. At each step, it maintains a hidden state (memory) that carries information forward.ht = tanh( Wh · ht-1 + Wx · xt + b )New hidden state = function of (previous hidden state + current word embedding)Continue

title: RNN
text: "The cat sat" - one word at a timeLet's process with hidden state dimension = 3 (real models: 256, 512, 1024+)Step 1: "The"Word embedding x_t:[0.2, 0.1, -0.4]Prev hidden h_{t-1}:[0, 0, 0]h_1 = tanh(W_h·[0,0,0] + W_x·[0.2,0.1,-0.4] + b)→ h_1 = [0.3, -0.1, 0.2]Step 2: "cat"Word embedding x_t:[-0.1, 0.5, 0.3]Prev hidden h_{t-1}:[0.3, -0.1, 0.2]h_2 = tanh(W_h·[0.3,-0.1,0.2] + W_x·[-0.1,0.5,0.3] + b)→ h_2 = [0.1, 0.4, -0.3]Step 3: "sat"Word embedding x_t:[0.4, -0.2, 0.1]Prev hidden h_{t-1}:[0.1, 0.4, -0.3]h_3 = tanh(W_h·[0.1,0.4,-0.3] + W_x·[0.4,-0.2,0.1] + b)→ h_3 = [0.5, 0.2, 0.0]Notice: the hidden state changes as we process each word. By step 3, it has incorporated meaning from all three words. Information flows forward in time.Continue

title: RNN
text: ng order matters: "Dog bites man" vs "Man bites dog"The same three word embeddings, but in different order, produce different hidden states. The network learns which word is the subject (first) and which is the object (last).Dog bites manFinal hidden state:[0.8, 0.1, -0.2]Semantically: dog is actorMan bites dogFinal hidden state:[-0.2, 0.7, 0.1]Semantically: man is actorContinue

title: RNN
text: derstands order!This solves the CNN problem. Now every word can indirectly influence every other word through the hidden state."The cat that I saw yesterday was sleeping." - as we read from left to right, "cat" shapes the hidden state, which then processes all the intermediate words, and influences the final prediction that it "was sleeping".Continue

title: RNN
text: algorithm: strict sequential processingProcess word 1 → get h_1 → process word 2 (using h_1) → get h_2 → process word 3 (using h_2) → ...Cannot parallelize. Word 2's computation depends on word 1's output. Word 3 depends on word 2. No amount of GPU cores helps.On a GPU with 1000 cores: only 1 core is actually computing at a time. The other 999 sit idle. This is a massive waste.

title: RNN
text: The Road to Transformers: RNN. This chapter covers rnn.

title: RNN's Fatal Flaws
text: Flaw #1: Vanishing Gradient (Information Loss)The hidden state is like a game of telephone. Information gets corrupted with each pass.Example: "The cat that I saw yesterday was sleeping."When training, the gradient (error signal) flows backward through time. At step 7 ("was"), the gradient tries to adjust weights to make the model understand that "cat" is the subject. But it has to flow back through 6 steps of gradient multiplication.was1.00yesterday0.80saw0.64I0.51that0.41cat0.33Result: The weights that should learn "cat → subject" barely get updated. The model struggles to remember long-distance dependencies.Continue

title: RNN's Fatal Flaws
text: : why gradients vanishIn RNN backpropagation, the chain rule multiplies gradients through every time step:∂L/∂Wh = (∂L/∂ht) × (∂ht/∂ht-1) × ...Each ∂ht/∂ht-1 term is often less than 1.0 (typical range: 0.1 to 0.9). Multiply seven of them together:0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 = 0.21The gradient is now 79% lost before it reaches the early weights.Continue

title: RNN's Fatal Flaws
text: It's SLOW (sequential processing)A modern GPU has 1000+ cores. If you're processing a sequence one word at a time, you're using 1 core and leaving 999 idle.RNNTime: 10 secondsGPU: 1 / 1000One word per step. Sequential.ParallelTime: 0.01 secondsGPU: 1000 / 1000All words at once.For a 1000-word document, RNN processes it 1000× sequentially. A parallel system could process all 1000 at once (with attention). That's potentially 1000× speedup.Continue

title: RNN's Fatal Flaws
text: solution: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)LSTM added "gates" to the hidden state - forget gate, input gate, output gate. These gates let the model decide what to remember and what to forget.Result: vanishing gradient is reduced (not eliminated). You can now model sequences up to ~100-200 words reliably instead of ~10-20.But: LSTM still processes sequentially. Still slow. Still has vanishing gradient for very long sequences (500+ words).Continue

title: RNN's Fatal Flaws
text: need: no sequence dependencyWe need a model where:1.Every word can directly look at every other word (no information loss through hidden states)2.All words are processed in parallel (use all GPU cores)3.Distance between words doesn't matter (word 1 and word 100 communicate equally well)

title: Positional Encoding - The Heatmap
text: The famous heatmap: all positions, all dimensions, at onceSo far we have seen bits and pieces: a formula, a few frequencies, one position vector. Now let's look at the entire positional-encoding matrix at once. Every row is one position's encoding, every column is one dimension across all positions, color shows the sin or cos value at that cell.Full positional-encoding heatmap with rows as positions (0 to 39) and columns as embedding dimensions (0 to 127). Each cell is colored by the sin or cos value at that position and dimension, ranging from dark red at negative one to white at zero to dark blue at positive one. The left side shows a fast checkerboard pattern while the right side shows slow vertical stripes.010203039Position (row)0326496127Dimension i (column)+10-1d_model = 128. Positions 0 to 39 shown. Red = -1, white = 0, blue = +1.Two things jump out: the left side is a wild checkerboard that changes every step, and the right side is calm vertical stripes that barely change with position. Why?Continue

title: Positional Encoding - The Heatmap
text: left side flips fastEach column uses its own frequency. The formula:f_i = 1 / 10000^(2 · floor(i/2) / d)For small i (left side of the heatmap), the exponent is near 0, so f_i is close to 1.0. Each step in position moves the angle by about 1 radian (roughly 57 degrees). sin and cos swing wildly.Column i = 0 (f = 1.0): sin(pos · 1.0)pos01234567sin0.000.840.910.14-0.76-0.96-0.280.66Look at those values jumping around: 0.00, 0.84, 0.91, 0.14, -0.76, -0.96, -0.28, 0.66. Every position is a very different number. That's the checkerboard on the left of the heatmap.Continue

title: Positional Encoding - The Heatmap
text: right side barely changesFor large i (right side of the heatmap), the exponent approaches 1, so f_i approaches 1 / 10000 = 0.0001. Across 40 positions the angle only moves by 40 · 0.0001 = 0.004 radians (about 0.23 degrees). sin and cos barely move.Column i = 127 (f ≈ 1/10000): cos(pos · 0.0001)pos05101520253039cos1.00001.00001.00001.00001.00001.00001.00001.0000All values stay near 1.0000. Every position looks nearly identical here. That's the solid blue stripe on the far right of the heatmap.So what are these slow dimensions for? They encode very long-range position information. Two tokens 1000 positions apart differ meaningfully in the slow columns, even if they look identical across 40 positions.Continue

title: Positional Encoding - The Heatmap
text: sition is a unique fingerprintPick any single row. That row is exactly the positional encoding vector for that position. It's 128 numbers, a unique barcode. Compare rows side by side:pos 5pos 6pos 25Position 5 and position 6 are neighbors. Their left-side cells differ strongly (fast dims), but their right-side cells look identical (slow dims barely moved). Position 25 looks very different from both on the left, but still nearly identical on the far right.This is exactly the property we want: each position gets a unique vector, AND nearby positions have similar vectors. The model can learn both "which position is this" and "how far apart are these two positions."Continue

title: Positional Encoding - The Heatmap
text: ounting: the same idea, one bit at a timeYou have seen this pattern before. Count in binary. Every integer gets a unique bit pattern, and the bits flip at different speeds: the rightmost bit flips every step, the next one flips every 2 steps, the next every 4, and so on.Binary counting table showing positions 0 through 15 with 4-bit binary representations. Each bit is colored: bit 0 (rightmost, fast) flips every step, bit 1 flips every 2 steps, bit 2 flips every 4 steps, bit 3 (leftmost, slow) flips every 8 steps. Each position row gets a unique bit pattern, analogous to how each position in the heatmap gets a unique sin/cos signature.bit 3 (slow)bit 2bit 1bit 0 (fast)00000100012001030011401005010160110701118100091001101010111011121100131101141110151111The mapping is direct:Binary encodingBit 0 (rightmost, fast) flips 1, 0, 1, 0, ...Bit 3 (leftmost, slow) flips every 8 stepsValues: 0 or 1 (discrete)Each number = unique bit patternSin/cos encodingDim 0 (fast, f = 1.0) oscillates every stepDim 127 (slow, f = 0.0001) barely movesValues: any number in [-1, +1] (smooth)Each position = unique sin/cos signatureSin/cos positional encoding is essentially binary counting made smooth and continuous. The fast dimensions play the role of low-order bits, the slow dimensions play the role of high-order bits. The magic of using sin/cos (instead of 0/1) is that the resulting vectors have nice geometric relationships: dot products capture relative distance, values stay bounded, and the encoding is differentiable so gradients flow through it cleanly during training.

title: RoPE - Rotary Position Embeddings
text: The problem with sinusoidal position encodingRecall chapters 5.3-5.7: we compute a position vector using sin/cos and ADD it to each embedding. This gives every token a unique position signal.But there are two fundamental problems:Problem 1: Absolute positioningPosition 5 always gets the same encoding vector, regardless of context. But "cats" at position 5 in a 10-word sentence and "cats" at position 5 in a 1000-word sentence should feel different - the relative context is different.Problem 2: Fixed lengthIf we train on sequences up to 2048 tokens, what happens at position 2049? The model has never seen that position encoding. Performance degrades badly - the model cannot extrapolate to longer contexts.RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) solves both problems with a single elegant idea.Continue

title: RoPE - Rotary Position Embeddings
text: each vector as a clock handPicture every word's Query (and Key) vector as a clock hand. RoPE doesn't add anything to it - it simply turns the hand. How far? An angle equal to position · frequency. Position 1 is a small turn, position 10 is a bigger turn, position 100 is a huge turn.No new numbers are bolted on. No dimensions are inflated. The hand just points somewhere else.See it turn: Q = [0.8, 0.6], f = 0.3 rad per stepSame vector, 4 different positions. Watch the hand sweep counter-clockwise.Clock-face diagram showing a 2D query vector Q = [0.8, 0.6] rotated by position times frequency. Four labeled arrows spread counter-clockwise across the upper quadrant of a dashed unit circle, representing the same vector at positions 0, 1, 2, and 3.xypos 0angle 0pos 10.3 radpos 20.6 radpos 30.9 radposangle (rad)rotated Q00.0[0.800, 0.600]10.3[0.587, 0.810]20.6[0.321, 0.947]30.9[0.027, 1.000]Key property: turning preserves lengthAll four rotated vectors still have the same length (1.0 in this example). Turning only changes direction, not length. Sinusoidal PE, by contrast, adds a position vector that changes both direction and length.For the curious: the 2D rotation formulaR(t) · [q1, q2] = [q1 cos(t) - q2 sin(t), q1 sin(t) + q2 cos(t)]where t = position · frequencyContinue

title: RoPE - Rotary Position Embeddings
text: c: only the gap between Q and K mattersThe attention score between two words is just the dot product of their clock hands. And the dot product only cares about one thing: the angle between the two hands. Same angle = same score, no matter where the hands are pointing absolutely.Since RoPE spins both Q and K by their own positions, the common spin cancels out. Only the gap (position difference) is left. Watch two very different scenarios land on the same answer:Two side-by-side clock faces comparing near versus far scenarios in RoPE. The left clock shows Query at position 3 and Key at position 0; the right clock shows Query at position 10 and Key at position 7. In both cases the angular gap between the Q and K hands is the same 0.9 radians, illustrating that RoPE gives the same attention score whenever the relative distance is the same, regardless of absolute positions.Near: Q at pos 3, K at pos 0Far: Q at pos 10, K at pos 7Q (pos 3)K (pos 0)gapQ spun 0.9 rad · K spun 0 radgap between Q and K: 0.9 radQ (pos 10)K (pos 7)gapQ spun 3.0 rad · K spun 2.1 radgap between Q and K: 0.9 radNear (pos 3, pos 0)gap = 0.9 rad (same for both)score = cos(0.9) ≈ 0.62Far (pos 10, pos 7)gap = 0.9 rad (same for both)score = cos(0.9) ≈ 0.62Why the common spin cancels (one-line intuition)Imagine both Q and K are glued to the same spinning turntable. Whether the turntable is at position 0 or at position 97, the relative angle between Q and K on the table never changes. The dot product only sees that relative angle, so the score stays the same. The absolute rotation is invisible to attention.The model gets relative position for free, with zero extra parameters.For the curious: the actual algebraQ at position m is rotated by angle m·f. K at position n is rotated by n·f. Their dot product becomes:(R(m·f) Q) · (R(n·f) K) = Q · R((n - m)·f) KRotation matrices are orthogonal, so R(a)ᵀ R(b) = R(b - a). Only the gap (n - m) survives. For unit vectors at angular gap Δ, the dot product is exactly cos(Δ).Continue

title: RoPE - Rotary Position Embeddings
text: orks in practiceReal embeddings have 128 dims (or more), not 2. RoPE handles this by processing dimensions in pairs, and each pair rotates at its own frequency. Fast frequencies for early pairs, slow ones for late pairs:Three mini clock faces side by side showing pair 0 rotating fast, pair 10 rotating medium, and pair 60 rotating slow. Each face has a vector at position 0 and position 10 to illustrate that early pairs rotate through large angles while late pairs barely move across the same 10 positions.pos 0Pair 0fastafter 10 steps: 2.5 rad (143 deg)pos 0Pair 10mediumafter 10 steps: 0.5 rad (29 deg)pos 0Pair 60slowafter 10 steps: 0.05 rad (3 deg)Each mini clock is one (x, y) pair out of 64. The white arrow is the pair at position 0; the colored arrow is the same pair at position 10. Fast pairs rotate far; slow pairs barely move.128-dim vector split into 64 pairs(d0, d1)fast(d2, d3)fast(d4, d5)medium...(d124, d125)slow(d126, d127)very slowEach pair gets its own 2D rotation. The key: each pair rotates at a different frequency, just like sinusoidal PE's fast and slow dimensions.Early pairs (d0, d1): rotate FASTHigh frequency - sensitive to nearby position differences. "Is the next word 1 or 2 positions away?"Late pairs (d126, d127): rotate SLOWLow frequency - sensitive to distant position differences. "Is this word 500 or 1000 positions away?"The frequency formula is the same idea as sinusoidal PE: f_i = 1 / 10000^(2i/d), where i is the pair index and d is the total dimension. Pair 0 rotates fastest, pair 63 rotates slowest.Continue

title: RoPE - Rotary Position Embeddings
text: wonSinusoidalLearned PERoPETypeAbsoluteAbsoluteRelativeApplied toEmbedding (add)Embedding (add)Q and K only (rotate)Max lengthFixed at trainingFixed at trainingCan extrapolateExtrapolationFails beyond maxFails beyond maxWorks with scaling tricksParameters0 (fixed formula)pos x dim0 (fixed formula)RoPE extrapolation: NTK-aware scalingIf a model trained on 4096 tokens needs to handle 16384, you can scale the rotation frequencies by a factor. The "NTK-aware" variant adjusts different frequency bands differently - slowing down the fast-rotating pairs while keeping the slow ones nearly unchanged. This extends context length 4x or more with minimal quality loss.Models using RoPELLaMALLaMA 2LLaMA 3MistralQwenGPT-NeoXPaLMNearly every modern open-source LLM uses RoPE. text: Words alone are meaningless. Context is everything.After embedding, every instance of the same word gets the exact same vector. But the same word can mean completely different things depending on surrounding words.Continue

title: The Problem - Context is Everything
text: 't just about "bank" - it's about every word.The model has a big dictionary (learned during training) that maps every word to a fixed list of numbers. Take "love": after embedding, "love" always gets the same numbers, no matter what sentence it's in. But "love" in "I love cats" is different from "love" in "love is blind."These numbers capture the word's meaning in isolation. It has no idea what words are around it. That's a problem - because we need context.The goal: take "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1] and transform it into a NEW list of numbers that represents "love in the context of I and cats."Continue

title: The Problem - Context is Everything
text: what Attention solves.It lets each word look around at the other words and ask "which other words are relevant to me?" and then absorb context - absorbing information from the relevant ones. After attention: "love" in "I love cats" absorbs info from "I" and "cats" → now represents "affection from me toward cats". Same input vector, different output vector depending on context.

title: The Dot Product - Measuring Similarity
text: Here's the problem.We have three lists of numbers (one per word). We need some mathematical operation that computes "how relevant is word A to word B?"The tool is the dot product - multiply pairs, add them up. Big result = related. Small result = unrelated.Continue

text: o vectors, multiply pairs and sum:A = [1, 2, 3]B = [4, 5, 6]Multiply each pair:1 × 442 × 5103 × 618Sum them up:4 + 10 + 18 = 32That's it. Multiply pairs, add them up, get one number.Continue

title: The Dot Product - Measuring Similarity
text: s this number mean?It measures how similar two vectors are in direction. Think of you and a friend both pointing somewhere:→ →Both point same way → Large positive"These are similar!"→ ←Opposite directions → Large negative"These are opposites!"→ ↑Perpendicular (unrelated) → Close to zero"Nothing in common"Continue

title: Why Not Dot Product Embeddings Directly?
text: We can't just dot product the raw word numbers.In "I love cats": "I" = pronoun, "love" = verb, "cats" = noun. Their raw meanings aren't similar at all - the dot product of "I" and "love" would be low."I" = pronoun, self-reference"love" = verb, emotionBut they are related - "I" is the one who loves! title: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: So far, Q, K, V are just concepts in our head.We know each word needs:🔍 Query - "what am I looking for?"🏷️ Key - "what do I offer?"📦 Value - "what info do I carry?"But each word starts as just one list of numbers - its embedding. For example:"love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]That's one list. We need three different lists from it (Query, Key, Value). How do you get three different things from one thing?You transform it three different ways. And the tool for each transformation is: a grid of numbers.Continue

title: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: this "grid of numbers"?It's called a W matrix (W = "weights"). But don't let the name scare you. It's just a table of numbers. Like a spreadsheet. Rows and columns filled with numbers:Example: a W grid might look like this (tiny version):0.02-0.150.310.080.410.07-0.22-0.19-0.090.330.140.270.18-0.050.420.11That's it. Just numbers in a grid. When you multiply a word's embedding by this grid, you get a new, smaller list of numbers - the Query (or Key, or Value, depending on which grid).Multiply input by a table → get an output. Nothing more complicated than that.Continue

title: How Are Q, K, V Created?
text: it like a photo with three filters.You have a photo of a person. You want three different views:View 1:highlight only the eyes→ captures where the person is lookingView 2:highlight only the nose→ captures the nose shapeView 3:highlight only the mouth→ captures the expressionHow do you create these views? You apply three different filters. Each filter is a grid of numbers that, when multiplied with the photo, produces a different view.That's EXACTLY what W_Q, W_K, W_V are. They're three grids of numbers (filters).Word embedding: [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] ← the "photo"Grid 1 (W_Q):a 4×2 grid of numbers← filter for "what am I looking for?"Grid 2 (W_K):a 4×2 grid of numbers← filter for "what do I offer?"Grid 3 (W_V):a 4×2 grid of numbers← filter for "what info do I carry?"When you multiply the embedding by Grid 1, you get the Query - a 2-number list representing "what this word is looking for."When you multiply the same embedding by Grid 2, you get the Key - a 2-number list representing "what this word can be found for."When you multiply the same embedding by Grid 3, you get the Value - a 2-number list representing "what actual info this word carries."The grids are just tables of numbers. Nothing magical. Multiply input by a table → get an output.Continue

title: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: The puzzle: why is W_Q shaped 4×2, not 4×4?In the last chapter we saw W_Q written as a 4×2 grid. That might feel odd. If the embedding has 4 numbers, shouldn't the query also have 4 numbers? Shouldn't W_Q be 4×4?Short answer: no. Q (and K and V) are smaller than the embedding. W_Q's job is to project the 4-number embedding down to a 2-number query. The input and output sizes do not have to match.embedding (4 numbers)1.00.5-.30.8×W_Q (4×2)=query (2 numbers)0.30.74 numbers in, 2 numbers out. The matrix shape tells you exactly that: 4 rows = input size, 2 columns = output size.Continue

title: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: dding stays big. Q, K, V are small views.Think of the embedding as the word's full profile - it stays 4 numbers long for the entire journey through the Transformer. Q, K, V are three compressed lenses on that profile, built only for the attention step.embedding (always 4 numbers)1.00.5-.30.8Three arrows fanning out from the embedding down to Q, K, and V. Each arrow is labeled with its projection matrix, illustrating three parallel compressed views of the same embedding.× W_Q× W_K× W_VQuery (2)0.30.7Key (2)0.90.4Value (2)0.6-.2Three different W matrices, three different 2-number views. The 4-number embedding itself is untouched; it continues through the rest of the network in its full original size.Continue

title: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: ler? Multi-head attention splits the budgetHere is the real reason for the smaller shape. The Transformer runs several heads in parallel on the same embedding. Each head gets its own set of W_Q, W_K, W_V and works independently, looking at the embedding through its own 2-number lens.one embedding, 4 numbers1.00.5-.30.8Two arrows fanning out from the embedding to two different heads, each with its own W_Q matrix.× W_Q₁× W_Q₂Head 1: Q₁0.30.7Head 2: Q₂-.40.2Two heads, each with its own W_Q (4×2). Each produces a different 2-number Query because W_Q₁ and W_Q₂ learned different things. After both heads do their attention, their outputs are concatenated back to 4 numbers total (2 + 2) and fed forward.The budget intuitionFixed budget: 4 output numbers per token. Options: one big head with a 4-number Q, or two small heads each with a 2-number Q. Multi-head picks the second: more heads, each specialized. d_k = d_model / num_heads.Continue

title: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: nsformer numbers (GPT-style)Scale everything up. The chapter's 4 becomes 512. The chapter's 2 becomes 64. The chapter's 2 heads become 8.This chapter (teaching)d_model = 4 (embedding size)num_heads = 2d_k = 4 / 2 = 2 (Q/K/V per head)W_Q per head = 4 × 2Real GPT (typical)d_model = 512 (embedding size)num_heads = 8d_k = 512 / 8 = 64 (Q/K/V per head)W_Q per head = 512 × 648 heads × 64 numbers each = 512 numbers total, which fits exactly back into the embedding size. The budget is preserved across heads.Continue

title: Shapes - Why Q is Smaller Than the Embedding
text: ecapEmbeddingd_model (4 here, 512 in real)lives through the whole TransformerQ, K, V (per head)d_k (2 here, 64 in real)temporary, only inside attentionW_Q, W_K, W_V (per head)d_model × d_k (4×2 here, 512×64 in real)projects embedding → Q / K / VMulti-head concat + W_Oback to d_model (4 here, 512 in real)returns to the main residual streamBottom line: W_Q is not square. It's a rectangular projection from embedding space into a smaller query space. The embedding keeps its original size; the query is a compressed view used only for the attention computation.

title: W Matrices - Learned During Training
text: Where did those numbers in the grids come from?Last chapter, we saw three grids (W_Q, W_K, W_V) - each a table of numbers. Multiply an embedding by a grid, get Q or K or V.But who decided what numbers go in those grids?🔍W_Q?????????🏷️W_K?????????📦W_V?????????Answer: nobody decided. The numbers were learned during training.Continue

title: W Matrices - Learned During Training
text: tart with random garbageBefore training begins, every grid is filled with random numbers. Nobody thought about these. A random number generator just spit them out:W_Q (random)+0.47-0.82+0.15-0.63+0.91-0.24+0.38-0.71+0.56-0.09+0.77-0.45-0.33+0.62-0.88+0.19W_K (random)-0.24+0.38-0.71+0.56-0.09+0.77-0.45-0.33+0.62-0.88+0.19+0.47-0.82+0.15-0.63+0.91W_V (random)-0.45-0.33+0.62-0.88+0.19+0.47-0.82+0.15-0.63+0.91-0.24+0.38-0.71+0.56-0.09+0.77With random grids, the model computes:random W_Q x embedding = garbage Queryrandom W_K x embedding = garbage Keyrandom W_V x embedding = garbage ValueGarbage Q, K, V → garbage attention → garbage predictionsThe model is terrible at this point. And that's completely fine.Continue

title: W Matrices - Learned During Training
text: ning loop: predict, fail, nudgeThe model reads millions of sentences and tries to predict the next word each time. Here's what happens on every single attempt:📖Model sees: "The cat sat on the"⚙️Uses its current (bad) grids to compute attention❌Predicts next word: "elephant" (wrong! should be "mat")📏Calculates error: how wrong was that prediction?⬅️Error flows backward through every grid number🔧Each number gets nudged slightly to reduce the errorThis loop repeats billions of times. Every repetition nudges the numbers a tiny bit closer to useful values.Continue

title: W Matrices - Learned During Training
text: what does one "nudge" look like?Let's look at a single number in W_Q - say row 3, column 2:W_Q grid (one number highlighted)+0.47-0.82+0.15-0.63+0.91-0.24+0.38-0.71+0.56-0.09+0.77-0.45-0.33+0.62-0.88+0.19-0.09→-0.07The error signal says: "If this number were slightly larger, the Query for 'cat' would have paid more attention to 'sat' - and the prediction would have been a little better."That's it. Just +0.02. A tiny nudge. But multiply that by every number in every grid, across billions of training examples, and the grids slowly transform from random noise into something meaningful.Continue

title: W Matrices - Learned During Training
text: llions of examples: the grids have settledAfter training, the random numbers have been nudged millions of times. They've settled into values that actually mean something:BEFORE (random)AFTER (trained)W_QW_Q+0.47-0.82+0.15-0.63+0.91-0.24+0.38-0.71+0.56-0.09+0.77-0.45-0.33+0.62-0.88+0.19→+0.02-0.15+0.31+0.08+0.41+0.07-0.22-0.19-0.09+0.33+0.14+0.27+0.18-0.05+0.42+0.11W_Qnow produces Queries that correctly capture "what am I looking for?"W_Know produces Keys that correctly capture "what do I offer?"W_Vnow produces Values that correctly capture "what info do I carry?"Continue

title: W Matrices - Learned During Training
text: insightNobody programmed these numbers.No human sat down and said "row 3, column 2 should be 0.14." The numbers emerged from the nudging process (backpropagation) over billions of examples.This is what "learned" means in machine learning. The model figured out, on its own, what numbers to put in each grid so that the resulting Q, K, V vectors produce useful attention patterns.Random noise in → billions of nudges → meaningful grids out.

title: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit
text: s "transpose" mean?It means: rows become columns, columns become rows. That's it. No math changes - just a flip.Original K0.30.70.60.40.80.53 rows × 2 colsrow 1 = KIrow 2 = Kloverow 3 = Kcats→transposeKT0.30.60.80.70.40.52 rows × 3 colscol 1 = KIcol 2 = Klovecol 3 = KcatsSame numbers. title: Why K Transpose? - Making the Shapes Fit

That's the "relevance score".K_IK_loveK_catsQ_I0.380.560.74Q_love0.660.420.53Q_cats0.570.540.70Row = one word asking. Column = one word answering.
