title: Why Do We Need Softmax?
text: it like a budget.You have ₹100 to distribute among three friends based on how helpful they were.Helpfulness scores: Riya=38, Aman=56, Priya=74❌ Can't give ₹38 + ₹56 + ₹74 = ₹168 total. You only have ₹100.You need to convert scores into shares of ₹100 - percentages that add up to 100%.Total = 38 + 56 + 74 = 168Riya38/168=23%→ ₹23Aman56/168=33%→ ₹33Priya74/168=44%→ ₹44✅ Total: ₹100. This works!That's basically what softmax does - convert raw scores into shares that add up to 100%. But with one extra trick...Continue

title: Why Do We Need Softmax?
text: lem: dot products CAN be negative.Our example had positive scores. But what if they were:word_A-3word_B1word_C5Simple division: total = -3 + 1 + 5 = 3A:-3/3 = -100%← NEGATIVE? Can't pay minus attention!B:1/3 = 33%C:5/3 = 167%← More than 100%? Nonsense!Simple division is broken. We need something better.Continue

title: Why Do We Need Softmax?
text: tion: SoftmaxBefore dividing, put each score through e^score (e ≈ 2.718). This magical function makes ANY number positive.The Softmax FormulaSoftmax formula showing e to the power of each score divided by sum of all e scores, converting raw attention scores to probabilitiesσ(z)i=eziΣj=1Kezj← this word's e^score← sum of all e^scoresIn plain English: take e to the power of this score, then divide by the sum of e to the power of every score.Continue

title: Why Do We Need Softmax?
text: It's ALWAYS positive.No matter what you feed it - negative, zero, huge - the output is always a positive number.-3→e^(-3) =0.05negative → small positive0→e^(0) =1zero → exactly 11→e^(1) =2.72positive → bigger positive5→e^(5) =148.4large → much largerNotice the pattern: higher input → exponentially bigger output. This means softmax naturally amplifies the winner.Continue

title: Why Do We Need Softmax?
text: kthrough: scores [-3, 1, 5]Step 1: Apply e^score to each-3↓ e^(-3)0.051↓ e^(1)2.725↓ e^(5)148.4All negative numbers became positive!Step 2: Sum them all up (the denominator)0.05 + 2.72 + 148.4 = 151.17Continue

title: Why Do We Need Softmax?
text: kthrough: scores [-3, 1, 5]Step 3: Divide each e^score by the sumA (-3)0.05/151.17=0.03%B (1)2.72/151.17=1.8%C (5)148.4/151.17=98.2%All positive ✓Sum = 100% ✓Ranking preserved ✓Continue

title: Why Do We Need Softmax?
text: amplifies differences - by design.Raw scores-3, 1, 5Gap: 1 to 5 = just 4→After e^x0.05, 2.72, 148.4Gap: 2.72 to 148 = 145!The highest score doesn't just win - it dominates. This is good: the most relevant word SHOULD stand out.But - if scores are too huge (like 12, 18, 25), softmax amplifies TOO aggressively → 99.99% on one word. That's why we scale first (next chapter) to keep scores in a useful range.Continue

title: Why Do We Need Softmax?

title: Step 3 - Scale by √d_k
text: The problem: dot products grow with dimensions.Remember, the dot product = multiply each pair, then sum all of them. More dimensions means more pairs being added up:2 dimensions → sum 2 terms0.24+0.14=0.3864 dimensions → sum 64 termsterm₁+term₂+term₃+... + term₆₄=~2564 small numbers added up → much bigger total512 dimensions → sum 512 termsterm₁+term₂+term₃+... + term₅₁₂=~180512 small numbers added up → huge totalEach individual term might be small (like 0.3 or 0.5). But when you add up 64 or 512 of them, the total becomes very large. The score grows just because of more dimensions - not because the words are more related.Continue

title: Step 3 - Scale by √d_k
text: big scores a problem?Remember: softmax uses e^score. When scores are big, e^big_number becomes astronomically huge, and one word gets 99.99% of the attention - the model becomes blind to everything else.Small scores (2-dim)[0.38, 0.56, 0.74]I28%love33%cats39%✅ Looks at all words. Can blend info from multiple sources.Huge scores (64-dim)[12.1, 17.8, 25.6]I0.1%love0.4%cats99.6%❌ 99.6% on one word. Others invisible. Can't blend info.Continue

title: Step 3 - Scale by √d_k
text: divide by √d_kIf d_k = 64, then √64 = 8. Dividing brings scores back to a manageable range:Before scaling: [12.1, 17.8, 25.6]Divide by √64 = 8:After scaling: [1.51, 2.23, 3.20]Softmax of [1.51, 2.23, 3.20]:I14%love28%cats58%✅ Focuses on the most relevant word (58%) but still gathers info from others (14%, 28%). That's exactly what you want.Continue

title: Step 3 - Scale by √d_k
text: specifically? Why not ÷2 or ÷100?There's a mathematical reason. When Q and K have random values, the dot product's variance grows proportionally to d_k. The standard deviation = √d_k. So dividing by √d_k brings the variance back to 1 - normalizing scores to a consistent range no matter the dimension.In simple terms: √d_k is the exact right amount to undo the growth caused by summing d_k terms. Not too much (all scores become equal), not too little (problem remains).Continue

title: Why Multi-Head? - The Compromise Problem
text: Single-head attention has a problem.Our "I love cats" example has only 3 words with 2 main relationships (who loves? what's loved?). To really see why one head isn't enough, we need a longer sentence with more relationships.Consider: "The cat that I adopted last week sat on the mat.""sat" needs answers to MULTIPLE questions at once:Who sat?catsubject ↔ verbSat where?matverb ↔ locationSat when?last weekverb ↔ timeContinue

title: Why Multi-Head? - The Compromise Problem
text: head produces one set of weights - forced to compromise:cat40%got somemat30%got somelast week5%almost nothing!others25%scatteredTemporal info ("last week") got only 5% - almost lost! One head can't focus on all relationships.Continue

title: Why Multi-Head? - The Compromise Problem
text: : give it MULTIPLE heads - like multiple ears.Each head has its own W_Q, W_K, W_V, so each asks a different question:Head 1subject-verbsat asks: "who is my subject?"→ cat (70%)Head 2verb-locationsat asks: "where am I happening?"→ mat (65%)Head 3temporalsat asks: "when am I happening?"→ week (55%)Now NO info is lost. Each head specializes in one relationship and captures it fully.

title: The Split - How 8 Heads Work
text: One head = one Query arrow = one direction.Each word's 512-dim embedding produces ONE Query vector through W_Q. That single arrow can only point one way, so it can only ask one type of question.512-dim embedding → W_Q (512×512) → One 512-dim QueryOne arrow. One question. Information lost.Solution: don't make one big arrow. Make 8 smaller arrows, each pointing in its own direction.Continue

title: The Split - How 8 Heads Work
text: e 512 dimensions into 8 chunks of 64.Imagine the embedding as a long bar. We slice it into 8 equal pieces:512 dimensionsH1H2H3H4H5H6H7H86464646464646464Total dimensions:512Number of heads:8Dims per head:512 / 8 = 64We don't run the full 512-dim attention 8 times. We split it - same total computation, but 8 independent perspectives.Continue

title: The Split - How 8 Heads Work
text: - doesn't splitting lose precision?Why not give each head the full 512 dims? Let's compare:Option A: 8 heads × 512 dims each512512512512512512512512Cost: 8 × 512 = 4,096 dims total (8x the compute!)Quality per head: very high, but diminishing returns past ~64 dimsOption B: 8 heads × 64 dims each (the split)6464646464646464Cost: 8 × 64 = 512 dims total (same budget as one head!)Quality per head: 64 dims is plenty - "is this the subject?" doesn't need 512 numbers to answerThe insight: more dims per head = diminishing returns.Going from 64 to 512 dims per head barely improves quality, but costs 8x more. The split gives you 8 different perspectives for the price of 1 large one.Continue

title: The Split - How 8 Heads Work
text: d gets its own W_Q, W_K, W_V.8 heads = 8 independent sets of weight matrices. Each set is smaller (512×64 instead of 512×512):Head 1W_Q W_K W_V512×64 eachHead 2W_Q W_K W_V512×64 eachHead 3W_Q W_K W_V512×64 eachHead 4W_Q W_K W_V512×64 eachHead 5W_Q W_K W_V512×64 eachHead 6W_Q W_K W_V512×64 eachHead 7W_Q W_K W_V512×64 eachHead 8W_Q W_K W_V512×64 eachSame embedding goes into all 8 heads. Each head's different W matrices produce a different Query arrow - pointing in a different direction, asking a different question.Continue

title: The Split - How 8 Heads Work
text: , 8 questions, nothing lost."sat" in "The cat sat on the mat last week" - each head's Query arrow points differently:Head 1asks: "who did the action?"→ cat 70%Head 2asks: "where?"→ mat 65%Head 3asks: "when?"→ week 55%Head 4asks: "what comes next?"→ on 60%...and heads 5-8 ask about grammar, coreference, proximity, etc.Each head specializes. Together they capture everything - no information lost.Continue

title: The Split - How 8 Heads Work
text: out: layers.A Transformer doesn't have just one set of 8 heads. It stacks multiple layers, each with its own 8 heads:Layer 96reasoning, generation......Layer 3meaning, sentimentLayer 2syntax, clause structureLayer 1word proximity, basic grammar96 layers × 8 heads = 768 attention patternsEach layer reads the previous layer's output. Early layers see raw words. Later layers see increasingly abstract, context-rich representations.Nobody told the model which layer should handle grammar or reasoning. It organized itself this way during training.

Each produces [3 words × 64 dims].Continue

title: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims

Here's what "sat" sees in each head:Head 1 - "sat" attends to: (subject-verb)cat70%mat8%week5%others17%Head 2 - "sat" attends to: (verb-location)cat10%mat65%week8%others17%Head 3 - "sat" attends to: (temporal)cat8%mat7%week55%others30%Each head found what the single head missed. Head 3 got "last week" at 55% - single head only gave it 5%.

title: Concat + W_O - Blending All Heads
text: The outputs are separate - we need to combine them.After the 8 heads finish, each word has 8 separate results. For "love":Head 1 gave:[0.3, 0.5]← figured out "I" is the one who lovesHead 2 gave:[-0.2, 0.8]← figured out "cats" is what's lovedHead 3 gave:[0.7, -0.1]← figured out the sentiment is positive... 8 results totalWe stick them end-to-end (concatenate):[0.3, 0.5, -0.2, 0.8, 0.7, -0.1, ..., 0.1, 0.4]One long list - 512 numbers. But there's a problem.Continue

title: Concat + W_O - Blending All Heads
text: lem - these results are like 8 sealed envelopes.8 detectives investigated the same crime. Each wrote findings and sealed them:✉️Head 1"suspect is male, 30s"sealed✉️Head 2"happened at the park"sealed✉️Head 3"Tuesday evening"sealed✉️Head 4"weapon was a knife"sealedTape them together (concatenation):H1H2H3H4H5H6H7H8thick black walls between each section - they can't see each otherThey're physically side by side, but each section is still sealed. H1's numbers have no idea what H2 found. H3 can't see H4. They're taped together but not talking.Boss asks: "one-line summary?" No envelope has the full picture. H1 only knows the suspect. H2 only knows the location. Nobody can combine them.We need someone to open all the envelopes, read everything, and write one combined summary.Continue

title: Concat + W_O - Blending All Heads
text: e person who opens all the envelopes.W_O is just another weight matrix - a grid of numbers, same as W_Q, W_K, W_V. Multiply input by grid, get output. Nothing new in how it works.But what it does is special. When you multiply the concatenated list by W_O, every number in the output is computed from all numbers in the input.Before W_OPosition 1 = 0.3(only knows Head 1's finding)After W_OPosition 1 = blend of 0.3 + (-0.2) + 0.7 + ... all positions= knows Head 1 + Head 2 + Head 3 + ... everythingIt's like the combined summary: "A male suspect in his 30s used a knife at the park on Tuesday evening." One sentence that contains information from all envelopes.After W_O, every single position in the output carries a mix of what all 8 heads found. The envelopes have been opened, read, and combined.Continue

title: Concat + W_O - Blending All Heads
text: d W_O's numbers come from?Same exact process as all other grids:1.Start with random numbers2.Model tries to predict next word, gets it wrong3.Error flows backward, nudges each number in W_O4."If this number were 0.31 instead of 0.30, the blending would have been better → change to 0.31"5.Repeat billions of times6.W_O settles into numbers that produce good blendsThe training discovers the best way to combine the 8 heads' findings into one useful output. Nobody programs this - it emerges from training.Continue

title: Concat + W_O - Blending All Heads
text: "I love cats" - what W_O does for "love":After 8 heads run, "love"'s concatenated vector has:Dims 1–64Head 1 found: "I" is the subject who lovesDims 65–128Head 2 found: "cats" is the object being lovedDims 129–192Head 3 found: this is a positive sentimentWithout W_O: each section is isolated. Dim 1 has no idea "cats" is involved.After W_O: every dimension of "love"'s output encodes the combined meaning: "affection from 'I' directed at 'cats', with positive sentiment." One rich, integrated vector.This is what we set out to do in Chapter 1 - transform "love" from an isolated word into a context-aware representation.Continue

title: Concat + W_O - Blending All Heads
text: EARNED - not hardcoded.The model discovers during training which combinations of head outputs are useful. Different layers learn different W_O matrices:Layer 1's W_O might focus on combining basic syntax patternsLayer 3's W_O might focus on combining semantic relationshipsLayer 6's W_O might focus on combining abstract reasoningNobody programs what to combine. W_O's 512×512 = 262,144 weights are all learned through backpropagation - the same process from chapters 1.3–1.9.

title: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Why 8 heads? Why not 4 or 16?There's a tradeoff - the total dims (512) get divided equally:16 heads512 ÷ 16 = 32 dims each✅ More pattern types❌ Each head weaker4 heads512 ÷ 4 = 128 dims each✅ Each head powerful❌ Fewer patterns8 heads × 64 dims = sweet spotEnough capacity per head (64 dims) + enough perspectives (8 types)Continue

Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: ng: same count as single-head!Single-head with 512-dim Q, K, V needs 512×512 per matrix = same total. Multi-head doesn't add parameters - it reorganizes them into 8 independent groups. Same budget, much better results.Continue

title: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: this scale in a full model?We learned that one attention layer has ~1 million parameters. A Transformer stacks multiple layers on top of each other - each layer refines the context further:Original Transformer (2017)6 layers~6M attention paramsGPT-2 (2019)48 layers~48M attention paramsGPT-3 (2020)96 layers175 BILLION total paramsMore layers = deeper understanding. Each layer's attention asks different questions about the same words, building increasingly rich representations. Same mechanism we learned - just stacked.

title: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: Is W_O constant? Does it change?There are two phases in a model's life: training and usage. The answer depends on which phase you're asking about.Continue

title: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: raining: W_O changes.Every time the model sees a new batch of sentences and makes prediction errors, every number in W_O gets nudged slightly. Over billions of examples, W_O gradually improves. It changes millions of times during training - getting better and better at blending heads.After training is done: W_O is FROZEN. Completely constant.Once training is complete, the model is saved to a file. W_O's numbers are written to disk and they NEVER change again. When you use the model (send it a sentence), W_O is loaded from disk and used as-is.Continue

title: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: "I love cats" → model uses the same W_OYou type "dogs are great" → model uses the same W_OYou type "the weather is nice" → model uses the same W_OSomeone else types anything → model uses the same W_OThe same grid of numbers. Every input. Every user. Every sentence. Forever (until someone retrains the model).Continue

title: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: true for all grids, not just W_O:CONSTANT after training (stored on disk, never changes):├── Embedding dictionary├── W_Q₁ through W_Q₈ ← same grid for every input├── W_K₁ through W_K₈ ← same grid for every input├── W_V₁ through W_V₈ ← same grid for every input├── W_O ← same grid for every input├── All other layer weightsDifferent for every input (computed live, then thrown away):├── Q, K, V vectors ← different because the words are different├── Attention scores ← different because Q, K are different├── Softmax weights ← different because scores are different├── Blended outputs ← different because weights and V are different├── Final output ← different because everything above is differentContinue

title: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: ifferent sentences get different results if the grids are constant?Because the grids are like a coffee machine. The machine (W_O) is always the same. But the coffee beans you put in (your sentence) are different each time. Same machine + different beans = different coffee.Same W_O × "I love cats" embeddings = one outputSame W_O × "dogs are great" embeddings = completely different outputThe grids don't need to change. They've already learned the GENERAL SKILL of "how to blend heads" or "how to extract queries." That skill works on ANY input. Just like a coffee machine doesn't need to be rebuilt for each type of bean - it already knows how to grind and brew.

title: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text: The complete picture in plain English:📖1. Words become numbers(embedding lookup)🔧2. Those numbers × three grids(W_Q, W_K, W_V → Query, Key, Value)🔍3. Queries dot-product with Keys(find "who is relevant to whom")📊4. Scores get normalized(scale + softmax → percentages)🎯5. Percentages blend the Values(weighted sum → context-aware output)🔀6. Steps 2-5 happen 8 times with different grids(multi-head → 8 separate results)📎7. Stick the 8 results end-to-end(concatenate → 8 sealed envelopes taped together)✉️8. That list × one more grid(W_O → open all envelopes, write combined summary)✅9. Output: one blended vector per word(ready for the next layer)Continue

title: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text: ngle "grid" in this process (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) is just a table of numbers that started random, got nudged into useful values during training, and then stays frozen forever after. They all work the same way - multiply input by grid, get output. The only difference is what each grid was trained to do:W_Q gridslearned to extract "what a word is looking for"W_K gridslearned to extract "what a word can be found for"W_V gridslearned to extract "what actual info a word carries"W_O gridlearned to blend all heads' findings into one combined summaryContinue

title: Encoder & Decoder - The Two Halves
text: The original Transformer: 2017 "Attention Is All You Need"The paper that introduced Transformers was solving machine translation. It needed two different operations:"I love cats"English input→Transformer→"J'aime les chats"French outputThe key problem: English and French are different languages. You must fully understand the English input before you can generate French output. These are two separate tasks.Continue

title: Encoder & Decoder - The Two Halves
text: : Two separate componentsEncoder: reads the input, builds deep understanding → outputs compressed representationDecoder: reads the encoder's output, generates the translation one word at a timeEncoder✓ Process input in parallel✓ All words attend to all words✓ Build context for each wordEncoder output: rich representations of "I", "love", "cats"Decoder✓ Read encoder output✓ Generate translation token by token✓ Use attention to look back at inputDecoder: "J'aime" → "les" → "chats"Continue

title: Encoder & Decoder - The Two Halves
text: tention: the bridgeThe decoder doesn't just generate from nothing. It looks back at the encoder's output using a special connection called cross-attention.Encoder output: [rich understanding of "I", "love", "cats"]↓ (Keys & Values)Cross-Attention LayerDecoder's Q asks encoder: "What's relevant now?"↓Decoder generates: "J'aime les chats"When generating "les", the decoder's Query asks "what noun am I translating?" The encoder's Keys/Values answer: "you're translating the idea of 'love' and 'cats'". The decoder uses that context to choose the right French word.Continue

title: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer
text: Inside one Transformer block, there are 4 steps:Now let's zoom out and see what happens after attention inside a single Transformer block.Input Embeddings↓Multi-Head Attention↓Add & Norm↓FFN (Feed-Forward Network)↓Add & Norm↓OutputNotice: Add & Norm appears twice in every block - once after Attention, once after FFN. It's the stabilizer that keeps everything in check.Continue

title: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer
text: lem: Values Drift in Deep NetworksGPT-3 has 96 layers. Each layer multiplies values by weights (we learned this in chapters 1.4-1.6). The result depends on the weights - if they're slightly below 1.0, values shrink. If slightly above, values explode. Keeping them perfectly balanced across 96 layers is nearly impossible.Take 0.7 from "cats" embedding. Two scenarios:If weights average 0.9 (shrink)Start0.7Layer 100.24Layer 500.004Layer 960.00003Value vanishes to nearly zeroIf weights average 1.1 (grow)Start0.7Layer 101.8Layer 5082Layer 964,838Value explodes to thousandsBoth are broken. Whether the value goes to 0.00003 or 4,838 - it's no longer a useful embedding. And gradients during backpropagation (chapter 1.9) drift the same way, so the model can't learn either.Continue

title: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer
text: " - Keep the OriginalThe fix is beautifully simple: add the original input back to the output. That's it. The fancy name for this is "residual connection" - "residual" just means "leftover." You're keeping the leftover original so it never gets lost.How it works for "cats" (embedding = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]):original input[-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]↓ goes through Multi-Head Attention ↓attention output[0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]The Add Step: original + attention output[-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]+[0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]=[-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]Think of it as a highway: even if the attention layer learns nothing useful, the original embedding still passes through untouched. During backpropagation (chapter 1.9), the error can travel backward along this highway without shrinking to zero.Without the AddInput → Attention → OutputIf attention messes up, values are lostWith the AddInput → Attention → Output + InputOriginal values always surviveContinue

title: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer
text: m" - Layer NormalizationAfter adding, values can drift to unpredictable ranges. Layer Norm rescales them to a stable range. Here is the actual formula used in every Transformer:The Layer Normalization FormulaLayerNorm(xi) =γ·xi - μ√(σ2 + ε)+βxi = each value in the vectorthe raw input we want to normalizeμ (mu) = average of all valuescenters the data around zeroσ² (sigma squared) = variance of all valuesmeasures how spread out values areε (epsilon) = tiny number (1e-5)prevents division by zero if all values are identicalγ (gamma) = learnable scale (starts at 1.0)lets the model stretch or shrink the rangeβ (beta) = learnable shift (starts at 0.0)lets the model move the center up or downgamma and beta are learned during training - they let the model undo the normalization if needed. They let the model stretch, shrink, or shift the normalized values to whatever range works best for each dimension.Continue

title: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer
text: g Layer Norm - Step by StepLet's apply the formula to real numbers. We're normalizing the result from the Add step:Normalizing [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] (the result from Add)Step 1: Compute the mean (mu)mu = (-0.2 + 0.2 + 0.9 + 1.0) / 4 =0.475Step 2: subtract the mean from each value (xi - mu)-0.2 - 0.475 = -0.675 0.2 - 0.475 = -0.275 0.9 - 0.475 = 0.425 1.0 - 0.475 = 0.525 Centered: [-0.675, -0.275, 0.425, 0.525]Step 3: Compute variance, then divide by sqrt(σ² + ε)variance = mean of squared deviations: (0.675 squared + 0.275 squared + 0.425 squared + 0.525 squared) / 4 = 0.2469sqrt(0.2469 + 0.00001) = 0.4969 (epsilon = 1e-5 prevents division by zero if all values were identical)-0.675 / 0.4969 = -1.36 -0.275 / 0.4969 = -0.55 0.425 / 0.4969 = 0.86 0.525 / 0.4969 = 1.06 After this step: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] - centered at 0, unit variance.Step 4: Scale by gamma, shift by betagamma and beta are per-dimension learnable parameters. Initially gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0, so at first this step does nothing. During training, the model learns optimal values.With initial gamma=1.0, beta=0.0:1.0 x -1.36 + 0.0 = -1.36 1.0 x -0.55 + 0.0 = -0.55 1.0 x 0.86 + 0.0 = 0.86 1.0 x 1.06 + 0.0 = 1.06 Why have gamma and beta if they start as identity? Because during training, the model may learn that some dimensions need more spread (gamma > 1) or a non-zero center (beta != 0). Without them, normalization would be too rigid.Final output: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]Values centered around 0 with controlled spread. Gamma and beta will adjust these during training to whatever the model needs.Continue

title: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer
text: lete Add & Norm PipelineWatch "cats" flow through the entire Add & Norm after Attention:InputInput (cats embedding)[-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]goes into Attention↓AttentionAttention Output[0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]Add: input + output↓AddAfter Add[-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]Normalize↓NormAfter Norm[-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]ready for FFN↓NextTo FFN[-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]This exact same Add & Norm happens again after FFN. Every Transformer block does this twice - once after Attention, once after FFN.Continue

title: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer
text: atters - The Big PictureWithout Add & NormLayer 10.7Layer 100.24Layer 500.004Layer 96~0Values drift to extremes. Model stops learning.With Add & NormLayer 10.7Layer 100.67Layer 500.62Layer 960.57Values stay stable. Gradients flow. Training works even at 96 layers deep.In one sentence:Add & Norm is the reason Transformers can be 96 layers deep without losing information. The "Add" preserves the original values, and the "Norm" keeps them in a stable range for the next layer.

title: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer
text: The Encoder: Add & Norm - The Stabilizer. This chapter covers add & norm - the stabilizer.

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: Where Does the Feed-Forward Network Sit?In chapter 8.2 we covered Add & Norm after Attention. Now let's zoom into the next step.Input Embeddings↓Multi-Head Attention↓Add & Norm↓FFN (Feed-Forward Network)↓Add & Norm↓OutputThe FFN sits between the two Add & Norm layers. It processes each token independently - no communication between words here. That's what makes it different from Attention.Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: o Linear Layers with ActivationYou already know what a layer does (chapter 1.19): multiply by a weight matrix W, add bias b. The FFN is just two of those layers stacked, with an activation function in between.The FFN FormulaFFN(x)=W2·GELU(W1·x+b1)+b2x = input from Add & NormW1, b1 = first layer weights & biasGELU = activation functionW2, b2 = second layer weights & biasThat's it. No attention, no softmax, no multi-head anything. Just two matrix multiplies with an activation in between - the same building blocks from Section 1.Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: nd-Then-Compress ShapeHere's the interesting part: the first layer expands the dimension, and the second layer compresses it back. In GPT-2 (d_model = 512):Feed-forward network architecture showing expand-then-compress structure: 512-dim input through W1 to 2048-dim hidden with GELU, then W2 back to 512-dim outputInput (512)Hidden (2048)Output (512)W₁512 x 2048W₂2048 x 512x₁x₂x₃h₁h₂h₃h₂₀₄₈y₁y₂y₃+ GELU3 of 512 input, 4 of 2048 hidden, 3 of 512 output shownThe hidden layer has 4x more neurons than the input or output. Every input neuron connects to every hidden neuron (W1), GELU activates each hidden neuron, then every hidden neuron connects to every output neuron (W2). The data expands into a wider "thinking space" (512 → 2048), then compresses back (2048 → 512).Why 4x?Imagine solving a complex problem: you spread your notes across a big desk (expand to 2048), work things out, then summarize your answer on a small card (compress back to 512). The 4x ratio was found in the original "Attention is All You Need" paper and has become standard across GPT-2, GPT-3, and most Transformers.Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: Step: FFN on "cats"Let's trace "cats" through the FFN. In chapter 8.2, Add & Norm output [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] for "cats". We'll use a tiny 4-dim version to show the real math (real models use 512 dims but the process is identical).Step 1: Multiply by W1 + b1 (expand 4 → 8 dims)[-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]·W1+b1Each of the 8 output values is a dot product of the input with one column of W1, plus a bias - exactly like chapter 1.19.Result (8 dims):[0.42, -1.80, 0.95, 2.10, -0.33, 1.47, -0.88, 0.61]Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: Step: FFN on "cats" (continued)Step 2: Apply GELU activationGELU applies to each value independently - similar to ReLU (chapter 1.6) but smoother.0.42 → 0.28-1.80 → -0.040.95 → 0.772.10 → 2.07-0.33 → -0.121.47 → 1.37-0.88 → -0.170.61 → 0.44[0.28, -0.04, 0.77, 2.07, -0.12, 1.37, -0.17, 0.44]Step 3: Multiply by W2 + b2 (compress 8 → 4 dims)The 8-dim expanded vector gets compressed back to the original 4 dims.[0.28, -0.04, ..., 0.44]·W2+b2FFN output for "cats" (4 dims):[0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]Same size in, same size out - but the values have been transformed through a "thinking space" 4x wider. This output now goes to the second Add & Norm.Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: he Activation FunctionIn chapter 1.6 we learned ReLU. Modern Transformers use a smoother alternative called GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit). Here is the real formula:The GELU FormulaGELU(x)=x·Φ(x)where Φ(x) is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution:Φ(x)=12[1 + erf(x√2)]x = the input valuethe raw number coming from the linear layerΦ(x) = probability x is "kept"ranges from 0 (fully blocked) to 1 (fully passed)erf = Gauss error functiona smooth S-curve that maps any number to [-1, 1]In plain English: GELU multiplies x by the probability that x would be considered "large" under a standard normal bell curve. Large positive numbers get through almost fully (Φ near 1). Large negative numbers get almost fully blocked (Φ near 0). Values near zero get partially passed - that's the smooth part.Worked Examples2.0·0.977=1.95Large positive: almost fully passed0.5·0.691=0.35Small positive: 69% passed through0.0·0.500=0.00Zero: exactly half of zero is zero-0.5·0.309=-0.15Small negative: 31% leaked through-2.0·0.023=-0.05Large negative: almost fully blockedReLU (chapter 1.6)if x < 0: output = 0if x > 0: output = xHard cutoff. Negative = dead. Gradient = 0 forever.GELU (smooth)if x < 0: output = small leakif x > 0: output = ~xSmooth transition. Small negatives leak through. Gradient never fully dies.Why does the smooth curve matter? During backpropagation (chapter 1.15), ReLU's gradient is exactly 0 for all negative inputs - the neuron is "dead" and can never recover. GELU's smooth curve means even slightly negative values get a small gradient, so neurons can recover. GPT-2 and GPT-3 both use GELU.Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: Stores - Knowledge vs RoutingAttention and FFN play completely different roles. Think of it this way: attention decides which words to listen to, FFN decides what to do with what it heard.Remember: inside every block, Attention runs first, then FFNFFN never sees the raw input - it always receives attention's output. Attention gathers context from other words. Then FFN transforms that context-enriched vector.Example 1: "The area of this rectangle depends on its length"Notice: "breadth" is never mentioned - but the model knows it mattersAttention Runs FirstQ · K scores for "area""area"'s Query asks "what determines me?" "length"'s Key scores high (0.55 after softmax) - they're clearly related. "rectangle"'s Key also matches (0.30) - it tells area what shape we're talking about.Weighted sum of ValuesOutput = 0.55 · Vlength + 0.30 · Vrectangle + ... The "area" vector now carries: "I'm the area of a rectangle and length is involved."Result: context assembled, but incompleteAttention found real connections between words that exist in the input. But "breadth" is not in the sentence. Attention can only mix what's there - it cannot add knowledge that's missing from the text.Then FFN Adds Stored KnowledgeW1: "rectangle-area" detector firesFFN receives the attention-enriched "area" vector (which already encodes "rectangle + length"). Row 1203 of W1 was trained on millions of math texts - it detects "area of a rectangle with length mentioned." Dot product: W1[1203] · x = 3.9GELU activates the right knowledgeGELU(3.9) = 3.89 → neuron 1203 fires strongly. Meanwhile, neuron 502 ("circle-area-pi") got -0.8 → GELU suppresses it. The network knows this is a rectangle, not a circle.W2: pushes "area" toward breadth-aware regionColumn 1203 of W2 shifts the vector into a region of embedding space that encodes "area = length x breadth, breadth is the missing factor." The model now implicitly knows breadth matters - even though the word never appeared in the input.This is what "stored knowledge" meansDuring training, the FFN saw "area = length x breadth" thousands of times. That relationship got baked into W1 row 1203 (detector) and W2 column 1203 (output). Attention found the words in the sentence. FFN added what it knows from training.Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: 2: "The capital of France is ___"Attention gathers context, FFN recalls the factAttention Runs FirstQ · K scores for "___" position"___"'s Query asks "what country am I about?" "France"'s Key scores highest (0.72 after softmax), "capital" gets 0.18, rest get scraps.Weighted sum of ValuesOutput = 0.72 · VFrance + 0.18 · Vcapital + ... This is a blend - a weighted average. It mixes "France" info into the "___" position.Result: context assembled, but no answer yetThe "___" vector now encodes "I need the capital of France." But attention can only average existing vectors - it cannot look up a fact. It assembled the question, not the answer.Then FFN Recalls the FactW1: "capital-of-France" detector firesRow 847 of W1 was trained on millions of geography texts. It detects the "capital-of-France" pattern in the vector that attention assembled. Dot product: W1[847] · x = 4.2GELU: only relevant knowledge survivesGELU(4.2) = 4.19 → neuron 847 fires. GELU(-1.3) = -0.05 → neuron 200 ("capital-of-Germany") is suppressed. Of 2048 neurons, maybe 100-300 fire meaningfully for any given input.W2: writes "Paris" into the vectorColumn 847 of W2 was trained to shift vectors toward "Paris." Since neuron 847 fired at 4.19, its column dominates the output: output += 4.19 · W2[:,847]. The answer is now in the vector.Key insight: W1 rows = "what to detect", W2 columns = "what to output"This is the key-value memory interpretation from research. The FFN is a lookup table: 2048 entries, each asking "does this pattern match?" and outputting the corresponding knowledge.Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: n + FFN Across Multiple BlocksExample 3: "She sat by the river bank"Does "bank" mean money or riverbed? Watch attention and FFN collaborate across blocks to resolve the ambiguity.Block 1: Basic context gatheringAttention"bank"'s Query matches "river"'s Key (adjacent words, high positional score). After softmax, "bank" absorbs ~40% of "river"'s Value vector. "sat" gets ~20% (nearby verb). Result: "bank" now carries "I'm next to the word river and someone sat."FFN (receives attention's output)Sees "bank + some river context." W1 row 310 ("river-location" detector) fires weakly: 1.2. W1 row 744 ("financial-institution" detector) also fires weakly: 0.8. GELU passes both through. W2 nudges the vector slightly toward both meanings. Ambiguity not yet resolved.Block 5: Deeper semantic understandingAttention"bank" now attends to "sat" (physical action, not financial), "by" (location preposition), and "river" with much higher scores than Block 1. Why higher? Because Blocks 1-4 FFNs already strengthened the "nature/location" features in "sat" and "river." Their Value vectors are now richer, so the weighted sum gives "bank" a much stronger location signal.FFN (receives attention's output)Now row 310 ("river-location") fires at 3.8 (strong match). Row 744 ("financial") fires at -1.1 → GELU(-1.1) = -0.03, effectively dead. W2 column 310 shifts the vector hard toward geography/nature features, column 744 contributes almost nothing. "Bank" now means "riverbank" - ambiguity resolved.Why this takes multiple blocksEach round, attention provides better context because FFN improved the vectors, and FFN makes better decisions because attention gathered more context. They bootstrap each other. Block 1 FFN nudges slightly, Block 2 attention uses that nudge to focus better, Block 2 FFN nudges harder, and so on across all 96 blocks.Three examples, three types of FFN knowledge:1. Implicit knowledge (area/breadth) - FFN adds concepts that aren't in the input but are related by training data2. Factual recall (France/Paris) - FFN looks up a stored fact and writes the answer into the vector3. Disambiguation (bank/river) - FFN resolves ambiguity by suppressing wrong meanings and boosting the right oneContinue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: stions You Should Be Able to AnswerWhy can't attention replace FFN?Attention computes weighted averages of Value vectors - a linear operation. It can mix information from different tokens, but it can never create new features. "If river AND bank are both present, output riverbank" requires non-linearity (GELU). Only FFN has that.Why can't FFN replace attention?FFN processes each token in complete isolation. It never sees any other word. Without attention first injecting "river" into "bank"'s vector, FFN would process "bank" identically in "river bank" and "investment bank" - same input, same output, unable to disambiguate.What exactly does "FFN stores knowledge" mean?Each row of W₁ is a pattern detector trained during pre-training. Row 847 learned to fire when the input looks like "capital-of-France." The corresponding column of W₂ learned to output a vector that shifts toward "Paris." This row-column pair is literally one stored fact. GPT-3 has 2048-49152 such detectors per block across 96 blocks.How do researchers prove this isn't just a metaphor?Ablation studies: zero out specific FFN neurons and watch "The capital of France is" stop producing "Paris" while grammar stays intact. Activate those same neurons artificially and watch "Paris" appear in unrelated contexts. The knowledge is localized to specific neurons - not distributed across the whole network.Continue

title: FFN - The Feed-Forward Network
text: the Parameters Live?Parameters are all the learnable numbers in the model - every weight and bias that got tuned during training (chapter 1.4). They are the model's "memory." More parameters = more capacity to store knowledge.Here's how they split between Attention and FFN in one Transformer block:One Transformer Block (~3.15M parameters total)Attention (1/3)FFN (2/3)Attention: ~1.05MW_Q, W_K, W_V = 3 matrices that create the Queries, Keys, and Values (chapter 6.9)W_O = the matrix that blends multi-head outputs (chapter 7.12)These learn how to find relationships between wordsFFN: ~2.1MW1 (512 x 2048) = expands to thinking spaceW2 (2048 x 512) = compresses back to model sizeThese learn facts and transformations - the actual knowledgeGPT-3 has 96 blocks. That's 96 copies of this structure = 96 x 3.15M = ~302M parameters just in one layer type - and GPT-3's total is 175 billion because d_model is 12,288 (not 512), making each matrix vastly larger.

title: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Recap: FFN With Just One WordIn chapter 8.3 we traced one word ("cats") through FFN. Let's make the matrix shapes explicit. Say "cats" is a 3-dimensional vector:"cats"0.5-0.30.91x3 (one word, 3 dims)×W1wwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwww3x6 (3 dims in, 6 dims out)=Hiddenhhhhhh1x6 (expanded)One word: 1x3 times 3x6 = 1x6. The weight matrix W1 has 3 x 6 = 18 weights. These 18 weights are the same for every single word that ever goes through this FFN.Continue

title: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: k: Stack Words Into a MatrixWhat if we have 3 words? We don't run the FFN 3 times. We stack all 3 word vectors into a single matrix and multiply once:3 words stacked0.5-0.30.90.20.8-0.10.7-0.50.33x3 ("I", "love", "cats")×same W1wwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwww3x6 (unchanged!)=3 outputshhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhh3x6 (one row per word)3x3 times 3x6 = 3x6. Still the same W1. Still 18 weights. Row 1 of the input only interacts with W to produce row 1 of the output. No mixing between words - that's how matrix multiplication works.Continue

title: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: "The cat sat on the mat"Now let's use our full example sentence. 6 words, each a 3-dim vector. Stack them into a 6x3 matrix:6 words0.2-0.90.50.60.3-0.2-0.50.90.10.1-0.40.80.2-0.90.50.70.2-0.66x3×same W13x618 weights=6 outputs6x6one row per word6x3 times 3x6 = 6x6. The W1 matrix is still 3x6 = 18 weights. Doesn't matter that we went from 3 words to 6 - the weight matrix stays the same size.Think about it: row 1 ("The") hits W and produces its own output. Row 4 ("on") hits the exact same W and produces its own output. The rows never interact. Each word is independently transformed by the same 18 weights.Continue

title: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: ? 1000 Words? Same 18 Weights.This is the key insight. The number of words is just the number of rows in the input matrix. The weight matrix W never changes:1 word1x3×3x6=1x6W1 = 18 weights3 words3x3×3x6=3x6W1 = 18 weights6 words6x3×3x6=6x6W1 = 18 weights10 words10x3×3x6=10x6W1 = 18 weights1000 words1000x3×3x6=1000x6W1 = 18 weightsThe Pattern(num_words x d_model) × W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (num_words x d_ff)The "num_words" dimension is a free variable. It can be anything. W1 only cares about d_model (3 in our toy example, 512 in real transformers). That's why the model can accept any length input with the same 18 parameters.In GPT-2 (d_model=512, d_ff=2048): W1 has 512 x 2048 = 1,048,576 weights. That number stays the same whether you feed it 10 words or 100,000 words.Continue

title: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Back Down: W2 Has the Inverse ShapeAfter GELU activation, we need to compress back to the original dimension. W2 simply has the flipped shape:6 words: expand then compressInput6x3×W13x6=Expanded6x6→GELU'd6x6×W26x3=Output6x310 words: same W1, same W2Input10x3×W13x6=Expanded10x6→GELU'd10x6×W26x3=Output10x3The General PatternLayer 1: (words x d_model) × W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (words x d_ff)GELU: (words x d_ff) → (words x d_ff)Layer 2: (words x d_ff) × W2 (d_ff x d_model) = (words x d_model)Word count flows through as the row dimension. W1 and W2 only depend on d_model and d_ff. In, expand, compress back out - same size going in and coming out, regardless of how many words.Continue

title: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Matters: Massive GPU ParallelismThe reason we stack words into a matrix isn't just math elegance - it's about hardware speed.Without stacking (for loop)for word in words:output = word × W1000 words = 1000 sequential matrix multiplies. Each one waits for the previous to finish. The GPU sits mostly idle because each multiply is tiny (1x3 × 3x6).With stacking (one multiply)output = all_words × W1000 words = one massive parallel matrix multiply (1000x3 × 3x6). GPU processes all rows simultaneously across thousands of cores. One instruction, all words done.The Deep InsightThis parallel trick works because FFN treats each word independently. No word's output depends on any other word. That's the fundamental difference from Attention, which creates cross-word dependencies (Q · K). You can't parallelize Attention the same way because token 3's output depends on tokens 1 and 2.But FFN? Each row hits the same W, gets the same GELU, comes out independently. It's embarrassingly parallel - the GPU's dream workload.This is also why the KV cache during generation only caches attention K,V - not FFN outputs. FFN has no cross-word state to remember. Run it on the new word, get the answer. Previous words' FFN results are already baked into the K,V cache.

title: Add & Norm (Again) - The Second Stabilizer
text: The Second Add & NormThe FFN just transformed each token's representation. But remember the problem from chapter 8.2 - values drift in deep networks. We need to stabilize again.Multi-Head Attention↓Add & Norm (first)↓FFN↓Add & Norm (second)↓Block OutputThis second Add & Norm works exactly the same way as the first - but its input is the FFN output instead of the Attention output.Continue

title: Add & Norm (Again) - The Second Stabilizer
text: - Residual Around FFNSame idea as chapter 8.2: keep the original input by adding it back. The input to FFN was the first Add & Norm's output.Continuing with "cats":FFN input[-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]↓ goes through FFN (chapter 8.3) ↓FFN output[0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]The Add Step: FFN input + FFN output[-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]+[0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]=[-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]The residual connection ensures that even if the FFN distorts values, the original signal from Add & Norm #1 survives by being added back.Continue

title: Add & Norm (Again) - The Second Stabilizer
text: - Stabilize AgainSame Layer Normalization formula from chapter 8.2 - compute mean, subtract, divide by standard deviation, scale by gamma, shift by beta.Normalizing [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]mu = (-0.85 + -1.28 + 2.00 + 1.28) / 4 = 0.2875variance = 1.9172, sqrt(variance + epsilon) = 1.3846Normalize each: (x - mu) / 1.3846-0.85 → -0.82-1.28 → -1.132.00 → 1.241.28 → 0.72Block output for "cats": [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]With gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0 (initial values). The model will learn optimal gamma and beta during training.Continue

title: Add & Norm (Again) - The Second Stabilizer
text: lete Single Block - All 4 StepsHere's "cats" flowing through one entire Transformer block, start to finish:InputInput (cats)[-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]Multi-Head Attention↓AttnAttention Output[0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]Add & Norm #1↓A&N 1After 1st Add & Norm[-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]Feed-Forward Network↓FFNFFN Output[0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]Add & Norm #2↓A&N 2Block Output[-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]One block: Attention moves information between tokens, Add & Norm stabilizes, FFN processes each token's knowledge, Add & Norm stabilizes again. This is the heartbeat of every Transformer.Continue

title: Add & Norm (Again) - The Second Stabilizer
text: e? - One Per Sub-LayerAdd & Norm appears twice because each Transformer block has two sub-layers: Attention and FFN. Each sub-layer gets its own Add & Norm because each one can independently cause value drift.Sub-layer 1: AttentionInput x↓Attention(x)↓Add & Norm #1Stabilizes after attention changesSub-layer 2: FFNFrom A&N #1↓FFN(x)↓Add & Norm #2Stabilizes after FFN changesThe RuleIn Transformers, every sub-layer that transforms values gets wrapped with its own residual connection and layer norm. This is what makes it possible to stack 96 blocks deep without the signal degrading. If you only normalized once at the end of the block, the FFN's output could already be in a bad range before it gets stabilized.

title: Add & Norm (Again) - The Second Stabilizer
text: The Encoder: Add & Norm (Again) - The Second Stabilizer. This chapter covers add & norm (again) - the second stabilizer.

title: Nx - The Transformer Block Repeats
text: One Block Does Not Repeat EnoughWe've now seen all 4 steps inside one Transformer block: Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm. But one block can only learn simple patterns. To understand language, the model needs to repeat this entire block many times.Think of it like this:1 blockbasic word associations ("cat" relates to "sat")4 blocksgrammar structure (subject-verb agreement)12 blockscontext and nuance (sarcasm, tone)96 blocksdeep reasoning, world knowledge, complex inferenceEach repeat of the block refines the token representations further. The output of block 1 becomes the input of block 2, and so on.Continue

title: Nx - The Transformer Block Repeats
text: k - How Many Blocks?Different models use different numbers of blocks. In the original paper and in practice, this number is called N (hence "Nx" in architecture diagrams).GPT-2 Small12 blocksd=768GPT-2 Medium24 blocksd=1024GPT-2 Large36 blocksd=1280GPT-396 blocksd=12288What This MeansIn GPT-3, every single token in your prompt passes through 96 identical blocks in sequence. Each block runs the full Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm pipeline. That's 96 rounds of attention, 96 rounds of FFN, and 192 Add & Norm operations per token.Continue

title: Nx - The Transformer Block Repeats
text: ucture, Different WeightsEvery block has the same structure: Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm. But every learnable weight inside each block is unique - learned separately during training. Here's every weight and where it lives:Multi-Head AttentionW_Q(ch 6.9)Creates Queries - "what am I looking for?"W_K(ch 6.9)Creates Keys - "what do I contain?"W_V(ch 6.9)Creates Values - "what info do I pass along?"W_O(ch 7.12)Blends all 8 heads back togetherAdd & Norm #1 (after Attention)γ₁ (gamma)(ch 8.2)Learnable scale per dimension - stretches or shrinks normalized valuesβ₁ (beta)(ch 8.2)Learnable shift per dimension - moves the center up or downFeed-Forward NetworkW₁, b₁(ch 8.3)First layer - expands from 512 to 2048 dims (the thinking space)W₂, b₂(ch 8.3)Second layer - compresses from 2048 back to 512 dimsAdd & Norm #2 (after FFN)γ₂ (gamma)(ch 8.5)Same role as gamma above, but a separate copy learned independentlyβ₂ (beta)(ch 8.5)Same role as beta above, but a separate copy learned independentlyEvery block has its own copy of ALL these weightsBlock 1 has its own W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, W1, W2, gammas, and betas. Block 2 has completely different weights. Block 96 has yet another set. If they shared the same weights, stacking 96 blocks would be no better than having one - you'd just repeat the same transformation. Each block's unique weights let it specialize: Block 1 learns different patterns than Block 50.Continue

title: Nx - The Transformer Block Repeats
text: h Layer Learns - Shallow to DeepResearch on Transformer internals has revealed a clear pattern: early blocks learn simple things, deep blocks learn abstract things.Blocks 1-4Surface Patterns●Part-of-speech (noun, verb, adjective)●Basic grammar and word order●Simple co-occurrence ("New" near "York")↓Blocks 5-12Syntax & Structure●Subject-verb agreement across distance●Clause boundaries and nesting●Pronoun resolution ("she" refers to "Alice")↓Blocks 13-24Semantics & Meaning●Word sense disambiguation ("bank" = river vs money)●Sentiment and tone detection●Entity relationships and facts↓Blocks 25-96Abstract Reasoning●Multi-step inference and logic●World knowledge and common sense●Task-specific meaning and generationThis is why depth matters - you need many blocks to build from grammar all the way up to meaning and reasoning. It's the same principle as "deep" networks from chapter 1.21, but now each block has both attention (for context) and FFN (for knowledge).Continue

title: Nx - The Transformer Block Repeats
text: lete Picture - Token to Output Through N BlocksHere's the full journey of a single token from input to output:Vertical Transformer encoder block stack showing token embedding plus positional encoding feeding through repeated blocks with self-attention, add and norm, and FFN layersToken Embedding + Positional Encoding(Sections 5.2-5.7)Block 1AttentionAdd & NormFFNAdd & NormBlock 2AttentionAdd & NormFFNAdd & NormBlock NAttentionAdd & NormFFNAdd & Norm...Encoder Outputcontextual hidden states for each tokenNxGPT-2 SmallN = 12 blocks, 117M parametersGPT-3N = 96 blocks, 175B parametersThe full recipe of a Transformer encoder:Tokenize → Embed → Add positions → Pass through N blocks (each: Self-Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm) → Rich contextual hidden states. The encoder uses bidirectional attention - every word can see every other word, no masking. This gives the encoder a full understanding of the entire input.

title: Residual Connections - The Gradient Highway
text: The Problem: Gradients Vanish in Deep NetworksIn chapter 1.15, we learned that gradients shrink as they travel backward through layers. During backpropagation, each layer multiplies the gradient by its derivative. If that derivative is even slightly below 1.0, the gradient shrinks exponentially.Multiplying through 96 layers:F'(x) = 0.990.99^96 = 0.38Even near-perfect derivatives lose 62% of the gradientF'(x) = 0.90.9^96 = 0.0000396Gradient is effectively zero by layer 1F'(x) = 0.50.5^96 = 1.3 x 10^-29Completely gone - no learning possibleGPT-3 has 96 layers. Without a fix, the gradient arriving at layer 1 from layer 96 would be essentially zero. Layer 1 would never learn. The model would be dead on arrival.Continue

title: Residual Connections - The Gradient Highway
text: dual Connection: output = F(x) + xThe fix is deceptively simple: add the input directly to the output. The "+ x" creates a shortcut path that bypasses the entire layer.Path A: Without Residualinput x↓weight1↓activation↓weight2↓activation↓outputGradient must travel through every layer. It shrinks at each step.Path B: With Residualinput x↓weight1↓activation↓weight2↓activation↓F(x) + x↓skip!The "+ x" adds a direct highway that bypasses all layers.Continue

title: Residual Connections - The Gradient Highway
text: olves Vanishing GradientsThe math is elegant. Take the derivative of the residual output:The Residual Gradientddx[F(x)+ x]=F'(x)+ 1F'(x) = gradient through the layer weightscan shrink toward 0+ 1 = gradient through the skip connectionALWAYS exactly 1, no matter whatThat + 1 is everything. Even if F'(x) approaches 0, the total gradient is at least 1. The gradient through the skip connection is always 1, regardless of what the layer weights do.Without residualGradient = F'(x)If F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 0.01With residualGradient = F'(x) + 1If F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 1.01Continue

title: Residual Connections - The Gradient Highway
text: ient HighwayVisualize a 6-layer transformer. Gradients travel backward from Layer 6 to Layer 1 via two paths:Layer 6Through weights1.00Through skip: always 1.01.00↑ gradient flows backward ↑Layer 5Through weights0.70Through skip: always 1.01.00↑ gradient flows backward ↑Layer 4Through weights0.49Through skip: always 1.01.00↑ gradient flows backward ↑Layer 3Through weights0.34Through skip: always 1.01.00↑ gradient flows backward ↑Layer 2Through weights0.24Through skip: always 1.01.00↑ gradient flows backward ↑Layer 1Through weights0.17Through skip: always 1.01.00Through weights: 0.7^5 = 0.17 (lost 83% of the signal). Through skip connections: 1.00 (full signal preserved). The skip path delivers the gradient from layer 6 to layer 1 without any multiplication.Continue

title: Residual Connections - The Gradient Highway
text: Is Critical for TransformersModern transformers are absurdly deep. Without residual connections, training them would be impossible.GPT-212 layers24 gradient highwaysGPT-396 layers192 gradient highwaysGPT-4 (est.)120 layers240 gradient highwaysTWO residual connections per blockEvery transformer block has two sub-layers: Attention and FFN. Each gets its own residual connection (chapter 8.2). That means GPT-3's 96 blocks have 192 gradient highways - one around every Attention layer and one around every FFN layer.Input↓Attention + Residual #1↓FFN + Residual #2↓Block OutputWithout residual connections, gradients would vanish by layer 3. With them, gradients flow freely through all 96 layers on the skip path, letting even the earliest layers learn from the loss signal. This is what makes deep transformers trainable.

title: Residual Connections - The Gradient Highway
text: The Encoder: Residual Connections - The Gradient Highway. This chapter covers residual connections - the gradient highway.

title: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm
text: Post-Norm (Original 2017 Paper)In the original "Attention Is All You Need" paper, LayerNorm comes after the residual Add. This is what we covered in chapter 8.2. Here is the exact order:Input x↓Attention(x)↓Add: x + Attention(x)↓LayerNorm↓FFN↓Add: prev + FFN(prev)↓LayerNormKey feature: LayerNorm sits after the residual add. The residual path passes through LayerNorm before reaching the next sub-layer.Continue

title: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm
text: (Modern Approach)In pre-norm, LayerNorm comes before each sub-layer. The residual add gets the raw, unnormalized input - not the normalized version.Input x↓LayerNorm(x)↓Attention(LayerNorm(x))↓Add: x + Attention(LayerNorm(x))↓LayerNorm(prev)↓FFN(LayerNorm(prev))↓Add: prev + FFN(LayerNorm(prev))Key difference: the Add step receives the raw input x, not the normalized version. LayerNorm only feeds into the sub-layer, not into the skip path.Continue

title: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm
text: Side ComparisonLet's trace input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] ("cats" embedding) through the Attention sub-layer in both approaches:Post-NormInput x[-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]Attention(x)[0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]Add: x + Attn[-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]LayerNorm[-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]Norm is last. Skip path passes through Norm.Pre-NormInput x[-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]LayerNorm(x)[-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]Attn(LN(x))[0.28, -0.12, 0.18, 0.38]Add: x + Attn[-0.22, 0.18, 0.88, 0.98]Norm is first. Skip path is clean - just addition.Notice the critical difference: in post-norm, the skip path goes through LayerNorm. In pre-norm, the skip path is just pure addition - no transformations on the highway.Continue

title: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm
text: Norm WinsTwo key advantages make pre-norm the clear winner for deep models:1. Cleaner Gradient FlowIn post-norm, the gradient on the skip path must pass through LayerNorm at every block. LayerNorm has its own derivative that modifies the gradient - not as bad as vanishing, but it adds noise and makes optimization harder.In pre-norm, the skip path is pure addition. The gradient flows from the last block to the first block on a clean highway with derivative = 1 at every step. No LayerNorm interference on the residual stream.2. Training StabilityPost-norm requires very careful learning rate warmup - start with a tiny learning rate and slowly increase it over thousands of steps. Without warmup, gradients in early training are unstable and the model diverges (loss goes to infinity).Pre-norm is more forgiving. It works with larger learning rates from the start. The clean gradient highway stabilizes training even without careful warmup schedules. This makes training faster and less finicky.Continue

title: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm
text: WhatPost-Norm●"Attention Is All You Need" (2017)●Early BERT models●Some early translation modelsHistorical. Used mainly in the original paper.Pre-Norm●GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4●LLaMA, LLaMA 2, LLaMA 3●Mistral, Mixtral●Claude●Virtually all modern LLMsThe industry standard since ~2019.The Field ConvergedThe shift happened because pre-norm trains faster, scales better to deep models, and is more robust. When you hear about a modern LLM with 96+ layers, it is almost certainly using pre-norm. The original paper used post-norm, but practice proved pre-norm superior for large-scale training.

title: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm
text: The Encoder: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm. This chapter covers pre-norm vs post-norm.

title: Batch Norm vs Layer Norm
text: Batch Normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015)Batch Norm normalizes across the batch dimension. Given a batch of examples, for each feature, compute the mean and variance across all examples in the batch.Batch of 4 examples, each with 3 features:Feature 1Feature 2Feature 3Example 10.51.2-0.3Example 20.80.90.1Example 30.31.5-0.5Example 40.60.60.2↑ Normalize this column ↑Batch Norm picks one feature (one column) and computes the mean and variance across the 4 examples. For Feature 2: mean = (1.2 + 0.9 + 1.5 + 0.6) / 4 = 1.05. Then it normalizes each example's value for that feature.Continue

title: Batch Norm vs Layer Norm
text: h Norm Works for Images and CNNsIn a CNN processing images, batch normalization works beautifully. Here's why:Shared statisticsImages in a batch share similar structure. The feature "edge detector at position (3,5)" has a meaningful distribution across a batch of cat photos - they all have edges in roughly similar places.Fixed input sizeImages are always the same dimensions (e.g., 224x224). Position (3,5) means the same thing in every image. Computing statistics across the batch at that position makes sense.Training speedupBN reduced internal covariate shift (layer inputs changing distribution during training), letting CNNs train 10-14x faster. It also acted as a mild regularizer.Batch Norm revolutionized CNN training when it was introduced in 2015. It made training deep image models much faster and more stable. But language is a fundamentally different domain...Continue

title: Batch Norm vs Layer Norm
text: h Norm Fails for LanguageThree fundamental problems make Batch Norm a poor fit for text:1. Variable Sequence LengthsSentences have different lengths. In a batch of 4 sentences:"I love cats"3 tokens"The cat sat on the mat last week"8 tokens"Hello"1 tokens"Deep learning is fascinating"4 tokensWhat is "position 5" across this batch? Only one sentence even has a position 5. Normalizing across the batch at position 5 is meaningless - there's nothing to compare.2. Batch Size 1 at InferenceWhen you chat with an LLM, your prompt is batch size 1. There's no batch to compute statistics over. Batch Norm must fall back to running averages stored from training - a fragile approximation that can drift from the true distribution.3. Tokens Are Fundamentally DifferentNormalizing feature 42 across "the", "cat", "sat", and "love" from different sentences treats them as comparable. But they're completely different words with different roles. Unlike pixels at the same position in different images, tokens at position 3 across different sentences share nothing semantically.Continue

title: Batch Norm vs Layer Norm
text: rmalization (Ba et al., 2016)Layer Norm normalizes across the feature dimension. For each token independently, compute the mean and variance across its own features.Same 4x3 grid, different dimension:Feature 1Feature 2Feature 3Example 10.51.2-0.3Example 20.80.90.1Example 30.31.5-0.5Example 40.60.60.2← Normalize this row →Layer Norm picks one example (one row) and computes mean and variance across its 3 features. For Example 2: mean = (0.8 + 0.9 + 0.1) / 3 = 0.60. Each token normalizes itself independently.●No batch dependency: Each token normalizes using only its own features. Works with batch size 1.●Any sequence length: Token 5 normalizes itself the same way whether the sentence has 5 or 500 tokens.●Semantically meaningful: Normalizing across a single token's features rescales its representation without mixing different words' statistics.Continue

title: Batch Norm vs Layer Norm
text: Difference - Which Dimension?Batch NormNormalizes down columns(across examples in the batch)highlighted column = one BN operationNeeds a batch of examplesUsed in CNNs (image models)Fragile at batch size 1Layer NormNormalizes across rows(across features of one token)highlighted row = one LN operationIndependent per tokenUsed in Transformers (LLMs)Works with any batch sizeThe Key InsightLayer Norm treats each token as its own universe. Its mean and variance depend only on that token's own feature values - not on what other sentences happen to be in the same batch, not on how long other sequences are, and not on what other tokens exist. This independence is exactly what Transformers need: each token's normalization should be a property of that token alone.

title: Batch Norm vs Layer Norm

title: Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work
text: Plot twist: Modern LLMs are decoder-onlyChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini - they all use only the Decoder half. They threw away the Encoder.Why throw away half the architecture? Because translation and chatting are fundamentally different.Continue

title: Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work
text: ion vs Chatting: different tasksTranslation: input (English) and output (French) are different languages. You must fully process the input before generating output.Chatting: input (your prompt) and output (my response) are both English. They're part of one continuous conversation, not two separate things.TranslationEnglish input (complete)↓French output (generated)Two separate "sides"ChattingPrompt: "What is 2+2?"Response: "The answer is 4."One continuous streamContinue

title: Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work
text: asking: the key differenceIn a decoder-only model, when generating token N, you can only look at tokens 0 through N-1 (the past and present). You cannot look at future tokens.This is called causal masking - causality: effect cannot precede cause.Processing "What is 2+2?" sequentially:Step 1:GenerateWhatby looking atnothingStep 2:Generateisby looking atWhatStep 3:Generate2+2by looking atWhat, isStep 4:Generate?by looking atWhat, is, 2+2This is the fundamental constraint of LLMs: they generate one token at a time, and each new token is based only on what came before.Continue

title: Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work
text: der-only winsDecoder-only is simpler (half the architecture) but it turns out that a large decoder trained on enough data can:1.Read and understand your prompt (implicit encoder task)2.Generate natural, coherent responses (explicit decoder task)3.Reason, code, translate, summarize (with prompting/fine-tuning)The result: decoder-only models are more general-purpose than any other architecture. They're simpler, faster, and more scalable.Continue

title: Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work
text: chitectures: the complete pictureEncoder-DecoderEDTask: TranslationModels: T5, BART, mT5Best for: When input and output are different languages or modesEncoder-OnlyETask: UnderstandingModels: BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERTBest for: Classification, sentiment, similarity - no generationDecoder-OnlyDTask: GenerationModels: GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, GeminiBest for: Chat, writing, reasoning, all modern LLMsContinue

title: Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work
text: understand the complete evolutionCNN (local patterns) → RNN (sequential memory, slow) → Transformer (attention, parallel) → Decoder-only (the architecture of modern AI).You now have the full history. The upcoming sections go deep into each piece of the Transformer: how input enters, how attention works, and how the complete encoder and decoder blocks are built.

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: The problem: during generation, future words don't exist yetWhen the model generates text one token at a time, it has only produced the tokens so far. The next word hasn't been generated yet - it can't attend to something that doesn't exist.generating "The cat sat on the mat"1.The→cat?"The" can only see itself2.Thecat→sat?"cat" sees "The" and itself3.Thecatsat→on?"sat" sees "The", "cat", and itself4.Thecatsaton→the?"on" sees all 4 previous tokensBut during training, the model sees the entire sentence at once for efficiency. So we need to artificially hide future tokens - that's what the causal mask does.Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: look at whom?Each row is a word asking "who can I attend to?" Each column is a word being looked at. A word can only see words generated before it (+ itself):row asks →col answers ↓Thecatsaton"The" asks →✓✗✗✗"cat" asks →✓✓✗✗"sat" asks →✓✓✓✗"on" asks →✓✓✓✓"The"1st word - only itself exists"cat"2nd word - sees "The" + itself"sat"3rd word - sees "The", "cat" + itself"on"4th word - sees everything before it + itselfThe diagonal = each word seeing itself (always allowed)Below diagonal = looking at earlier words (allowed - they already exist)Above diagonal = looking at future words (forbidden - not generated yet)Now here is the actual Q·KT / √dk score matrix:Thecatsaton"The" →2.10.50.30.8"cat" →1.22.40.70.4"sat" →0.61.82.01.1"on" →0.90.31.52.3allowed (past + self) - keep these scoresfuture - must be blocked!Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: : a triangle of negative infinityWe add a mask matrix to the scores. The mask has 0 where a word is allowed to look, and -∞ where it's forbidden:Thecatsaton"The" →0-∞-∞-∞"cat" →00-∞-∞"sat" →000-∞"on" →0000Adding -∞ to a score makes it -∞. When softmax sees -∞, it outputs exactly 0 - zero attention, as if that word doesn't exist. The lower triangle (0s) keeps scores unchanged.masked_scores = scores + maskWhere mask[i][j] = 0 if j ≤ i, else -∞Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: sking → softmax only sees allowed tokensHere are the masked scores - future positions are now -∞:masked scoresThecatsaton"The" →2.1-∞-∞-∞"cat" →1.22.4-∞-∞"sat" →0.61.82.0-∞"on" →0.90.31.52.3The green cells keep their original scores. The red -∞ cells will become exactly 0 after softmax - as if those future words don't exist.Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: turns masked scores into attention weights↓ softmax (each row)attention weights (probabilities)Thecatsaton"The" →1.000.000.000.00"cat" →0.230.770.000.00"sat" →0.120.400.480.00"on" →0.130.070.250.55Every -∞ became exactly 0.00 after softmax!"The" puts 100% attention on itself. "cat" splits between "The" and itself. Each row still sums to 1.0 - but only across the visible tokens.Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: ional vs Causal - see the differenceThe attention formula is identical. The ONLY difference is whether the upper triangle is masked:No mask (bidirectional)every word sees every word0.610.120.100.170.190.620.110.080.100.330.400.160.130.070.250.55Full matrix - all cells activeCausal maskeach word sees only past + self1.000000.230.77000.120.400.4800.130.070.250.55Lower triangle only - upper triangle is darkSame Q, K, V matrices. Same formula. Same softmax. The mask is a single triangle of -∞ values - and it completely changes what the model can do.Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: ning Insight: Every Position Makes a Prediction4 tokens go in, 4 output vectors come out - always, regardless of masking. Each output vector passes through Linear + Softmax to predict the next token. The mask determines whether those predictions are honest.one forward pass through the transformerThecatsaton↓ ↓ ↓ ↓Transformer Blocks (N layers)↓ ↓ ↓ ↓v1v2v3v4↓ ↓ ↓ ↓Linear + Softmax → 4 next-token predictionsNo Mask (Bidirectional)Every position sees the full sequence"The"saw:Thecatsaton→ saw the answer before predicting"cat"saw:Thecatsaton→ saw the answer before predicting"sat"saw:Thecatsaton→ saw the answer before predicting"on"saw:Thecatsaton→ saw the answer before predicting0 usable training examplesEvery prediction "saw the answer"Causal MaskEach position sees only past + self"The"saw:The→ predicts"cat"HONEST"cat"saw:Thecat→ predicts"sat"HONEST"sat"saw:Thecatsat→ predicts"on"HONEST"on"saw:Thecatsaton→ predicts"the"HONEST4 usable training examples!Each prediction was made without peekingThis is why causal masking exists during trainingFrom one forward pass with N tokens, you get N honest next-token predictions to learn from. Without the mask, you'd get zero - the model would "cheat" at every position, then completely fail at actual generation.Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: chitectures - who uses what mask and whyENCODER-ONLYBERT, RoBERTaFull matrix - all cells activeNo mask - every word sees every other word (bidirectional).Why? The encoder's job is to understand the full input. When classifying "The bank by the river", "bank" NEEDS to see "river" (which comes after it) to know it means riverbank, not a financial bank. Hiding future words would cripple understanding.Used for: text classification, sentiment analysis, question answering, named entity recognition.Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: ONLYGPT, Claude, LLaMALower triangle only - upper triangle maskedCausal mask - each word only sees past words + itself.Why? The decoder generates text one token at a time. At generation step 3, token 4 doesn't exist yet - attending to it would be looking at something that isn't there. During training, the full sentence is fed at once for speed, so we use the mask to simulate the generation condition. Without it, the model would "cheat" by seeing the answer, then fail at actual generation.Used for: text generation, chatbots, code completion, reasoning - all modern LLMs.Continue

title: Causal Masking - Hiding the Future
text: DECODEROriginal Transformer, T5, BARTencoder+decoderEncoder: full matrix + Decoder: lower triangleBoth - encoder uses no mask, decoder uses causal mask, plus cross-attention connecting them.Why? The input (e.g., English sentence) already exists fully - the encoder should see all of it. But the output (e.g., French translation) is generated word by word - the decoder must not peek ahead. Cross-attention lets the decoder look at the encoder's full understanding while still generating left-to-right.Used for: translation, summarization, any "input → different output" task.The core rule is simpleIf the text already exists (input you're reading) → no mask needed, let every word see everything for maximum understanding.If the text is being generated (output you're writing) → causal mask required, because future words don't exist yet.
