title: Cross-Attention - The Encoder-Decoder Bridge
text: The setup: translating "I love cats" to FrenchThe encoder has processed the English sentence. Each word is now a rich contextual vector - "love" doesn't just mean "love" anymore, it means "love in the context of I-love-cats".Encoder output (English - fully processed)I768 dimslove768 dimscats768 dimsThese vectors are the encoder's final output - rich with context↓ feeds into decoder's cross-attentionDecoder (French - generating word by word)J'generatedaimegeneratedlesgeneratedchatsnext?The decoder needs to look at the English to know what to generate nextThe decoder's self-attention lets French words look at other French words. But how does the decoder look at the English words? That's what cross-attention does.Continue

title: Cross-Attention - The Encoder-Decoder Bridge
text: idea: Q from decoder, K and V from encoderIn self-attention, Q, K, and V all come from the same sentence. In cross-attention, they come from different sources:QQuery (Q) ← Decoder (French)The decoder is asking: "what English word should I pay attention to right now?"KKey (K) ← Encoder (English)The encoder's words offer their 'labels' for matching - "I'm the subject", "I'm the verb"VValue (V) ← Encoder (English)The encoder's words offer their actual content - the rich contextual meaning to borrow fromSame formula: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax( Q·KT / √dk ) · VThe math is identical to self-attention. Only the source of Q, K, V changes.Continue

title: Cross-Attention - The Encoder-Decoder Bridge
text: cross-attention: French Q meets English KEach French word's Query asks "which English word is most relevant to me?" The score matrix is not square - it's 4 French rows x 3 English columns:cross-attention scores (Q·KT / √dk)decoder Q ↓encoder K →Ilovecats"J'" →2.80.40.3"aime" →0.53.10.6"les" →0.20.32.5"chats" →0.30.73.4↓ softmax (each row)attention weightsIlovecats"J'" →0.850.080.07"aime" →0.060.860.07"les" →0.080.090.83"chats" →0.040.060.90"J'"→85% on "I""J'" (I) focuses on "I" - direct translation"aime"→86% on "love""aime" (love) focuses on "love" - the verb it's translating"les"→83% on "cats""les" (the) focuses on "cats" - it's the article FOR cats"chats"→90% on "cats""chats" (cats) focuses on "cats" - direct translationContinue

title: Cross-Attention - The Encoder-Decoder Bridge
text: ention vs Cross-attentionSame formula, different wiring:SELF-ATTENTIONsame sentenceQKVQ, K, V all from the same sentence. Words attend to each other within one language.Score matrix is square (N x N)CROSS-ATTENTIONdecoderencoderQKVQ from decoder, K and V from encoder. Decoder attends to encoder's output.Score matrix is rectangular (M x N)Each decoder layer has BOTH types of attention:1. Masked self-attention - French words look at other French words (with causal mask)2. Cross-attention - French words look at English words (no mask - the full English sentence already exists)Continue

title: Cross-Attention - The Encoder-Decoder Bridge
text: detail: the encoder runs ONCEA common misconception is that encoder layer 1 feeds decoder layer 1, encoder layer 2 feeds decoder layer 2, etc. That's not how it works.WRONGLayer-to-layer mappingE6→D6E5→D5E4→D4E3→D3E2→D2E1→D1✗CORRECTOnly the final layer mattersEncoder Layer 6 (final)↓K, V (computed once)↓↓↓↓↓↓Decoder Layer 1Decoder Layer 2Decoder Layer 3Decoder Layer 4Decoder Layer 5Decoder Layer 6Encoder: runs all 6 layers once on "I love cats". The output of layer 6 (the final layer) is the encoder's output - 3 contextual vectors, one per English word.K and V: computed once from the encoder's final output using WK and WV weight matrices. These are then cached and reused.Decoder layers 1-6: each layer computes its own Q (because the decoder's representation changes layer by layer as it gets refined). But K and V are always the same - the encoder's final output.Continue

title: Cross-Attention - The Encoder-Decoder Bridge
text: data flow with dimensionsd_model = 512, d_k = 64, 8 headsCross-attention architecture showing encoder stack producing K and V projections fanned out to decoder layers, where Q comes from decoder while K and V come from encoder outputENCODERLayer 6 (final)Layer 5Layer 4Layer 3Layer 2Layer 1"I love cats"[3 x 512]Projection (once)K = encoder_out · W_KV = encoder_out · W_V[3 x 512] · [512 x 64] = [3 x 64](per head)K, V CACHEK: [3 x 64] V: [3 x 64]DECODERLayer 6QchangesK,VsameLayer 5QchangesK,VsameLayer 4QchangesK,VsameLayer 3QchangesK,VsameLayer 2QchangesK,VsameLayer 1QchangesK,Vsame"J' aime les chats" [4 x 512]Continue

title: Cross-Attention - The Encoder-Decoder Bridge
text: pens at each decoder layerEvery decoder layer performs cross-attention with the exact same K and V, but a different Q:Layer 1:Attn(Q1,K,V) = softmax(Q1 · KT / √64) · VLayer 2:Attn(Q2,K,V) = softmax(Q2 · KT / √64) · V... same K, V⋮Layer 6:Attn(Q6,K,V) = softmax(Q6 · KT / √64) · VQ1 comes from the decoder's layer 1 output. Q6 comes from layer 6's output. The decoder's representation evolves layer by layer - Q captures "what I'm looking for" at each stage of refinement. K and V capture "what the encoder said" - and that never changes.Dimension walkthrough (d_model = 512, heads = 8, d_k = 64)Encoder output[3 x 512]3 English tokens, each 512 dimsW_K projection[512 x 64]per head, learned during trainingK (cached)[3 x 64]3 keys, one per English tokenV (cached)[3 x 64]3 values, one per English tokenDecoder layer n[4 x 512]4 French tokens, each 512 dimsQ_n (per layer)[4 x 64]4 queries, changes each layerQ_n · K^T scores[4 x 3]every French word scores every English wordWhy is this efficient?The encoder runs once. K and V are computed once. Only Q changes per decoder layer. For a 1000-token input, this saves computing 1000-token K and V six times - a 6x reduction in encoder-side computation.6 weight matrices per layerEach decoder layer has 6 separate learned matrices: WQ, WK, WV for masked self-attention (Q, K, V all from French) + WQ, WK, WV for cross-attention (Q from French, K and V from English). All learned independently.Continue

title: Cross-Attention - The Encoder-Decoder Bridge
text: es cross-attention live?decoder-onlyGPT, Claude, LLaMANo cross-attention at allThere's no encoder, so nothing to cross-attend to. The decoder handles everything with just self-attention. Your prompt and the response are one continuous sequence.encoder-decoderOriginal Transformer, T5, BARTCross-attention in every decoder layerEach decoder layer has three sub-layers in order:1.Masked self-attentionFrench looks at French (causal)2.Cross-attentionFrench looks at English (full)3.Feed-forward networkProcess each position independentlyThis is exactly what the architecture diagram in chapter 5.1 shows - the green arrow "K, V from encoder" connects to this cross-attention layer.Cross-attention is elegant but optionalModern LLMs prove you can get world-class results without it. But for tasks with clear input/output separation (translation, summarization), the encoder-decoder architecture with cross-attention remains powerful.

title: The Output Head - Linear + Softmax
text: From vectors to words - the final stepAfter all transformer layers, every position holds a rich, context-aware vector of 512 numbers. This vector encodes everything the model understood about that position.But we need an actual word, not 512 numbers. The model's vocab has 50,257 possible tokens. How does it choose one?Decoder output[0.82, -0.31, 0.57, 0.14, ...]512 numbers→?Which of 50,257 words?That is exactly what the output head does. It has two parts: a Linear layer and Softmax. These are the two blocks sitting at the very top of the architecture diagram.Continue

title: The Output Head - Linear + Softmax
text: ar layer - one giant matrix multiplyThe Linear layer is a weight matrix W with 512 rows and 50,257 columns. Each column represents one word in the vocabulary.The matrix multiplicationhidden state[1 x 512]×W[512 x 50,257]=logits[1 x 50,257]One matrix multiply. That is it. No activation function, no non-linearity - just multiply the 512-dim hidden state by this giant matrix and you get 50,257 raw scores, one for every word in the vocabulary.This single matrix has 512 x 50,257 = 25.7 million parameters. It is the second largest weight matrix in the model (after the embedding table).Continue

title: The Output Head - Linear + Softmax
text: its mean - dot product similarityEach logit is a dot product between the hidden state and one column of W. A high dot product means the hidden state is pointing in the same direction as that word's template.Computing one logithidden state:[0.82, -0.31, 0.57, 0.14, ... 512 dims]W column for "cats":[0.71, -0.25, 0.63, 0.08, ... 512 dims]logit = dot product:(0.82)(0.71) + (-0.31)(-0.25) + (0.57)(0.63) + ... = 4.2This happens for all 50,257 words simultaneously:cats+4.2dogs+3.1the+1.5love-0.3banana-2.1... and 50,252 more words, each with a logit scoreHigh logit = the model's hidden state is similar to that word's representation. Low or negative = dissimilar. But these raw numbers are hard to interpret - we need probabilities.Continue

title: The Output Head - Linear + Softmax
text: - from scores to probabilitiesRaw logits can be any number: positive, negative, huge, tiny. Softmax converts them into probabilities that are all positive and sum to exactly 1.The softmax formulaP(word_i) = elogit_i / Σ elogit_jExponentiate each logit, then divide by the sum of all exponentiated logits.Watch the transformation:Raw logitscats+4.2dogs+3.1the+1.5love-0.3banana-2.1→After softmaxcats70.8%dogs23.6%the4.8%love0.8%banana0.1%Notice how softmax amplifies differences. A logit gap of just 1.1 between "cats" (4.2) and "dogs" (3.1) becomes a 3x probability difference (71% vs 24%). The exponential function makes the winner win big.Continue

title: The Output Head - Linear + Softmax
text: ure - the creativity dialBefore softmax, divide each logit by a number called temperature (T). This controls how "peaked" or "spread out" the resulting probabilities are.softmax( logit / temperature )T = 0.2Focused - Almost deterministiccats100%dogs0%the0%T = 1.0Default - Balancedcats71%dogs24%the5%T = 2.0Creative - More spread outcats50%dogs29%the13%Low temperature (0.2): divides logits by 0.2, making differences 5x larger before softmax. The top word dominates. High temperature (2.0): divides by 2.0, shrinking differences. More words get a fair chance. Temperature = 0 would be pure greedy (pick the top word every time).Continue

title: The Output Head - Linear + Softmax
text: strategies - how to pick the next tokenAfter softmax gives us probabilities, we still need to decide: which word do we actually pick? There are three main strategies:Greedy decodingAlways pick the highest probability word. Simple but boring - the same input always produces the same output.cats 71% ✓dogs 24% the 5% love 1% Top-k sampling (e.g., k = 3)Keep only the k highest-probability words. Re-normalize their probabilities and randomly sample from them. Balances quality with variety.cats 71%dogs 24%the 5%love 1%→ sample from top 3Top-p sampling / nucleus (e.g., p = 0.9)Keep adding words (highest probability first) until their cumulative probability reaches p. More adaptive than Top-k - uses fewer words when the model is confident, more when uncertain.cats 71% (Σ71%)dogs 24% (Σ95%)the 5% (Σ100%)love 1% (Σ101%)→ stop at 87% (under 90%)In practice, most APIs combine temperature + Top-p. ChatGPT defaults: temperature = 1.0, Top-p = 1.0 (no filtering). For more focused output: temperature = 0.7, Top-p = 0.9.Continue

title: The Output Head - Linear + Softmax
text: ying - the elegant shortcutHere is a surprising fact: the output Linear layer's weight matrix W is the same matrix as the embedding table from the input - just transposed.Embedding (input)word → vector[50,257 x 512]SAME MATRIX⇆Linear (output)vector → word scores[512 x 50,257]Why does this work?The embedding table maps each word to a point in 512-dimensional space. The output layer asks: "which word's point is closest to this hidden state?" That is the same operation in reverse.If the embedding for "cats" is [0.71, -0.25, 0.63, ...] and our hidden state is [0.82, -0.31, 0.57, ...], the dot product is high because they point in similar directions. The model is literally asking: "which embedding vector is this hidden state most similar to?"Parameter savings from weight tyingWithout tying: 50,257 x 512 = 25.7M extra parameters. With tying: zero extra parameters - the output layer reuses the embedding matrix. GPT-2 and most modern LLMs use this trick.Continue

title: The Output Head - Linear + Softmax
text: lete output pipelineHere is every step from hidden state to predicted token:hidden state512 dims→Linear (Wᵀ)512 → 50,257→logits50,257 scores→÷ Ttemperature→softmax→ probabilities→sampleTop-k / Top-p→next token"cats"During trainingThe model computes this output head at every position simultaneously. At position 1 ("I"), it should predict "love". At position 2 ("love"), it should predict "cats". Cross-entropy loss measures how wrong each prediction was, and backprop updates the weights.During inferenceOnly the last position's hidden state goes through the output head. That is the position where the model predicts the next token. This single prediction gets sampled, appended to the input, and the whole process repeats - one token at a time.The big pictureThe entire transformer - embeddings, positional encoding, attention, FFN, layer stacking - exists to produce one good hidden state at the final position. The output head then converts that single 512-dimensional vector into a probability distribution over 50,257 words. One matrix multiply, one softmax. That is all it takes to go from "understanding" to "speaking."

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: What is a Transformer actually doing?You give it a sentence: "I love cats"It needs to understand what each word means in context. The word "love" alone could mean many things - romantic love, love of food, a tennis score. But in "I love cats" it specifically means personal affection for cats.The Transformer figures this out through a pipeline of steps:Ilovecats→contextual understandingContinue

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Each word becomes a vector (embedding)The model's embedding table maps each token to a vector of numbers. These numbers capture meaning in isolation - "love" doesn't know about "I" or "cats" yet.I→[0.2, -0.5, 0.8, 0.1, ...]512 dimslove→[0.9, 0.1, -0.3, 0.6, ...]512 dimscats→[0.4, 0.7, 0.2, -0.4, ...]512 dimsSimilar words get similar vectors. "cat" and "dog" are nearby in this 512-dimensional space. But "love" here is generic - it could be any kind of love.Continue

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Add position informationWithout position, "I love cats" and "cats love I" look identical to the model - same words, same embeddings. Order matters!Positional encoding adds WHERE each word sits in the sentence:Example for "love" at position 1:embedding[0.9, 0.1, -0.3, ...]+position[0.84, 0.54, 0.00, ...]=positioned[1.74, 0.64, -0.30, ...]Result: each word now knows WHAT it means AND WHERE it sits. "love" at position 1 is different from "love" at position 5.Continue

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Self-attention - words look at each otherThis is the magic step. Each word sends a Query ("what am I looking for?"), every word offers a Key ("here is what I can offer"), and the model computes attention scores - how relevant is each word to every other?Attention scores for "I love cats"IlovecatsI0.600.250.15love0.150.300.55cats0.100.550.35"love" attends strongly to "cats" (0.55) - loves WHAT? And "cats" attends to "love" (0.55) - cats in what context?After the weighted sum of Values: "love" is no longer generic. It now means "I-have-affection-for-cats" - encoded as a completely new vector shaped by its context.Continue

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Add & Norm + FFNTwo important things happen after attention:Attention OutputContext-aware vectors+Original (residual)Pre-attention vectors→Add & NormStabilize values→FFNTwo linear + GELU→Add & NormStabilize againResidual connectionAdds the ORIGINAL vector (before attention) back to the output. This prevents information loss and helps gradients flow during training. Without it, deep networks cannot learn.FFN (Feed-Forward Network)Two linear layers with GELU activation. Processes each word INDEPENDENTLY. Attention mixed words together - the FFN thinks about each word alone, creating richer features.Continue

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Repeat N timesOne attention + FFN block is ONE transformer layer. Real models repeat this block many times. Each layer refines understanding further.Layer 1-2Basic syntax, word boundariesLayer 3-6Grammar, subject-verb agreementLayer 7-10Semantic meaning, analogiesLayer 11-12Complex reasoning, long-range dependenciesGPT-2: 12 layers. GPT-3: 96 layers. LLaMA 3 70B: 80 layers.After all layers: "love" has been refined from "generic positive emotion" to "specific first-person affection directed at small furry animals in this particular sentence."Continue

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: Predict the next tokenAfter all layers, each position holds a rich context-aware vector (512 numbers). The model takes the LAST position's vector and projects it through a Linear layer.Linear: 512 dims → 50,257 (one score per word in vocabulary)Softmax: converts raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1Top predictions after "I love cats":very35%so22%a12%the8%really5%The highest probability word becomes the prediction. The model picks "very" - "I love cats very..." and then repeats the whole process for the next token.Continue

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: lete pipelineHere is every step connected end to end:"I love cats"→Tokenize→Embed→+Position→Self-Attention→Add & Norm→FFN→Add & Norm→×N layers→Linear→Softmax→"very" (35%)The key insightThe word "love" entered as a generic 512-number vector and exited as a completely different 512-number vector that encodes "first-person affection directed at cats in a casual sentence."This is what "understanding context" means - every word's vector is reshaped by all the other words around it. That reshaping is the pipeline you just saw.

title: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?
text: The Decoder: What is a Transformer Actually Doing?. This chapter covers what is a transformer actually doing?.

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: The Attention Formula - What Gets ComputedBefore we can understand KV caching, we need to see exactly what a single attention layer computes. For a sequence of N tokens, each represented as a d-dimensional embedding vector:Three matrix multiplications per layer:QQ = X · W_Q(N × d) - one query per tokenKK = X · W_K(N × d) - one key per tokenVV = X · W_V(N × d) - one value per tokenThen attention combines them:output = softmax( Q · Kᵀ / √d ) · VEach row of the output is one token's "context-aware" representation. Row i is computed from token i's query attending to all keys and aggregating all values.The key point: Q, K, V are computed from X using fixed weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V. The same input always produces the same Q, K, V. This is what makes caching possible.Continue

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: on Without Cache - See the WasteGenerating "I love cats" token by token. At each step, the model must compute Q, K, V for the entire sequence so far. Watch the redundant work pile up:Step 1: Generate "I"Sequence so far: ["I"]. Compute Q, K, V for 1 token:q_I = W_Q · x_Ik_I = W_K · x_Iv_I = W_V · x_I3 matrix-vector multiplications. All new, nothing wasted.Step 2: Generate "love"Sequence so far: ["I", "love"]. Recompute Q, K, V for ALL 2 tokens:q_I = W_Q · x_I ← Redundant! Same as Step 1k_I = W_K · x_I ← Redundant! Same as Step 1v_I = W_V · x_I ← Redundant! Same as Step 1q_love = W_Q · x_lovek_love = W_K · x_lovev_love = W_V · x_love6 multiplications, but 3 are redundant - identical to Step 1.Step 3: Generate "cats"Sequence so far: ["I", "love", "cats"]. Recompute Q, K, V for ALL 3 tokens:q_I = W_Q · x_I ← Redundant! (computed 3rd time)k_I = W_K · x_I ← Redundant! (computed 3rd time)v_I = W_V · x_I ← Redundant! (computed 3rd time)q_love = W_Q · x_love ← Redundant! (computed 2nd time)k_love = W_K · x_love ← Redundant! (computed 2nd time)v_love = W_V · x_love ← Redundant! (computed 2nd time)q_cats = W_Q · x_catsk_cats = W_K · x_catsv_cats = W_V · x_cats9 multiplications, but 6 are redundant!The Waste Grows QuadraticallyAt step N, we do 3N matrix-vector multiplications but only 3 are new. The other 3(N-1) are identical to work already done in earlier steps. After 1000 tokens, we have performed ~1.5 million redundant multiplications. This is the computation KV cache eliminates.Continue

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: Last Row of Output MattersThe attention formula computes an output for every token in the sequence. But during generation, we already have the outputs for all previous tokens - we computed them in earlier steps. We only need the output for the new token.Full attention output (N rows):output = softmax( Q · Kᵀ / √d ) · V ← shape: (N × d)Row 1 (for "I"):already computed at step 1Row 2 (for "love"):already computed at step 2Row 3 (for "cats"):THIS is what we need nowThe formula for JUST the new token's output:output_new = softmax( q_new · K_allᵀ / √d ) · V_allq_newSingle vector (1 x d) - only the new token's queryK_allMatrix of ALL tokens' keys (N x d) - needed for dot product scoresV_allMatrix of ALL tokens' values (N x d) - needed for weighted sumThis single formula is the entire reason KV cache exists. It needs just one Q vector, but ALL keys and ALL values. That asymmetry is the key.Continue

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: d V Are Cached - Not QLook at the formula again and ask: for each part, do we need the old tokens' values or just the new token's?Q (Query) - we never need old queries againThe formula is: q_new · K_allᵀ. Only q_new appears - the new token's query. We do not multiply q_I or q_love against anything. Those queries already served their purpose in steps 1 and 2. They are dead weight.Step 3: q_cats · [k_I, k_love, k_cats]ᵀOnly q_cats is used. q_I and q_love are not part of this computation at all.K (Key) - ALL old keys are needed every stepThe new token must compute a dot product with every key to find which tokens to attend to. When generating "cats", its query must score against k_I, k_love, AND k_cats. If we threw away k_I, the model could not attend to "I" at all.scores = q_cats · [k_I, k_love, k_cats]ᵀ = [0.25, 0.29, 0.27]Every key participates. Missing one key = missing one attention score.V (Value) - ALL old values are needed every stepThe output is a weighted sum of ALL value vectors. The attention weights tell us how much of each token's meaning to include. If v_I were missing, the output could not incorporate any information from "I".output = 0.33 · v_I + 0.34 · v_love + 0.33 · v_catsEvery value participates. Missing one value = losing that token's contribution.The AsymmetryQ (Query)Only new token's Q is usedOld Q values are never needed againDo not cacheK (Key)ALL tokens' keys needed every stepRecomputing is pure wasteCache itV (Value)ALL tokens' values needed every stepRecomputing is pure wasteCache itContinue

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: ache - Exact OperationsLet's compare exactly what happens at Step 3 (generating "cats") with and without the cache:Without Cacheq_I = W_Q · x_Iwastedq_love = W_Q · x_lovewastedq_cats = W_Q · x_catsusedk_I = W_K · x_Iwastedk_love = W_K · x_lovewastedk_cats = W_K · x_catsusedv_I = W_V · x_Iwastedv_love = W_V · x_lovewastedv_cats = W_V · x_catsused9 multiplications (6 wasted)With CacheK_cache = [k_I, k_love] from steps 1-2V_cache = [v_I, v_love] from steps 1-2Compute only for new token:q_cats = W_Q · x_catsk_cats = W_K · x_catsv_cats = W_V · x_catsAppend to cache:K_cache = [k_I, k_love, k_cats]V_cache = [v_I, v_love, v_cats]Compute attention:q_cats · K_cacheᵀ / √d → softmax → · V_cache3 multiplications (0 wasted)Same result, less workBoth approaches produce the exact same output_cats. The attention scores are identical because the same q_cats, k_I, k_love, k_cats are used. The only difference is where k_I and k_love come from - freshly computed (waste) or read from cache (free).At step N: without cache = 3N multiplications. With cache = 3 multiplications. For N = 1000, that is 3000 vs 3 - a 1000x reduction in projection work alone.Continue

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: xample with Real NumbersLet's trace through the exact computation with d = 2 to see every number. Small dimensions, real math, no hand-waving.Token embeddings (d = 2):x_I = [1.0, 0.0]x_love = [0.0, 1.0]x_cats = [0.5, 0.5]Weight matrices (2 x 2):W_Q[0.5, 0.1][0.2, 0.6]W_K[0.3, 0.7][0.4, 0.2]W_V[0.8, 0.1][0.3, 0.9]Step 1: Process "I" - compute everythingq_I = W_Q · [1.0, 0.0] = [0.5, 0.2]k_I = W_K · [1.0, 0.0] = [0.3, 0.4]v_I = W_V · [1.0, 0.0] = [0.8, 0.3]Cache after step 1: K = [[0.3, 0.4]] V = [[0.8, 0.3]]Step 2: Process "love" - compute new, load cachedk_I and v_I loaded from cache - not recomputed!q_love = W_Q · [0.0, 1.0] = [0.1, 0.6]k_love = W_K · [0.0, 1.0] = [0.7, 0.2]v_love = W_V · [0.0, 1.0] = [0.1, 0.9]Cache after step 2: K = [[0.3, 0.4], [0.7, 0.2]] V = [[0.8, 0.3], [0.1, 0.9]]Step 3: Process "cats" - the full attention computationk_I, k_love, v_I, v_love all loaded from cache. Only compute for "cats":q_cats = W_Q · [0.5, 0.5] = [0.3, 0.4]k_cats = W_K · [0.5, 0.5] = [0.5, 0.3]v_cats = W_V · [0.5, 0.5] = [0.45, 0.6]Full K matrix (from cache + new):k_I = [0.3, 0.4] (from cache)k_love = [0.7, 0.2] (from cache)k_cats = [0.5, 0.3] (just computed)Attention scores: q_cats · each keyq_cats · k_I = 0.3×0.3 + 0.4×0.4 = 0.25q_cats · k_love = 0.3×0.7 + 0.4×0.2 = 0.29q_cats · k_cats = 0.3×0.5 + 0.4×0.3 = 0.27Scale by √d = √2, then softmax:scaled = [0.177, 0.205, 0.191]softmax = [0.329, 0.338, 0.333]Weighted sum of values:0.329 × [0.8, 0.3] + 0.338 × [0.1, 0.9] + 0.333 × [0.45, 0.6]= [0.263, 0.099] + [0.034, 0.304] + [0.150, 0.200]output_cats = [0.447, 0.603]This exact same [0.447, 0.603] would result without the cache. The cache changes WHERE k_I, k_love, v_I, v_love come from (cache vs recompute) but the numbers are identical.Continue

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: Cache Looks Like in MemoryA real model has many layers and many attention heads. Each one maintains its own independent KV cache.Cache structure per layer, per head:K cache(seq_len, d_head)Grows by 1 row per tokenV cache(seq_len, d_head)Grows by 1 row per tokenGrowing cache for "I love cats" (one head):After "I":K cache:[0.3, 0.4] ← newV cache:[0.8, 0.3] ← newAfter "love":K cache:[0.3, 0.4][0.7, 0.2] ← newV cache:[0.8, 0.3][0.1, 0.9] ← newAfter "cats":K cache:[0.3, 0.4][0.7, 0.2][0.5, 0.3] ← newV cache:[0.8, 0.3][0.1, 0.9][0.45, 0.6] ← newTotal cache for a real model:KV cache = 2 (K+V) × layers × heads × d_head × seq_len × bytesA 70B model with 80 layers and 64 heads: that is 2 × 80 × 64 = 10,240 separate cache matrices, each growing by one row per generated token.Continue

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: - Operations and MemoryOperation savings:Without cachePer step: 3N projections + N2 attentionPer step: O(N2) Total: O(N3)With cachePer step: 3 projections + N attentionPer step: O(N) Total: O(N2)N = 1000: ~333 million ops without cache vs ~500 thousand with cache. ~660x faster.Memory cost - the price we pay:cache = 2 × seq_len × d_model × layers × bytes_per_paramExample: LLaMA 70BLayers80d_model8,192Max sequence4,096 tokensPrecisionFP16 (2 bytes)2 × 4,096 × 8,192 × 80 × 2 bytes= 10.7 GB per sequenceWhy this matters:●Long context is expensive: Doubling sequence length doubles cache. 128K context = enormous memory.●Batch size is limited: Each concurrent user needs their own cache. 10 users = 107 GB just for caches.●GQA helps: Grouped-Query Attention shares K, V across Q heads, shrinking cache 4-8x.Continue

title: KV Cache - Why Inference is Fast
text: amental TradeoffKV cache trades memory for speed. This is the central tension of LLM inference:Without KV CacheSpeed:Slow (recompute all Q, K, V)Memory:Low (no cache stored)O(N3) compute, O(1) extra memoryWith KV CacheSpeed:Fast (only new token's Q, K, V)Memory:High (GBs per sequence)O(N2) compute, O(N) extra memoryThe Bottleneck of LLM InferenceThis memory-speed tradeoff is the fundamental bottleneck of LLM serving. Every technique you hear about - GQA (Grouped-Query Attention), quantized KV cache, sliding window attention, paged attention - exists to reduce the memory cost of the KV cache while keeping the speed benefit. When companies say "serving LLMs is expensive," the KV cache is a major reason why.Note: KV cache only applies to inference (generation). During training, all tokens are processed in parallel - so there is no sequential generation and no need for a cache.

title: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: Recall: Multi-Head AttentionIn chapters 7.9-7.14, we learned standard MHA. With 8 heads, the model maintains 8 independent sets of Q, K, V weight matrices:QueriesW_Q1W_Q2W_Q3W_Q4W_Q5W_Q6W_Q7W_Q8KeysW_K1W_K2W_K3W_K4W_K5W_K6W_K7W_K8ValuesW_V1W_V2W_V3W_V4W_V5W_V6W_V7W_V8That is 8 W_Q + 8 W_K + 8 W_V = 24 weight matrices per attention layer. Each head has its own complete set. This works great for quality, but there is a hidden cost during inference.Continue

title: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: rence problem: KV cache memoryDuring generation, we cache the K and V vectors for all previous tokens. This avoids recomputing them at every step. But this KV cache grows fast.KV cache memory per layer2 x num_heads x seq_length x d_head x bytes_per_paramConcrete example: LLaMA 2 70Bnum_heads6464 attention headsd_head128128 dims per head (8192 / 64)seq_length40964096 token context windowlayers8080 transformer layersbytes22 bytes per param (float16)Total KV cache per batch item2 x 64 x 4096 x 128 x 2 bytes = 134,217,728 bytes per layer134,217,728 x 80 layers = 10,737,418,240 bytes~10 GB per batch item!Serving 8 users simultaneously = 80 GB just for KV caches. That is an entire A100 GPU of memory - before the model weights themselves.Continue

title: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: tion: share K and V across query groupsInstead of giving every head its own K and V, we share K and V across groups of query heads. Each head still has its own unique Q - queries stay personal.Example: 8 query heads, 2 KV groupsGroup 1: Heads 1-4 share K_1, V_1Q_1Q_2Q_3Q_4all useK_1V_1Group 2: Heads 5-8 share K_2, V_2Q_5Q_6Q_7Q_8all useK_2V_2Weight matrices needed: 8 W_Q + 2 W_K + 2 W_V = 12 projections (down from 24). The K and V computations (and their caches) shrink by 4x.Continue

title: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: omparison: MHA vs GQA vs MQAMHA (Multi-Head Attention)8Q heads8K heads8V heads24 projections total8 K + 8 V cached per layerGQA (Grouped-Query Attention, 4 heads per group)8Q heads2K heads2V heads12 projections total2 K + 2 V cached per layer (4x less)MQA (Multi-Query Attention)8Q heads1K heads1V heads10 projections total1 K + 1 V cached per layer (8x less)KV cache savings (LLaMA 2 70B, 4096 context)MHA (64 KV heads)~10 GBGQA-8 (8 KV groups)~1.25 GBMQA (1 KV shared)~160 MBContinue

title: Grouped-Query Attention - Shrinking the KV Cache
text: sharing K and V work?The query is the "question" each head asks. Each head needs its own unique question to capture different patterns (syntax, semantics, coreference, etc.).But keys and values are the "answers" - and it turns out adjacent heads often attend to similar positions anyway. The K and V vectors across heads are highly correlated. Sharing them loses very little information.Research resultGQA matches standard MHA quality within 1% on most benchmarks while using 4x less KV cache memory. The quality-to-efficiency tradeoff is exceptionally favorable.Models using GQALLaMA 2 70B8 KV groupsLLaMA 38 KV groupsMistral 7B8 KV groupsGemmavaries by sizeWhy not always use MQA (1 shared KV)?MQA saves the most memory but does lose quality - about 2-5% on harder tasks. GQA is the sweet spot: most of the memory savings with almost no quality loss. That is why most modern large models use GQA with 8 groups, not full MQA.
